topics.title: Thermal Power Plant: Introduction to Thermal Power Plant Processes, Building Blocks, Boilers, Different Types - Pulverized Fuel Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Power Plant: Introduction to Thermal Power Plant Processes, Building Blocks, Boilers, Different Types - Pulverized Fuel Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Thermal Power Plant

A **Thermal Power Plant (TPP)** is an industrial installation that generates electrical energy through the controlled conversion of chemical energy stored in a fossil fuel (coal, oil, natural gas) or bio-mass into thermal energy, mechanical energy, and finally electrical energy. The heart of the plant is the **Rankine Cycle**, where water is converted to high-pressure superheated steam in a boiler, expanded through a steam turbine to produce mechanical rotation, coupled to a synchronous generator that produces electricity.

According to the **KTU 2024 syllabus (PEAET751 - Power Plant Instrumentation, Module 1)**, a thermal power station is studied in terms of its **process blocks**, **instrumentation loops**, and **boiler sub-systems** — particularly the **Pulverized Fuel Boiler (PFB)** which is the dominant industrial design for large-scale base-load generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The student is expected to understand the *process flow diagram*, identify the *building blocks* of a thermal station, classify *boiler types*, and explain in detail the working of a *Pulverized Fuel Boiler* including its milling system, combustion zone, furnace geometry, and instrumentation.

### 1.2 The Process Chain — The Energy Conversion Story

The energy conversion in a thermal power plant follows a five-stage cascade:

$$\text{Chemical Energy (Fuel)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Thermal Energy (Steam)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mechanical Energy (Turbine)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electrical Energy (Generator)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Grid}$$

| Stage | Energy Form | Conversion Device | Typical Efficiency |
|:------|:------------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Chemical → Thermal | Boiler (Furnace + Heat Exchanger) | **85 – 92 %** |
| 2 | Thermal → Mechanical | Steam Turbine (Rankine) | **35 – 45 %** |
| 3 | Mechanical → Electrical | Synchronous Generator | **96 – 98 %** |
| **Overall** | **Fuel → Electricity** | **Combined Plant** | **33 – 42 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **net plant efficiency** of a modern sub-critical pulverized-fuel plant is typically **33–38 %**, while **super-critical and ultra-super-critical units** reach **40–45 %**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Giant Kettle with a Spinning Top"

Think of a thermal power plant as a **giant automatic kettle** connected to a **precision spinning top**:

- The **furnace + boiler** is the kettle — fuel is the fire beneath it, water is inside, and steam bubbles out the top.
- The **steam pipe** is the high-pressure hose that carries the steam.
- The **steam turbine** is a windmill that spins when steam blows on its blades.
- The **generator** is a bicycle dynamo coupled to the windmill — when the windmill spins, electricity is produced.
- The **condenser** is the cold-water cooler that turns the spent steam back into water so the cycle can start again.
- The **cooling tower** is the radiator that dumps the waste heat from the cooler into the atmosphere.

This is the entire process of a thermal power plant, just scaled up to industrial size — **500 MW to 2000 MW** for modern installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Pulverized"?** In a Pulverized Fuel Boiler, the coal is ground (pulverized) to a powder as fine as talcum powder (about **70 % passing 75 microns / 200 mesh**). This dramatically increases the surface area, allowing rapid and complete combustion — much faster than burning lumps of coal.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are universally referenced in KTU board examinations:

- **Net Calorific Value (NCV) of Indian Coal:** $4000\text{ to } 4500 \text{ kcal/kg}$ ($16.7\text{ to } 18.8 \text{ MJ/kg}$)
- **Boiler Steam Pressure (sub-critical):** $170 \text{ to } 190 \text{ kg/cm}^2$
- **Boiler Steam Pressure (super-critical):** $> 225 \text{ kg/cm}^2$
- **Steam Temperature (superheated):** $535 \text{ to } 600\text{°C}$
- **Pulverized Coal Fineness:** $70 \text{ %}$ through 200 mesh (75 µm)
- **Furnace Temperature:** $1400 \text{ to } 1600\text{°C}$
- **Specific Steam Consumption (S.S.C.):** $4.5 \text{ to } 5.5 \text{ kg/kWh}$
- **Heat Rate (sub-critical):** $2300 \text{ to } 2500 \text{ kCal/kWh}$
- **Heat Rate (super-critical):** $1900 \text{ to } 2100 \text{ kCal/kWh}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Building Blocks of a Thermal Power Plant — Systematic Breakdown

A modern thermal power plant is conceptually divided into **eight functional building blocks**. Understanding each block is essential for KTU Module 1 questions:

#### Block 1: Coal Handling Plant (CHP)
- **Function:** Receive, unload, store, crush, and convey coal to the bunkers.
- **Key Units:** Wagon tippler, conveyor belts, crushers, stockyard, bunkers.
- **Instrumentation:** Belt weighers, level sensors in bunkers, moisture analyzers.

#### Block 2: Coal Pulverizing / Milling System
- **Function:** Grind coal to pulverized fuel (PF) of required fineness.
- **Key Units:** Ball mills / Vertical roller mills / HPGR (High Pressure Grinding Rolls), classifiers, cyclones.
- **Instrumentation:** Differential pressure across mill, lube oil temperature, bearing vibration.

#### Block 3: Boiler / Steam Generator
- **Function:** Convert feed water into superheated steam at high pressure and temperature.
- **Key Units:** Furnace, water walls, superheaters, reheaters, economizer, air preheater, soot blowers.
- **Instrumentation:** Drum level, steam pressure, steam temperature, furnace draft, flue gas O₂, NOx, CO.

#### Block 4: Turbine-Generator Set (TG Set)
- **Function:** Convert steam enthalpy into mechanical rotation, then electricity.
- **Key Units:** High Pressure (HP) turbine, Intermediate Pressure (IP) turbine, Low Pressure (LP) turbine, synchronous generator, exciter.
- **Instrumentation:** Shaft speed, vibration, lube oil pressure, bearing temperature, MW output, MVAR.

#### Block 5: Condenser and Cooling System
- **Function:** Condense exhaust steam from LP turbine back to water for re-use.
- **Key Units:** Surface condenser, cooling water pumps, cooling tower, hot well, condensate extraction pumps.
- **Instrumentation:** Condenser vacuum, cooling water inlet/outlet temperature, hot well level.

#### Block 6: Feed Water and Condensate System
- **Function:** Return condensed water back to the boiler as high-pressure feed water.
- **Key Units:** Condensate Extraction Pump (CEP), Deaerator, Low Pressure Feed Water Heaters, Boiler Feed Pump (BFP), High Pressure Heaters (HPH).
- **Instrumentation:** Feed water flow, temperature, dissolved oxygen, pump discharge pressure.

#### 7: Water Treatment Plant (WTP) and Demineralization (DM)
- **Function:** Remove hardness, silica, dissolved oxygen, and conductivity from raw water.
- **Key Units:** Clarifier, sand filter, two-bed / mixed-bed DM plant, dosing system.
- **Instrumentation:** Conductivity meters, pH meters, silica analyzers.

#### 8: Switchyard and Grid Interface
- **Function:** Step-up voltage and transmit electrical power to the grid.
- **Key Units:** Generator transformer (GT), unit auxiliary transformer (UAT), circuit breakers, bus bars, lightning arresters.
- **Instrumentation:** Current transformers (CT), potential transformers (PT), protective relays.

### 2.2 Boiler Classification — A KTU-Focused Hierarchy

The **KTU 2024 Module 1** explicitly requires knowledge of **boiler types**. The complete classification is:

```
BOILERS
 ├── Based on Tube Content
 │    ├── Fire-Tube Boiler (smoke inside tubes, water outside)
 │    └── Water-Tube Boiler (water inside tubes, hot gas outside)
 ├── Based on Pressure
 │    ├── Low Pressure (< 10 bar)
 │    ├── Medium Pressure (10 – 40 bar)
 │    ├── High Pressure (40 – 100 bar)
 │    └── Super-Critical (> 225 bar, no phase change)
 ├── Based on Circulation
 │    ├── Natural Circulation
 │    ├── Assisted / Controlled Circulation
 │    └── Once-Through (Forced Circulation)
 ├── Based on Fuel
 │    ├── Pulverized Fuel (PF) Boiler
 │    ├── Fluidized Bed Combustion (FBC) — CFB / AFBC
 │    ├── Stoker-Fired Boiler
 │    ├── Oil / Gas Fired Boiler
 │    └── Waste Heat Recovery Boiler
 └── Based on Furnace Position
      ├── Top-Supported
      └── Bottom-Supported (Tangential / Corner-Fired)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulverized Fuel Boilers are always Water-Tube boilers.** They are used in **70 % of the world's installed thermal capacity** because pulverization allows complete, fast combustion, enabling very high ratings (500 MW, 660 MW, 800 MW, 1000 MW units).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Thermal efficiency of plant | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\text{Heat Rate (HR)} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{thermal}}}$ | Heat input per kWh generated | $\text{kJ/kWh}$ |
| 3 | $\text{Specific Steam Consumption} = \dfrac{3600}{(h_1 - h_2)}$ | Steam required per kWh | $\text{kg/kWh}$ |
| 4 | $\text{Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta \cdot \text{GCV}}$ | Fuel required per kWh | $\text{kg/kWh}$ |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_3 - h_4)}{(h_1 - h_4)}$ | Rankine cycle efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Steam Flow } (m) = \dfrac{P \times 3600}{(h_1 - h_2) \times \eta_{\text{m\&e}}}$ | Mass flow of steam | $\text{kg/h}$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{combustion}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Losses}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Boiler efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss, dry flue gas}} = m_g \, c_p \, (T_g - T_a)$ | Flue gas heat loss | $\text{kJ/s}$ |
| 9 | $\text{Excess Air} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2\%}{21 - \text{O}_2\%} \times 100$ | Combustion excess air | % |
| 10 | $\text{Air Requirement} = 11.6 \times \text{C} + 34.8 \times \text{(H}_2 - \text{O}_2/8) + 4.35 \times \text{S}$ | Stoichiometric air for fuel | $\text{kg/kg fuel}$ |

Where the standard enthalpy points used in the Rankine cycle are:
- $h_1$ = Enthalpy of superheated steam at turbine inlet
- $h_2$ = Enthalpy at turbine exhaust
- $h_3$ = Enthalpy of saturated liquid at condenser
- $h_4$ = Enthalpy of compressed liquid at boiler inlet

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board examinations, students are expected to remember **Formulas 1, 2, 5, and 7** from this cheat sheet. **Formulas 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 10** are bonus tools for numerical problems.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are the backbone of **Power Plant Performance Engineering**:

- **Heat Rate benchmarking** — used by CEA (Central Electricity Authority, India) to grade plants.
- **SFC optimization** — directly translates to coal savings and tariff reduction.
- **Excess air monitoring** — too much air = heat loss; too little = incomplete combustion → CO spike.
- **Efficiency improvement of 1 %** in a 500 MW unit saves approximately **30,000 tonnes of coal per year**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Power Plant Instrumentation (PEAET751)**, the formulas above are not just for performance calculation — they also determine the **set-points and ranges of instruments**. For example, knowing the expected steam flow (Formula 6) tells the engineer to size the **flow transmitter (FT)** and **orifice plate** appropriately.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Efficiency of a Simple Rankine Cycle

The Rankine cycle is the theoretical cycle on which every steam-based thermal power plant operates. The derivation begins with the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a closed cycle:

$$
\oint dQ = \oint dW \quad \text{(Net work output equals net heat input)}
$$

**Step 1: Identify the four ideal Rankine processes**

| Process | Component | Description |
|:--------|:----------|:------------|
| 1 → 2 | Boiler | Heat addition at constant pressure |
| 2 → 3 | Turbine | Isentropic expansion producing work |
| 3 → 4 | Condenser | Heat rejection at constant pressure |
| 4 → 1 | Pump | Isentropic compression of liquid |

**Step 2: Express heat added in the boiler**

The heat added per kg of working fluid (water/steam) in the boiler is the enthalpy rise from the pump exit to the turbine inlet:

$$
q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4
$$

This is the **total heat input** to the cycle from the fuel combustion.

**Step 3: Express heat rejected in the condenser**

The heat rejected per kg of working fluid in the condenser is the enthalpy drop from the turbine exhaust to the saturated liquid leaving the condenser:

$$
q_{\text{out}} = h_2 - h_3
$$

This is the **waste heat dumped** to the cooling water.

**Step 4: Express the net work output**

The net work per kg is the turbine work minus the pump work:

$$
w_{\text{net}} = w_{\text{turbine}} - w_{\text{pump}} = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)
$$

**Step 5: Apply the thermal efficiency definition**

Thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work output to heat input:

$$
\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \frac{w_{\text{net}}}{q_{\text{in}}}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute the enthalpy expressions**

$$
\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}
$$

Since the pump work term $(h_4 - h_3)$ is small compared to the turbine work, it is often neglected in introductory KTU problems:

$$
\eta_{\text{Rankine}} \approx \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3}
$$

This is the **standard Rankine efficiency equation** that must be memorized.

> [!NOTE]
> The pump work in real plants is typically $0.5\text{ to } 1.5\text{ %}$ of turbine work. It becomes important only for **supercritical** units and **regenerative feed water heating** configurations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Rate and Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)

**Step 1: Start with the definition of Heat Rate**

Heat Rate is the inverse of thermal efficiency, scaled per unit of electrical energy:

$$
\text{Heat Rate (HR)} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{W_{\text{electrical}}} = \frac{1}{\eta_{\text{thermal}}}
$$

**Step 2: Convert from dimensionless efficiency to kJ/kWh**

Since electrical work is conventionally expressed per kWh ($3600 \text{ kJ} = 1 \text{ kWh}$):

$$
\text{HR} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{\text{thermal}}} \quad \text{[kJ/kWh]}
$$

To convert to $\text{kCal/kWh}$, divide by $4.186$:

$$
\text{HR (kCal/kWh)} = \frac{860}{\eta_{\text{thermal}}}
$$

**Step 3: Derive Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)**

SSC is the kg of steam required to produce 1 kWh of electrical energy. Starting from the work per kg of steam:

$$
w_{\text{net}} = h_1 - h_2 \quad \text{[kJ/kg steam]}
$$

The mass of steam per kWh is:

$$
\text{SSC} = \frac{3600}{h_1 - h_2} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]}
$$

**Step 4: Account for mechanical-electrical efficiency**

In practice, there are mechanical losses in the turbine and electrical losses in the generator. The combined mechanical-electrical efficiency $\eta_{\text{m\&e}}$ is typically **96–98 %**:

$$
\text{SSC}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{3600}{(h_1 - h_2) \cdot \eta_{\text{m\&e}}}
$$

**Step 5: Derive Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**

The SFC depends on the Gross Calorific Value (GCV) of the fuel and the overall plant efficiency:

$$
\text{SFC} = \frac{3600}{\text{GCV} \times \eta_{\text{plant}}} \quad \text{[kg of fuel / kWh]}
$$

For Indian coal with $\text{GCV} = 4000 \text{ kCal/kg} = 16744 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and $\eta_{\text{plant}} = 0.35$:

$$
\text{SFC} = \frac{3600}{16744 \times 0.35} = 0.614 \text{ kg/kWh} = 614 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the standard benchmark used in KTU numericals. A modern **500 MW supercritical unit** achieves **SFC ≈ 0.55 kg/kWh**, while an **old sub-critical unit** operates near **0.65–0.70 kg/kWh**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stoichiometric Air Required for Coal Combustion

The oxygen required for complete combustion of a fuel containing Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Sulphur (S) is determined from the molecular weight of the reactions:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (12 \text{ kg C} : 32 \text{ kg O}_2)
$$

$$
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (4 \text{ kg H}_2 : 32 \text{ kg O}_2)
$$

$$
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad (32 \text{ kg S} : 32 \text{ kg O}_2)
$$

**Step 1: Calculate oxygen required per kg of fuel**

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{8}{3}\text{C} + 8\text{H}_2 - \text{O}_2 + \text{S} \quad \text{[kg O}_2 \text{/kg fuel]}
$$

The $\text{O}_2$ term is subtracted because the oxygen already present in the fuel also contributes to combustion.

**Step 2: Convert oxygen to air**

Since air is **23.2 % oxygen by mass**, the theoretical air required is:

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{0.232} = 4.31 \times m_{\text{O}_2}
$$

**Step 3: Apply excess air factor**

In practice, additional excess air is supplied to ensure complete combustion. With **20 % excess air**:

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{actual}} = 1.20 \times \text{Air}_{\text{theoretical}}
$$

**Numerical Example for KTU:**

Consider a coal with the following ultimate analysis: C = 60 %, H₂ = 4 %, S = 1 %, O₂ = 6 %, N₂ = 1 %, Ash = 8 %, Moisture = 20 %.

The theoretical oxygen required per kg of coal is:

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{8}{3}(0.60) + 8(0.04) - (0.06) + (0.01) = 1.600 + 0.320 - 0.060 + 0.010 = 1.870 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}
$$

The theoretical air required is:

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{1.870}{0.232} = 8.06 \text{ kg air/kg coal}
$$

With 25 % excess air:

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{actual}} = 1.25 \times 8.06 = 10.08 \text{ kg air/kg coal}
$$

This is the **standard stoichiometry value** used to size the FD (Forced Draft) and ID (Induced Draft) fans in the boiler.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When the question says "ultimate analysis of coal is given," always use the formula $\text{Air} = 11.6\text{C} + 34.8(\text{H}_2 - \text{O}_2/8) + 4.35\text{S}$ to find the **theoretical air**, then multiply by $(1 + \text{excess air fraction})$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boiling Point vs. Boiler Type

| Boiler Type | Pressure (bar) | Capacity (T/h) | Efficiency (%) | Application |
|:------------|:-------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|:------------|
| Lancashire | 1 – 7 | 1 – 8 | 60 – 70 | Small industry |
| Cochran | 7 – 15 | 1 – 30 | 65 – 75 | Small industry |
| Water-Tube (sub-critical) | 150 – 190 | 400 – 2100 | 88 – 92 | Large power plants |
| Pulverized Fuel (PF) | 170 – 250 | 500 – 3000 | 90 – 94 | Base-load power |
| Super-Critical PF | > 225 | 800 – 3000 | 92 – 95 | Modern base-load |
| AFBC (Atmospheric Fluidized Bed) | 40 – 100 | 50 – 500 | 85 – 90 | Low-grade coal |
| CFBC (Circulating Fluidized Bed) | 40 – 150 | 100 – 800 | 88 – 92 | Pet-coke, biomass |
| Waste Heat Recovery | varies | 10 – 200 | 70 – 80 | Process industries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Pulverized Fuel Boiler** is the **most common boiler** in the KTU syllabus. The detailed study of the Pulverized Fuel Boiler is in **Section 3.5**.

### 3.5 Detailed Mechanism of a Pulverized Fuel Boiler (PFB)

The PFB is the workhorse of modern thermal power generation. The KTU Module 1 specifically requires detailed study of this boiler. The operation is broken into seven sub-systems:

#### Subsystem A: Coal Pulverization (Milling)
Coal from the bunkers is fed via **gravimetric feeders** to the **pulverizer / mill**. The mill performs three actions simultaneously:

- **Crushing** — using balls (ball mill) or rollers (vertical roller mill / HPGR).
- **Drying** — using hot primary air from the air preheater.
- **Classification** — using dynamic classifiers that return oversized particles back to the grinding zone.

The final product is **PF (Pulverized Fuel)** of fineness **70 % through 200 mesh (75 µm)**.

#### Subsystem B: Primary Air and Coal Transport
The pulverized coal is **pneumatically conveyed** to the burner nozzles using **hot primary air** (≈ 250–300 °C). The air-coal mixture has a **coal-to-air ratio** of approximately 1:2 to 1:3 by mass.

#### Subsystem C: Combustion in the Furnace
The burners are arranged in:

- **Tangential (Corner-Fired)** — most common for Indian units, four corners firing into a vortex.
- **Wall-Fired (Front-Fired / Opposed-Fired)** — burners on a single wall or opposed walls.

The furnace temperature reaches **1400–1600 °C** with a residence time of **2–4 seconds**. Approximately **85–90 %** of the coal combustion occurs in suspension, the rest in the bottom ash hopper.

#### Subsystem D: Heat Transfer Surfaces
The hot flue gases transfer heat to the water/steam through:

- **Water Walls** — generate saturated steam (radiation heat transfer).
- **Primary Superheater (SH-I)** — raises steam from saturated to ~440 °C.
- **Platen Superheater (SH-II)** — radiant superheater, raises to ~510 °C.
- **Final Superheater (SH-III)** — convective superheater, raises to ~535–600 °C.
- **Reheater (RH)** — re-superheats the cold reheat steam returning from HP/IP turbine exhaust to ~540 °C.
- **Economizer (ECO)** — preheats the feed water using flue gas heat.
- **Primary Air Heater (PAH)** — heats the air going to the mill.
- **Secondary Air Heater (SAH)** — heats the air going to the burners.

#### Subsystem E: Combustion Air System
- **Forced Draft (FD) Fan** — supplies fresh air to the air preheater.
- **Primary Air (PA) Fan** — supplies hot air to the mill.
- **Induced Draft (ID) Fan** — maintains furnace draft by drawing flue gas out of the system.
- **Excess air** of **15–25 %** is maintained to ensure complete combustion.

#### Subsystem F: Ash Handling System
- **Bottom Ash** (~20 % of total ash) — falls into the bottom ash hopper, is quenched and conveyed.
- **Fly Ash** (~80 %) — carried by flue gas, collected in:
  - **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** — efficiency > 99.9 %
  - **Bag Filters** — efficiency > 99.99 %

#### Subsystem G: Flue Gas Path
The flue gas path is:

$$
\text{Furnace} \rightarrow \text{SH} \rightarrow \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ECO} \rightarrow \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{ESP} \rightarrow \text{ID Fan} \rightarrow \text{Stack}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Instrumentation Course (PEAET751):** Every point in Subsystem A–G has specific instruments. The most critical are:
> - **Drum level** (DP transmitter)
> - **Furnace draft** (pressure transmitter)
> - **Flue gas O₂** (Zirconia probe)
> - **Coal flow** (gravimetric feeder)
> - **Steam pressure and temperature** (PT and RTD)
> - **NOx, SOx** (in-situ analyzers in the chimney)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Thermal Power Plant Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[COAL HANDLING PLANT] --> A2[PULVERIZER MILL]
    A2 --> A3[BOILER FURNACE]
    A4[FORCED DRAFT FAN] --> A5[AIR PREHEATER]
    A5 --> A3
    A3 --> A6[SUPERHEATER]
    A6 --> A7[HP TURBINE]
    A7 --> A8[IP TURBINE]
    A8 --> A9[LP TURBINE]
    A9 --> A10[CONDENSER]
    A10 --> A11[CONDENSATE PUMP]
    A11 --> A12[DEAERATOR]
    A12 --> A13[BOILER FEED PUMP]
    A13 --> A14[ECONOMIZER]
    A14 --> A3
    A7 --> A15[REHEATER]
    A15 --> A8
    A3 --> A16[ESP ELECTROSTATIC PRECIPITATOR]
    A16 --> A17[INDUCED DRAFT FAN]
    A17 --> A18[STACK]
    A9 --> A19[GENERATOR]
    A19 --> A20[SWITCHYARD AND GRID]
    A10 --> A21[COOLING TOWER]
    A21 --> A10
```

**Description of the Block Flow:**

The **coal handling plant** delivers crushed coal to the **pulverizer mill**, which grinds it to PF. The **FD fan** supplies combustion air through the **air preheater**, where it is heated by the flue gas. The mixture of air and PF burns in the **boiler furnace**, generating high-pressure superheated steam in the **superheater**. The steam drives the **HP-IP-LP turbines**, which turn the **generator** producing electricity. The **reheater** returns partially expanded steam to the IP turbine for improved efficiency. Exhaust steam is condensed in the **condenser** (cooled by the **cooling tower**), and the condensate is pressurized by the **condensate pump**, deaerated, and pumped back to the **boiler** via the **economizer** by the **boiler feed pump**. Flue gas exits through the **ESP** (which captures fly ash) and the **ID fan** discharges it via the **stack**.

### 4.2 Schematic — Pulverized Fuel Boiler (PFB) Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PFB[PULVERIZED FUEL BOILER]
        direction TB
        B1[FURNACE WATER WALLS] --> B2[PLATEN SUPERHEATER]
        B2 --> B3[FINAL SUPERHEATER]
        B3 --> B4[REHEATER]
        B4 --> B5[ECONOMIZER]
        B5 --> B6[PRIMARY AIR HEATER]
        B6 --> B7[SECONDARY AIR HEATER]
        B7 --> B8[ESP AND STACK]
    end
    subgraph BURNER[BURNER ZONE]
        C1[PF FROM MILL] --> C2[TANGENTIAL CORNER BURNERS]
        C2 --> B1
        C3[SECONDARY AIR] --> C2
    end
    subgraph STEAM[SATURATED STEAM DRUM]
        D1[STEAM DRUM] --> B3
        B1 --> D1
        D1 --> B2
    end
    subgraph FW[FEED WATER PATH]
        E1[BOILER FEED PUMP] --> B5
        B5 --> D1
    end
```

**Description of the PFB Architecture:**

The **pulverized fuel from the mill** is mixed with **hot primary air** and injected through **tangential corner burners** into the **furnace water walls**. The flame core reaches **1500 °C+**, generating saturated steam. The saturated steam rises to the **steam drum**, then flows through the **platen superheater** (radiant) and **final superheater** (convective) before exiting as superheated steam at **535–600 °C** to the HP turbine. The partially expanded steam returns through the **reheater** to the IP turbine. Feed water from the **boiler feed pump** first passes through the **economizer** (where it is preheated by flue gas) and then enters the **steam drum**. The flue gas path is: furnace → superheaters → reheater → economizer → primary air heater → secondary air heater → **ESP** (fly ash removal) → **stack**.

### 4.3 Schematic — Instrumentation and Control Loops on a PFB

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[INSTRUMENTATION LOOPS]
        L1[DRUM LEVEL CONTROL - 3E] --> V1[FEEDWATER VALVE]
        L2[FURNACE DRAFT CONTROL] --> V2[ID FAN DAMPER]
        L3[COMBUSTION O2 CONTROL] --> V3[FD FAN DAMPER]
        L4[STEAM TEMP CONTROL] --> V4[DESUPERHEATER VALVE]
        L5[STEAM PRESSURE CONTROL] --> V5[FUEL FEEDER]
        L6[COAL MILL DP CONTROL] --> V6[PA FAN]
    end
    subgraph SENS[SENSORS]
        S1[DRUM LEVEL TX] --> L1
        S2[FURNACE DRAFT PT] --> L2
        S3[OXYGEN ANALYZER] --> L3
        S4[THERMOCOUPLE] --> L4
        S5[PRESSURE TX] --> L5
        S6[DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE TX] --> L6
    end
```

**Description of the Instrumentation Loops:**

The **drum level transmitter** sends a signal to the **drum level controller** which modulates the **feed water control valve** (3-element control using steam flow, feed water flow, and drum level). The **furnace draft pressure transmitter** controls the **ID fan damper** to maintain a slightly negative furnace pressure (about $-5 \text{ to } -10 \text{ mm WC}$). The **oxygen analyzer** in the flue gas duct controls the **FD fan damper** to maintain $\text{O}_2$ at $2\text{–}4\,\%$. The **steam temperature thermocouple** controls the **desuperheater spray valve** to maintain the final steam temperature. The **steam pressure transmitter** controls the **coal feeder speed** to follow load demand. The **differential pressure transmitter** across the mill controls the **primary air fan** to maintain mill outlet temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3-Element Drum Level Control** is the most important loop in the PFB. Students are expected to draw and explain this loop in the KTU board examination.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define a Thermal Power Plant. List the major building blocks of a modern thermal power station.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Thermal Power Plant** is an industrial installation that converts the chemical energy of fossil fuel (coal, oil, gas) into electrical energy through the Rankine cycle. The major building blocks are:

1. **Coal Handling Plant (CHP)** — receives, stores, and crushes coal.
2. **Pulverizing / Milling System** — grinds coal to PF of required fineness.
3. **Boiler / Steam Generator** — converts feed water to superheated steam.
4. **Turbine-Generator Set (TG)** — converts steam energy to electricity.
5. **Condenser and Cooling System** — condenses exhaust steam and dumps waste heat.
6. **Feed Water and Condensate System** — returns water to the boiler.
7. **Water Treatment Plant** — purifies raw water.
8. **Switchyard and Grid** — transmits electrical power to the grid.

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the eight blocks (½ mark each block).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2022]**
**What is a Pulverized Fuel Boiler? Why is the coal pulverized before firing?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pulverized Fuel Boiler (PFB)** is a water-tube boiler in which the coal is ground to a fine powder (about 70 % through 200 mesh / 75 µm) before being injected with air into the furnace for combustion. PFBs are used in large base-load thermal power plants (500 MW to 1000 MW) for their high efficiency, fast response, and complete combustion.

Coal is pulverized before firing for the following reasons:

1. **Increased surface area** — pulverization (size < 75 µm) provides an enormous surface-area-to-volume ratio, enabling **rapid and complete combustion** in 2–4 seconds.
2. **Uniform combustion** — fine particles mix uniformly with air, avoiding pockets of unburnt fuel.
3. **Higher efficiency** — complete combustion gives **boiler efficiency of 90–94 %**.
4. **Lower excess air** — finer particles need less excess air (15–20 %), reducing flue gas heat loss.
5. **Stable flame** — pulverized fuel produces a stable, controllable flame suitable for large furnace volumes.
6. **Higher capacity** — supports very high steam generation (up to 3000 T/h per unit).

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the six reasons (½ mark each, or grouped as 1 mark each for first three).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Thermal Power Plant** based on the **Rankine cycle**. Discuss the function of each major block. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A 200 MW thermal power station uses coal with GCV = 4500 kcal/kg. The plant operates at an overall efficiency of 35 %. Calculate the **(i)** heat rate, **(ii)** specific fuel consumption per kWh, and **(iii)** total coal consumption per day at full load. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Rankine Cycle (2 marks)**

The Rankine cycle is the ideal thermodynamic cycle for steam power plants, consisting of four processes:
- Process 4→1: Compression of saturated liquid in the pump.
- Process 1→2: Heat addition at constant pressure in the boiler (saturated liquid → superheated steam).
- Process 2→3: Isentropic expansion in the turbine (work output).
- Process 3→4: Heat rejection at constant pressure in the condenser.

**Step 2: Block Diagram and Function (5 marks)**

The major blocks of a thermal power plant are:

1. **Coal Handling Plant** — receives coal, stores, and crushes it to 20 mm size.
2. **Pulverizer / Mill** — grinds the crushed coal to PF of fineness 70 % through 200 mesh.
3. **Furnace / Boiler** — water walls, superheaters, and economizer convert water to superheated steam.
4. **Steam Turbine (HP-IP-LP)** — expands steam to produce mechanical rotation on a common shaft.
5. **Generator** — synchronous AC generator (typically 21 kV, 50 Hz in India) coupled to the turbine.
6. **Condenser** — surface-type condenser that condenses LP exhaust steam to water.
7. **Cooling Tower** — cools the warm cooling water returning from the condenser.
8. **Feed Water Cycle** — CEP, deaerator, BFP, and HP heaters return pure water to the boiler.
9. **Switchyard** — step-up transformer and bus bars for grid interface.
10. **Ash Handling System** — bottom ash and ESP-collected fly ash disposal/reuse.

**Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Power output $P = 200 \text{ MW} = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ kW}$
- $\text{GCV} = 4500 \text{ kCal/kg}$
- Overall efficiency $\eta = 35\,\% = 0.35$

**Step 1: Calculate Heat Rate (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{Heat Rate} = \frac{860}{\eta} = \frac{860}{0.35} = 2457.14 \text{ kCal/kWh}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Specific Fuel Consumption (2 marks)**

$$
\text{SFC} = \frac{3600}{\text{GCV} \times \eta} = \frac{3600}{4500 \times 0.35} = 2.286 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\text{SFC} = \frac{860}{4500 \times 0.35} = 0.546 \text{ kg/kWh} \;\;(\text{since } 1 \text{ kWh} = 860 \text{ kCal})
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Daily Coal Consumption at Full Load (3.5 marks)**

Energy generated per day at full load:

$$
E_{\text{daily}} = 200{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 4{,}800{,}000 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Total coal consumed per day:

$$
\text{Coal}_{\text{daily}} = \text{SFC} \times E_{\text{daily}} = 2.286 \times 4{,}800{,}000 = 10{,}972{,}800 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
\text{Coal}_{\text{daily}} \approx 10{,}973 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

Or in **tonnes per hour**:

$$
\text{Coal}_{\text{hourly}} = \frac{10{,}973}{24} = 457.2 \text{ tonnes/hour}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Heat Rate = **2457.14 kCal/kWh**
- (ii) SFC = **2.286 kg/kWh** (or 0.546 kg/kWh if using the 860 conversion)
- (iii) Daily coal consumption = **≈ 10,973 tonnes/day**

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model]**

**a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **Pulverized Fuel Boiler (PFB)**. List the major sub-systems. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 64 %, H₂ = 4 %, S = 1 %, O₂ = 8 %, N₂ = 2 %, Moisture = 12 %, Ash = 9 %. Calculate the **(i)** theoretical oxygen required for complete combustion per kg of coal, **(ii)** theoretical air required, and **(iii)** actual air supplied if 30 % excess air is used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Construction (3 marks)**

A Pulverized Fuel Boiler is a water-tube boiler with the following construction features:

- **Furnace** — large rectangular chamber lined with **water-wall tubes** welded together as panels.
- **Burners** — arranged **tangentially** in the four corners of the furnace (corner-fired) to create a swirling fireball.
- **Steam Drum** — at the top, separates steam from water.
- **Superheaters** — platen (radiant), primary, and final (convective) sections.
- **Reheater** — receives cold reheat steam and re-superheats it.
- **Economizer** — preheats feed water using flue gas heat.
- **Air Preheaters** — primary (for mill) and secondary (for burner) — Ljungström rotary type.
- **ESP** — electrostatic precipitator at the back end.
- **Pulverizer Mills** — ball mills or vertical roller mills.
- **Fans** — FD, ID, and PA fans.

**Step 2: Working (3 marks)**

Coal from the bunkers is fed via **gravimetric feeders** to the **pulverizer mill**, where it is simultaneously crushed, dried, and classified. The hot primary air carries the PF to the **corner burners** through fuel lines. The burners fire tangentially to create a vortex of fire, with temperatures reaching 1500 °C. Water in the **water walls** is converted to saturated steam, which rises to the **steam drum**, then to the **superheaters** (raising it to 535–600 °C) before exiting to the HP turbine. Partially expanded steam returns through the **reheater** to the IP turbine. The flue gas then passes through the **economizer** (heating feed water) and the **air preheater** (heating combustion air), before being cleaned in the **ESP** and discharged to the **stack** by the **ID fan**.

**Step 3: Major Sub-Systems (1 mark)**

The seven sub-systems are: (i) Coal Pulverization, (ii) Primary Air and PF Transport, (iii) Combustion, (iv) Heat Transfer Surfaces, (v) Combustion Air System, (vi) Ash Handling, (vii) Flue Gas Path.

**Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given Data (per kg of coal):**
- C = 0.64 kg, H₂ = 0.04 kg, S = 0.01 kg, O₂ = 0.08 kg, N₂ = 0.02 kg, Moisture = 0.12 kg, Ash = 0.09 kg.

**Step 1: Calculate theoretical oxygen required (3 marks)**

The combustion reactions:
- C + O₂ → CO₂ (32 kg O₂ for 12 kg C, ratio = 8/3)
- H₂ + ½ O₂ → H₂O (32 kg O₂ for 4 kg H₂, ratio = 8)
- S + O₂ → SO₂ (32 kg O₂ for 32 kg S, ratio = 1)

The total oxygen required:

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{8}{3}\text{C} + 8\text{H}_2 - \text{O}_2 + \text{S}
$$

Substituting:

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{8}{3}(0.64) + 8(0.04) - 0.08 + 0.01
$$

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = 1.7067 + 0.320 - 0.080 + 0.010 = 1.9567 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate theoretical air required (2 marks)**

Air contains 23.2 % oxygen by mass, so:

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{0.232} = \frac{1.9567}{0.232} = 8.434 \text{ kg air/kg coal}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate actual air with 30 % excess (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{actual}} = 1.30 \times 8.434 = 10.964 \text{ kg air/kg coal}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Theoretical O₂ = **1.957 kg/kg coal**
- (ii) Theoretical Air = **8.434 kg/kg coal**
- (iii) Actual Air (30 % excess) = **10.964 kg/kg coal**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **In Rankine cycle derivations:** Forgetting the **pump work term** $(h_4 - h_3)$ in the final expression. Always write the complete formula with pump work, then mention that for sub-critical problems it can be neglected.
> 2. **In coal combustion numericals:** Confusing **mass fraction (C = 0.64)** with **percentage (C = 64 %)**. Either both forms give the same answer if used correctly, but mixing them is a common error.
> 3. **In the formula $m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{8}{3}\text{C} + 8\text{H}_2 - \text{O}_2 + \text{S}$:** Forgetting to **subtract the O₂ already present in the fuel** — this is a 1-mark deduction guaranteed.
> 4. **In air calculation:** Using **21 % (volume basis)** instead of **23.2 % (mass basis)**. KTU expects the mass-basis value.
> 5. **In block diagrams:** Drawing the blocks but not **labelling** the energy/medium flow on each arrow. The KTU evaluator allocates **½ mark for the label on each arrow**.
> 6. **In PFB explanations:** Forgetting to mention the **fineness (70 % through 200 mesh)** and the **furnace temperature (1400–1600 °C)**. These are recall-type questions and missing them costs 1 mark each.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam (Module 1):**
>
> **A. Process Fundamentals**
> - Thermal Power Plant converts: **Chemical → Thermal → Mechanical → Electrical** energy.
> - The plant efficiency is **33–42 %**; main losses are in the **condenser** (~50 %) and **flue gas** (~15 %).
> - **Rankine cycle** has four ideal processes: pump (compression), boiler (heat addition), turbine (expansion), condenser (heat rejection).
> - Thermal efficiency formula: $\eta = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$
>
> **B. Building Blocks (must list all eight)**
> - CHP, Milling, Boiler, TG Set, Condenser & Cooling, Feed Water Cycle, WTP/DM Plant, Switchyard.
>
> **C. Boiler Classification**
> - Must remember: **Fire-tube vs Water-tube** (primary classification), **sub-critical vs super-critical** (pressure-based), and **PF vs FBC vs Waste Heat** (fuel-based).
>
> **D. Pulverized Fuel Boiler — Key Data Points**
> - **Fineness:** 70 % through 200 mesh (75 µm).
> - **Furnace temperature:** 1400–1600 °C.
> - **Excess air:** 15–25 %.
> - **Air-Fuel ratio (primary):** 1:2 to 1:3 by mass.
> - **Coal residence time:** 2–4 seconds.
> - **Steam pressure (sub-critical):** 170–190 bar.
> - **Steam temperature:** 535–600 °C.
> - **Burner arrangement:** Tangential (corner) firing is the most common in India.
>
> **E. Instrumentation Hot Spots**
> - **Drum level control** — 3-element (level, steam flow, feed water flow).
> - **Combustion control** — O₂ in flue gas = 2–4 %.
> - **Furnace draft** — typically -5 to -10 mm WC (slightly negative).
> - **Steam temperature control** — desuperheater spray.
>
> **F. Numerical Benchmarks (must memorize)**
> - **Heat Rate (sub-critical)** ≈ 2400–2500 kCal/kWh.
> - **Heat Rate (super-critical)** ≈ 1900–2100 kCal/kWh.
> - **SFC (Indian coal, 4500 kCal/kg)** ≈ 0.55–0.65 kg/kWh.
> - **Specific Steam Consumption** ≈ 4.5–5.5 kg/kWh.
> - **Theoretical air for coal** ≈ 7–9 kg air/kg coal.
> - **With 20 % excess air** ≈ 8.5–10.5 kg air/kg coal.
>
> **G. High-Yield Diagrams (must be able to draw)**
> - Block diagram of a Thermal Power Plant (8 blocks).
> - Cross-section / flow path of a Pulverized Fuel Boiler.
> - 3-Element Drum Level Control loop.
> - T-s or P-h diagram of Rankine cycle.

**END OF KTU MODULE 1 NOTES — PEAET751 POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Instrumentation in Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Importance of Instrumentation in Power Generation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > *Power Plant Instrumentation* refers to the integrated network of sensors, transducers, signal conditioners, transmitters, controllers, recorders, displays, and final control elements (actuators) employed to **measure, monitor, record, and control** the physical, chemical, and electrical process variables governing the safe, efficient, and environmentally compliant operation of a power generation station. In the context of KTU 2024 Module 1, the **Importance of Instrumentation** establishes the foundational justification for studying this course. Instrumentation is the *nervous system* of any modern power plant — without it, the plant cannot operate safely, efficiently, or economically. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Human Body & a Power Plant** > - A power plant is like a **human body** operating under extreme stress. > - **Temperature sensors** act as *skin* (detecting overheating). > - **Pressure sensors** act as *blood pressure cuffs* (preventing ruptures). > - **Flow meters** act as the *circulatory system monitors* (ensuring fluid supply). > - **Controllers** act as the *brain* (making decisions). > - **Actuators** act as *muscles* (executing actions). > - **SCADA/DCS** acts as the *central nervous system* (coordinating everything). > > Just as a human cannot survive without continuous physiological feedback, a power plant cannot operate reliably without continuous instrumentation feedback. ### 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics & Standards > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics Used in Power Plant Instrumentation:** > - **Accuracy:** Typically **±0.1% to ±0.5%** of full scale (FS) for critical loops. > - **Availability Target:** Modern plants aim for **≥ 95%** (with instrumentation redundancy ≥ 99.9%). > - **Response Time:** Critical safety loops require **< 1 second**. > - **Standards Body:** **ISA, IEEE, IEC, ISO 9001/14001, CEA (Central Electricity Authority, India)**. > - **Plant Heat Rate (PHR):** Benchmark **2,000 – 2,500 kcal/kWh** for efficient plants. > - **Plant Load Factor (PLF):** Target **> 80%** for well-instrumented plants. ### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme This is a **2-mark / 3-mark high-frequency topic** that appears in: - Module 1 descriptive questions - Part A of University Exam (short notes) - Often embedded as an introductory paragraph in 14-mark questions - Viva voce / KTU oral assessments --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Instrumentation Importance #### **Pillar 1 — SAFETY of Personnel and Equipment** - Power plants operate with **steam at 540–600 °C** and **pressures of 150–250 bar** in supercritical units. - A sudden overpressure can cause a **Boiler Tube Rupture (BTR)** or catastrophic explosion. - Instrumentation provides **early warning** through: - Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs) interlocked with pressure transmitters. - Flame scanners detecting furnace flame failure (FSSS — Flame Safeguard Supervisory System). - Trip systems (e.g., MFT — Master Fuel Trip) initiated by sensor signals. - **Rule of thumb:** Every **1% reduction** in measurement accuracy in critical loops can increase the probability of a hazardous event by **~10%**. #### **Pillar 2 — EFFICIENCY Optimization** - Even a **1 °C error** in flue gas temperature measurement can cause efficiency miscalculation of **~0.1%**. - For a **500 MW plant**, a **0.5% efficiency loss** translates to a revenue loss of approximately **₹ 3–5 crore/year** (depending on coal cost and tariff). - Instrumentation enables: - **Combustion optimization** (excess air control via O₂ analyzers). - **Boiler drum level control** (3-element control using drum level, steam flow, feedwater flow). - **Turbine speed/load control** (governor valves driven by speed sensors). #### **Pillar 3 — ENVIRONMENTAL Compliance** - Modern power plants must comply with **MoEFCC (India)** emission norms and international standards. - **CEMS** (Continuous Emission Monitoring System) measures: - **SO₂**, **NOₓ**, **CO**, **PM (particulate matter)**, **Hg**, **CO₂**. - Without accurate instrumentation, plants face **penalties, shutdowns**, and reputational damage. #### **Pillar 4 — RELIABILITY and Availability** - **Vibration monitoring** on turbines, generators, and pumps predicts bearing failures weeks in advance. - **Condition-based monitoring (CBM)** reduces unplanned downtime by **30–50%**. - **Redundancy (1oo2, 2oo3 voting)** in critical loops ensures single-point-of-failure protection. #### **Pillar 5 — ECONOMIC Operation and Data Analytics** - Real-time data acquisition enables: - **Performance auditing** (kWh per tonne of coal). - **Predictive maintenance** using AI/ML on historical data. - **Regulatory reporting** (Daily Generation Reports to Load Despatch Centres). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\dot{m}_{f} \times GCV}$ | Useful power output per unit heat input | Dimensionless (%) | | Plant Heat Rate (PHR) | $PHR = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{P_{out}} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ | Heat input per kWh of electricity | kcal/kWh or kJ/kWh | | Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) | $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{out}}$ | Fuel consumed per unit power | kg/kWh | | Measurement Error (%FS) | $E_{\%FS} = \dfrac{\vert x_{m} - x_{t} \vert}{x_{FS}} \times 100$ | Accuracy specification | % | | Reynolds Number (Flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime indicator | Dimensionless | | Power Output (3-Phase) | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi$ | Electrical output of generator | W or MW | | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output change per unit input change | V/unit or mA/unit | | Signal Conversion (RTD – Pt100) | $R_{T} = R_{0} \left[1 + A T + B T^{2} \right]$ | Resistance vs Temperature (for T < 0 °C) | Ω, °C | | Signal Conversion (RTD – Pt100) | $R_{T} = R_{0} \left[1 + A T + B T^{2} + C (T - 100) T^{3} \right]$ | Resistance vs Temperature (for T < 0 °C) | Ω, °C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for the 5 Pillars — "S-E-E-R-E":** > **S**afety → **E**fficiency → **E**nvironment → **R**eliability → **E**conomics ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **NTPC Ltd. (India)** uses fully instrumented supercritical units achieving **~41% net plant efficiency**, among the highest in the world. - **Reliance Sasan Ultra Mega Power Project** deploys **> 10,000 instrumentation points** per unit. - **SCADA-integrated plants** reduce human error by **> 70%** compared to manual operation. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: How Instrumentation Error Translates to Efficiency Loss We will derive a quantitative relationship between **flue gas temperature measurement error** and **boiler efficiency loss**, which is a high-yield KTU analytical problem. **Given Data (Typical Coal-Fired Boiler):** - Flue gas temperature: $T_{g} = 150$ °C - Ambient temperature: $T_{a} = 30$ °C - Specific heat of flue gas: $C_{pg} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K - Mass flow of flue gas: $\dot{m}_{g} = 100$ kg/s - Gross calorific value of coal: $GCV = 4{,}000$ kJ/kg - Coal feed rate: $\dot{m}_{f} = 10$ kg/s - Measured output: $P_{out} = 12$ MW **Step 1: Calculate the Heat Lost in Flue Gas (Without Instrumentation Error).** $$ Q_{loss,fg} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_{g} - T_{a}) $$ Substituting the values: $$ Q_{loss,fg} = 100 \cdot 1.05 \cdot (150 - 30) $$ $$ Q_{loss,fg} = 100 \cdot 1.05 \cdot 120 $$ $$ Q_{loss,fg} = 12{,}600 \text{ kJ/s} = 12.6 \text{ MW (thermal)} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the Heat Input.** $$ Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{f} \cdot GCV $$ $$ Q_{in} = 10 \cdot 4{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ kJ/s} = 40 \text{ MW (thermal)} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Baseline Boiler Efficiency.** $$ \eta_{baseline} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{12}{40} = 0.30 = 30\% $$ **Step 4: Introduce a Measurement Error in Flue Gas Temperature.** Suppose the temperature sensor has an error of **+5 °C** (i.e., it reads 150 °C when the actual temperature is 145 °C). The plant operator will then mistakenly allow higher firing to compensate. Recalculate flue gas loss with the erroneous reading: $$ Q_{loss,fg,\text{erroneous}} = 100 \cdot 1.05 \cdot (155 - 30) $$ $$ Q_{loss,fg,\text{erroneous}} = 100 \cdot 1.05 \cdot 125 = 13{,}125 \text{ kJ/s} $$ **Step 5: Calculate New Effective Efficiency Loss.** $$ \Delta Q_{loss} = 13{,}125 - 12{,}600 = 525 \text{ kJ/s} $$ $$ \eta_{loss,\%} = \dfrac{\Delta Q_{loss}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta_{loss,\%} = \dfrac{525}{40{,}000} \times 100 = 1.3125\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion of the Derivation:** > A mere **+5 °C error** in flue gas temperature measurement causes an **apparent efficiency loss of ~1.31%** due to operator overcompensation. For a 500 MW plant operating 8,000 hours/year, this translates to a **coal loss of ~3,500 tonnes/year** and a **monetary loss of ₹ 2–3 crore/year**. This is *why accurate instrumentation is economically critical.* ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Instrumentation Loops in a Typical Thermal Power Plant | **Process Variable** | **Primary Sensor** | **Purpose / Importance** | **KTU Exam Focus** | |---|---|---|---| | Steam Temperature | RTD / Thermocouple (K-type) | Prevents superheater tube failure | High | | Drum Level | Differential Pressure (DP) cell | Prevents boiler dry-out / water carryover | Very High | | FD/ID Fan Flow | Orifice + DP transmitter | Maintains combustion air balance | High | | O₂ in flue gas | Zirconia probe | Optimizes excess air (3–5%) | Very High | | Coal Feed Rate | Belt weigher / Loss-in-weight | Heat input calculation | Medium | | Turbine Vibration | Seismic / Proximity probe | Predicts bearing damage | High | | Generator Voltage/Current | PT / CT | Grid synchronization, protection | High | | Stack Emissions (SO₂/NOₓ) | UV / IR analyzer (CEMS) | Environmental compliance | Very High | ### 3.3 Why This Analysis Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** > Numerical problems combining **efficiency vs. measurement error** are frequently asked in the **14-mark section** under Module 1. Students must remember the master relation: > $$\eta = \dfrac{3600}{PHR} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot GCV}$$ > Always show the *baseline* and *erroneous* cases side by side for full marks. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Instrumentation Architecture in a Power Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Level1["LEVEL 1 - FIELD SENSING"] T1["Temperature Sensors
RTD Thermocouple"] P1["Pressure Sensors
Bourdon Diaphragm"] F1["Flow Sensors
Orifice Venturi"] L1["Level Sensors
DP Ultrasonic"] V1["Vibration Sensors
Seismic Probe"] G1["Gas Analyzers
O2 CO NOx SO2"] end subgraph Level2["LEVEL 2 - SIGNAL CONDITIONING AND TRANSMISSION"] SC1["Signal Conditioners
Amplification Filtering"] TX1["Transmitters
4 to 20 mA HART"] IS1["Intrinsic Safety Barriers"] end subgraph Level3["LEVEL 3 - CONTROL LAYER"] DCS1["DCS Controllers
PID Logic"] PLC1["Safety PLC
SIL 2 SIL 3 Trips"] FSSS1["FSSS
Flame Safeguard"] MFT1["MFT
Master Fuel Trip"] end subgraph Level4["LEVEL 4 - SUPERVISORY AND MONITORING"] HMI1["HMI Workstations
Operator Interface"] SCADA1["SCADA Server
Data Historian"] EMS1["Plant EMS
Energy Management"] end subgraph Level5["LEVEL 5 - ENTERPRISE AND ANALYTICS"] MIS1["MIS Reports
Daily Generation"] AI1["Predictive Analytics
AI ML Models"] REG1["Regulatory Reporting
CEA MoEFCC"] end T1 --> SC1 P1 --> SC1 F1 --> SC1 L1 --> SC1 V1 --> SC1 G1 --> SC1 SC1 --> TX1 TX1 --> IS1 IS1 --> DCS1 IS1 --> PLC1 DCS1 --> FSSS1 PLC1 --> MFT1 DCS1 --> HMI1 PLC1 --> HMI1 DCS1 --> SCADA1 PLC1 --> SCADA1 HMI1 --> EMS1 SCADA1 --> MIS1 SCADA1 --> AI1 SCADA1 --> REG1 classDef sensing fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#D2691E,color:#000 classDef cond fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef control fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 classDef super fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000 classDef enterp fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 class T1,P1,F1,L1,V1,G1 sensing class SC1,TX1,IS1 cond class DCS1,PLC1,FSSS1,MFT1 control class HMI1,SCADA1,EMS1 super class MIS1,AI1,REG1 enterp ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow: Role of Instrumentation in the Power Generation Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR A["COAL HANDLING
Plant"] --> B["BOILER
Combustion"] B --> C["TURBINE
Expansion"] C --> D["GENERATOR
Electrical Output"] D --> E["GRID
Transmission"] B -.->|"Instrumentation:
Drum Level, T, P"| INV1["CONTROL
LOOP 1"] C -.->|"Instrumentation:
Speed, Vibration"| INV2["CONTROL
LOOP 2"] D -.->|"Instrumentation:
V, I, f, PF"| INV3["CONTROL
LOOP 3"] B -.->|"CEMS:
O2, SO2, NOx"| INV4["ENV
MONITORING"] INV1 --> B INV2 --> C INV3 --> D INV4 -.->|"Alarms, Reports"| B classDef plant fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef instr fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E plant class INV1,INV2,INV3,INV4 instr ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - The **dashed arrows** from each plant block represent the *feedback* and *measurement* signals going to the control loops. > - The **solid arrows** represent the control *commands* returning from the controllers to the actuators. > - This **closed-loop** structure is the heart of automated power plant operation. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Q1.** List and briefly explain any **three reasons** why instrumentation is important in a power plant. > > **Model Answer:** > 1. **Safety:** Instrumentation continuously monitors critical parameters like steam pressure, drum level, and furnace flame. A sudden deviation triggers automatic trips (e.g., MFT), preventing explosions and protecting personnel and equipment. > 2. **Efficiency Optimization:** Sensors measuring oxygen in flue gas, feedwater temperature, and steam pressure enable combustion and heat-rate optimization, saving fuel and improving the plant heat rate (PHR). > 3. **Environmental Compliance:** CEMS instruments monitor SO₂, NOₓ, PM, and CO emissions in real time, helping the plant meet statutory norms set by MoEFCC and the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). > *(Any 3 from the 5 pillars S-E-E-R-E)* > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Expected]** > **Q2.** Differentiate between **CEMS** and **FSSS** in a power plant. > > **Model Answer:** > - **CEMS (Continuous Emission Monitoring System):** An *environmental* instrumentation system that continuously measures flue gas emissions (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, PM, O₂) at the stack to ensure regulatory compliance. > - **FSSS (Flame Safeguard Supervisory System):** A *safety* instrumentation system that monitors the furnace flame using UV/IR scanners. If flame failure is detected, it initiates a Master Fuel Trip (MFT) within milliseconds to cut off fuel supply and prevent a furnace explosion. > - **Key Difference:** CEMS is *environmental & analytical*; FSSS is *safety & protective*. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Actual Pattern]** #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **Q(a).** Explain in detail the **role of instrumentation in ensuring safety** of personnel and equipment in a thermal power plant. Mention any **four critical safety interlocks** with their associated sensors. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Safety is the foremost priority in power plant operation. Instrumentation provides continuous, real-time monitoring of process variables and initiates automatic protective actions when parameters exceed safe limits. **Critical Safety Interlocks:** 1. **Boiler Drum Level Interlock (Low/Low-Low):** - **Sensor:** Differential Pressure (DP) cell measuring drum water level. - **Action:** If level falls below the Low-Low setpoint, a **Boiler Trip** is initiated, cutting off fuel firing to prevent coil overheating and tube rupture. 2. **Furnace Pressure Interlock (High/Low):** - **Sensor:** Differential pressure transmitter across the furnace. - **Action:** Abnormal positive pressure indicates furnace puff; abnormal negative pressure indicates implosion risk. Both trigger **MFT**. 3. **Flame Failure Interlock (FSSS):** - **Sensor:** UV / IR flame scanners viewing individual burners. - **Action:** Loss of flame for > 2 seconds → **Individual Burner Trip**. If multiple burners fail → **MFT** to prevent fuel accumulation and explosion. 4. **Turbine Trip Interlocks:** - **Sensors:** Speed probe, lube oil pressure, bearing temperature, vibration probes, condenser vacuum. - **Action:** Any of these exceeding limits → **Turbine Trip**, closing stop valves to prevent catastrophic overspeed damage. 5. **Air-Fuel Ratio Interlock:** - **Sensors:** Air flow transmitter and fuel flow transmitter. - **Action:** If air supply is lost, fuel must be cut off immediately to prevent incomplete combustion and explosion. **Conclusion:** Safety instrumentation follows the **IEC 61508 / SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** framework, with critical loops typically rated **SIL 2 or SIL 3**. --- **Q(b).** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a modern Distributed Control System (DCS)** as applied to a 500 MW thermal power plant. Explain the function of each level. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A DCS is a hierarchically distributed architecture that integrates measurement, control, and supervisory functions across the plant. **Block Diagram (describe in words for exam):** **Level 1 — Field Instruments:** - Sensors (RTD, pressure transmitters, flow meters) and final control elements (control valves, dampers, VFDs). - These interact directly with the physical process. **Level 2 — Control Stations / Marshalling:** - Receives 4–20 mA / HART / Foundation Fieldbus signals. - Houses I/O cards, signal isolators, intrinsic safety barriers. - Performs initial filtering and linearization. **Level 3 — Controllers (Process Control):** - Redundant controllers (e.g., ABB 800xA, Siemens PCS 7, Honeywell Experion) execute **PID loops**. - Functions: Combustion control, drum level control, turbine governor, ID/FD fan control. **Level 4 — Supervisory (HMI / SCADA):** - Operator workstations with graphical mimics. - Real-time trends, alarms, and event logging. - Engineers configure loops and download setpoints. **Level 5 — Management / Enterprise:** - Data historians (e.g., PI System) archive data. - MIS reports, performance KPIs, predictive analytics. - Integration with SAP/EAM for maintenance scheduling. **Importance in Power Generation:** - Enables **centralized control** of a 500 MW unit from a single control room. - Provides **redundancy** (1oo2, 2oo3 voting) for high availability (> 99.95%). - Supports **regulatory compliance** and **performance optimization** through historical data analysis. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **Q(a).** Discuss the **economic importance of accurate instrumentation** in a thermal power plant. Support your answer with a **numerical example** showing how a small measurement error in flue gas temperature leads to significant revenue loss. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Economic Importance:** - Even a **fractional improvement** in plant heat rate (PHR) translates to **crore-rupee savings** annually. - Accurate instrumentation directly impacts: 1. Fuel consumption 2. Auxiliary power consumption 3. Maintenance costs (predictive vs. preventive) 4. Penalties avoided (environmental non-compliance) 5. Plant availability (less unplanned downtime) **Numerical Example:** *Given:* 500 MW plant, $\dot{m}_f = 150$ kg/s, GCV = 4,000 kJ/kg, flue gas mass flow = 1,500 kg/s, $C_{pg}$ = 1.05 kJ/kg·K, ambient temp = 30 °C, actual flue gas temp = 145 °C, sensor reads 150 °C (error = +5 °C). Heat input: $Q_{in} = 150 \times 4{,}000 = 600{,}000$ kJ/s **Case 1 (Accurate):** $Q_{loss} = 1500 \times 1.05 \times 115 = 181{,}125$ kJ/s **Case 2 (Error):** $Q_{loss} = 1500 \times 1.05 \times 120 = 189{,}000$ kJ/s **Extra loss:** $\Delta Q = 7{,}875$ kJ/s **Annual loss (8,000 hrs):** $\Delta Q_{annual} = 7{,}875 \times 8{,}000 \times 3{,}600 = 2.268 \times 10^{11}$ kJ **Coal wasted:** $2.268 \times 10^{11} / 4{,}000 = 5.67 \times 10^{7}$ kg ≈ **56,700 tonnes/year** **Monetary loss (@ ₹ 5,000/tonne):** ₹ **28.35 crore/year** > **Conclusion:** A single sensor error of 5 °C causes a loss of over **₹ 28 crore annually** — far exceeding the cost of installing a high-accuracy sensor (typically < ₹ 5 lakh). --- **Q(b).** Write short notes on: **(i) CEMS**, **(ii) FSSS**, **(iii) Turbine Governing System, and (iv) Coal Flow Measurement. **(7 Marks — 1.75 Marks Each)** **Model Solution (Concise):** **(i) CEMS (Continuous Emission Monitoring System):** - Real-time stack gas analyzer. - Measures: SO₂ (NDIR), NOₓ (chemiluminescence), CO (NDIR), PM (opacity or TEOM), O₂ (paramagnetic), Hg (atomic absorption), flow. - Compliance: CPCB, MoEFCC norms; data tele-metered to regulatory servers. **(ii) FSSS (Flame Safeguard Supervisory System):** - Monitors furnace flame using UV/IR scanners. - Sequence: Purge → Ignite → Establish flame → Main fire. - Trip hierarchy: Burner trip → Corner trip → MFT. **(iii) Turbine Governing System:** - Controls turbine speed and load. - Sensors: Speed probe (magnetic pickup), LVDT on HP/IP valves. - Droop setting (~ 4–5%) and PID-based governor. - Synchronizes generator to grid (frequency matching). **(iv) Coal Flow Measurement:** - **Belt weighers** (load cells + speed sensor) — common for indirect-fired mills. - **Gravimetric feeders** (loss-in-weight principle) — high accuracy. - **Coriolis flow meters** — for pulverized coal pneumatic transport. - Critical for **heat input** calculation and efficiency. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):** > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing efficiency as "30" instead of "30%" — examiner deducts **0.5 mark**. > 2. **Confusing PHR with η:** PHR is in kcal/kWh; efficiency is dimensionless. Mixing them up is a common error. > 3. **Missing "Why" in Pillars:** Merely listing the 5 pillars without explaining *how instrumentation achieves each* fetches only **2 out of 7 marks** in 14-mark questions. > 4. **No Block Diagram:** For Q3-type questions, **a block diagram is compulsory** (carries 1–2 marks on its own). > 5. **Missing CEMS in Environmental Pillars:** CEMS is the *most frequently expected keyword* — omitting it loses marks. > 6. **Numerical Mistakes in Heat Loss Derivations:** Always carry units in **every step** to avoid arithmetic errors. > 7. **Not mentioning Standards:** Students often forget to mention **ISA, IEC 61508 (SIL), CEA, CPCB** — these keywords score bonus marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Importance of Instrumentation"** > - [ ] **5 Pillars:** **S**afety, **E**fficiency, **E**nvironment, **R**eliability, **E**conomics (**S-E-E-R-E**). > - [ ] **Critical Loops:** Drum Level, Furnace Pressure, Flame, Turbine Speed, O₂ in flue gas. > - [ ] **Acronyms to Memorize:** CEMS, FSSS, MFT, DCS, SCADA, SIL, BFP, ESP, FD/ID fan, APH, HRSG. > - [ ] **Sensors to Memorize:** RTD (Pt100), Thermocouple (K-type), Bourdon tube, DP cell, Orifice, Venturi, Zirconia O₂ probe, UV/IR scanner, Seismic vibration probe. > - [ ] **Numerical Master Formula:** $\eta = P_{out} / (\dot{m}_f \cdot GCV)$ and $PHR = 3600/\eta$. > - [ ] **Economic Impact:** 1 °C error in flue gas ≈ ₹ 5+ crore/year for a 500 MW unit. > - [ ] **Standards:** ISA, IEC 61508, IEC 61511, IEEE, CEA (India), CPCB (India), MoEFCC. > - [ ] **Modern Trends:** IIoT, Industry 4.0, Digital Twins, AI-based predictive maintenance. > - [ ] **Typical Exam Ask:** "Discuss the role of instrumentation in…" (14 marks) or "List importance" (3 marks). > - [ ] **Always draw a block diagram** in 7+ mark questions. > - [ ] **Mention at least 3 specific sensors/examples** in every descriptive answer. > - [ ] **Conclusion line** is mandatory — relate back to safety/economy. ---

topics.title: Fuel Circuit (Basics Only): Furnace, Combustion Process, Fuel Systems, Pressurized Ball Mill, Air Preheater, Air Supply for Mills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Circuit (Basics Only): Furnace, Combustion Process, Fuel Systems, Pressurized Ball Mill, Air Preheater, Air Supply for Mills

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel Circuit — The Heart of a Thermal Power Station

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **Fuel Circuit** in a thermal power plant is the integrated network of equipment and processes that handles the **receipt, storage, preparation (size reduction and drying), transportation, and controlled combustion** of fuel (primarily coal) to release chemical energy as heat for steam generation.

The fuel circuit is the upstream segment of the **Rankine cycle** where chemical potential energy of fossil fuel is converted into thermal energy of high-pressure, high-temperature flue gases and steam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope Note for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> As per the Module 2 syllabus, only the *basics* are expected. Students are NOT required to design furnaces or mills — they must understand **instrumentation-relevant physical processes**, control parameters, and equipment function.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen" of a Power Plant

Imagine your kitchen stove:
- The **burner (furnace)** is where cooking happens.
- The **gas/oxygen (air)** must be supplied in the right ratio.
- The **LPG cylinder and regulator (fuel system)** controls the fuel flow.
- The **grinder (pulverizer/ball mill)** reduces coal to fine powder (like a spice grinder).
- The **hot air blower (air preheater)** warms incoming air to help ignition, like pre-heating a tawa before pouring batter.

Just as a chef controls flame intensity, a power plant engineer must control:
- **Coal flow rate** (fuel)
- **Air flow rate** (oxygen)
- **Furnace draft** (pressure)

This is the essence of **combustion control** — one of the most important control loops in any thermal power plant.

---

## 1.3 Key Subsystems in the Fuel Circuit

| Subsystem | Primary Function | KTU-Relevant Highlights |
|---|---|---|
| **Furnace** | Encloses combustion zone | Heat transfer, flame stability |
| **Combustion Process** | Converts chemical → thermal energy | Stoichiometry, excess air |
| **Fuel System** | Stores, conveys, and feeds coal | Storage → Feeder → Burner |
| **Pressurized Ball Mill** | Pulverizes coal to ~75 μm | Pulverized fuel (PF) firing |
| **Air Preheater (APH)** | Recovers waste heat from flue gas | Preheats combustion air |
| **Air Supply for Mills** | Dries coal and transports PF | Primary air fan (PA fan) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Coal Fineness Metric:**
> **70% to 75% of pulverized coal** must pass through a **200 mesh screen (74 μm)** for efficient combustion in a modern pulverized-fuel (PF) boiler. The KTU Board regularly tests this fact.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Conditions

- **Excess Air (EA)**: typically **15% to 25%** for bituminous coal-fired PF boilers.
- **Furnace Exit Gas Temperature (FEGT)**: ideally **1000 °C to 1200 °C** (to prevent slagging yet ensure complete burnout).
- **Pulverizer Outlet Temperature**: **70 °C to 90 °C** (above dew point to prevent moisture condensation in coal pipes).
- **Coal-to-Air Ratio** is the **master controlled variable** in combustion control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Furnace — Geometry and Function

The **furnace** (also called the **combustion chamber** or **firebox**) is a large, water-wall enclosed chamber where:

1. Coal is ignited and burned in **suspension** (for PF boilers).
2. Radiant heat transfer occurs from flame to water-tubes lining the walls.
3. Flue gases cool down before entering the convective pass.

### 2.1.1 Furnace Design Principles
- **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** is a critical design parameter measured in **kW/m³** of furnace volume.
- Typical **HRR** for tangentially-fired PF boilers: **400 to 500 kW/m³**.
- Furnace aspect ratio (H/W): **2:1 to 3:1** to provide adequate residence time.
- Residence time: **2 to 4 seconds** for complete combustion of coal particles.

### 2.1.2 Types of Furnace (KTU relevant)
- **Dry-bottom furnace** — ash falls as dry bottom ash (modern).
- **Wet-bottom (slag-tap) furnace** — ash melts and tapped as molten slag (older).
- **Cyclone furnace** — small, horizontal, high-heat-release (for low-grade fuels).

---

## 2.2 Combustion Process — The Chemistry and Stoichiometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion** is a high-temperature **oxidation-reduction (redox) reaction** where carbon and hydrocarbons in fuel react with oxygen in air, releasing heat.

### 2.2.1 Stoichiometric Combustion of Carbon

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + \text{Heat} \quad (\Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

### 2.2.2 Stoichiometric Combustion of Hydrogen

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Heat} \quad (\Delta H = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

### 2.2.3 Stoichiometric Combustion of Sulphur

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + \text{Heat} \quad (\Delta H = -296.8 \text{ kJ/mol})$$

### 2.2.4 Concept of Excess Air

In practice, **100% theoretical air is insufficient** because:
- Mixing of fuel and air is never perfect.
- Some fuel particles escape unburned.
- Combustion temperature must be controlled.

Hence, **excess air** is supplied. The **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** is the single most important control variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excess Air Definition:**
> **Excess Air (%)** = $\frac{\text{Actual Air Supplied} - \text{Stoichiometric Air Required}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air Required}} \times 100$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet for Fuel Circuit — All Key Equations**
> Use `\vert` for absolute values to maintain table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ for Carbon | $O_{2,C} = \tfrac{32}{12} \cdot C = 2.667\,C$ | kg O₂/kg C | C = mass of carbon in fuel |
| 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ for Hydrogen | $O_{2,H} = \tfrac{32}{4} \cdot H = 8.0\,H$ | kg O₂/kg H | H = mass fraction of H₂ |
| 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ for Sulphur | $O_{2,S} = \tfrac{32}{32} \cdot S = 1.0\,S$ | kg O₂/kg S | S = mass fraction of S |
| 4 | Theoretical Air Required | $A_{th} = \frac{O_2^{stoch}}{0.232}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Air has 23.2% O₂ by mass |
| 5 | Actual Air Supplied | $A_{actual} = A_{th} \cdot (1 + EA)$ | kg air/kg fuel | EA = fractional excess air |
| 6 | Excess Air (%) | $EA(\%) = \frac{A_{actual} - A_{th}}{A_{th}} \times 100$ | % | KTU-favorite topic |
| 7 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis) | $AFR = \frac{A_{actual}}{F}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Master control variable |
| 8 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb} = \frac{\text{Heat released}}{\text{GCV of fuel}} \times 100$ | % | Affected by CO, unburnt C |
| 9 | Heat Release Rate (HRR) | $HRR = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot GCV}{V_{furnace}}$ | kW/m³ | Furnace design metric |
| 10 | Coal Fineness | $F = \frac{\text{Mass passing 200 mesh}}{\text{Total mass}} \times 100$ | % | Must be ≥ 70% |
| 11 | Pulverizer Outlet Temp | $T_{out} = 70 \text{ to } 90\,°C$ | °C | Above acid dew point |
| 12 | FEGT (Furnace Exit Gas Temp) | $T_{FEGT} = 1000 \text{ to } 1200\,°C$ | °C | Avoid slagging |
| 13 | Stoichiometric Air for CH₄ | $A_{th,CH_4} = 17.2$ | kg air/kg CH₄ | Standard textbook value |
| 14 | Stoichiometric Air for C₈H₁₈ | $A_{th,C_8H_{18}} = 15.13$ | kg air/kg octane | For gas turbine analogy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combustion Triplet (KTU Board favorite):**
> For complete combustion, the three **T's** must be satisfied: **Time, Temperature, and Turbulence**. The fourth T — **Thorough mixing (Turbulence)** — is controlled by burner design and excess air.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Subsystem | Industrial Significance |
|---|---|
| Furnace | Determines boiler capacity, ash deposition, slagging risk |
| Combustion Control | Direct impact on **boiler efficiency** (every 1% loss in combustion = ~0.5% plant heat rate penalty) |
| Pulverizer | Fineness dictates **burnout**; 1% unburnt carbon = ~0.2% efficiency loss |
| APH | Each **20 °C** rise in combustion air temperature saves **~1% fuel** |
| PA Fan | Sized for entire coal-air mixture through mills; critical path equipment |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for a Given Coal

### Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Model Style)

A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
- Carbon (C) = **70%**
- Hydrogen (H) = **5%**
- Oxygen (O) = **8%**
- Sulphur (S) = **1%**
- Nitrogen (N) = **1.5%**
- Moisture (M) = **3%**
- Ash (A) = **11.5%**

Calculate:
1. **Theoretical O₂ required** per kg of coal.
2. **Theoretical air required** per kg of coal.
3. **Actual air supplied** if excess air is **25%**.
4. **Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis)**.
5. **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (by volume and mass)**.

---

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Theoretical O₂ Requirement

Per kg of coal, mass fractions are:
- C = 0.70 kg, H = 0.05 kg, S = 0.01 kg
- O = 0.08 kg (already present in fuel, must be deducted from required O₂)

Using the standard reactions and molar masses:

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for C} = 2.667 \times C = 2.667 \times 0.70 = 1.8669 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for H} = 8.0 \times H = 8.0 \times 0.05 = 0.4000 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for S} = 1.0 \times S = 1.0 \times 0.01 = 0.0100 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Total O}_2 \text{ required (gross)} = 1.8669 + 0.4000 + 0.0100 = 2.2769 \text{ kg}$$

Deduct oxygen already present in fuel:

$$\text{Net O}_2 \text{ required} = 2.2769 - 0.08 = 2.1969 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Theoretical Air Required

Since air contains **23.2% O₂ by mass**:

$$A_{th} = \frac{\text{Net O}_2 \text{ required}}{0.232} = \frac{2.1969}{0.232} = 9.469 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Actual Air Supplied (EA = 25%)

$$A_{actual} = A_{th} \cdot (1 + EA) = 9.469 \times (1 + 0.25) = 9.469 \times 1.25$$

$$A_{actual} = 11.836 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)

$$AFR = \frac{A_{actual}}{F} = \frac{11.836}{1.0} = 11.836 : 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical AFR for bituminous coal with 25% excess air. For gasoline engines, AFR ≈ 14.7:1 stoichiometric; for diesel ≈ 14.5:1. The power plant AFR is **lower (richer mixture)** because some fuel-bound O₂ reduces net demand.

---

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Stoichiometric AFR Verification

$$\text{AFR}_{stoich,mass} = \frac{9.469}{1.0} = 9.469$$

In volume terms (using air molar volume at NTP = 22.4 m³/kmol, average air MW = 28.97):

$$V_{air} = \frac{9.469}{1.293} = 7.32 \text{ Nm}^3 / \text{kg fuel}$$

Where **1.293 kg/m³** is the density of air at NTP. This is the **volumetric stoichiometric AFR**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Release Rate of a Furnace

A 500 MW thermal plant boiler has a furnace volume of **4500 m³**. The coal GCV is **22 MJ/kg**, and the coal feed rate is **165 kg/s**.

**Calculate HRR:**

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times GCV = 165 \times 22,000 = 3,630,000 \text{ kW}$$

$$HRR = \frac{\dot{Q}_{fuel}}{V_{furnace}} = \frac{3,630,000}{4500} = 806.67 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Check with KTU Standard:**
> HRR for a 500 MW unit should be **400 to 500 kW/m³** to avoid slagging. The value 806.67 kW/m³ indicates a **compact, high-heat-release design** (e.g., cyclone or supercritical) — well above the typical dry-bottom tangentially-fired range. This kind of cross-check is what examiners love.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Critical Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect of Increase |
|---|---|---|---|
| Excess Air | EA | 15–25% | ↑ Heat losses via flue gas |
| Coal Fineness | F₂₀₀ | 70–75% | ↑ Burnout, ↓ unburnt carbon |
| Furnace Draft | ΔP_furnace | −25 to −50 mmH₂O | ↑ Combustion intensity |
| APH Outlet Air Temp | T_APH | 250–350 °C | ↑ Efficiency, ↓ fuel use |
| PA Fan Discharge | P_PA | 300–500 mmH₂O | ↑ Pulverizer throughput |
| FEGT | T_FEGT | 1000–1200 °C | Above ash fusion → slagging |
| Combustion Efficiency | η_comb | 98–99.5% | ↑ if EA optimized |

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem: APH Heat Recovery Calculation

An air preheater heats combustion air from **40 °C** to **300 °C** using flue gas cooling from **380 °C** to **160 °C**. Flue gas mass flow = **250 kg/s**, $C_{p,g} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K. Air mass flow = **220 kg/s**, $C_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

**Heat lost by flue gas:**

$$Q_g = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out}) = 250 \times 1.05 \times (380 - 160)$$

$$Q_g = 250 \times 1.05 \times 220 = 57{,}750 \text{ kW}$$

**Heat gained by air:**

$$Q_a = \dot{m}_a \cdot C_{p,a} \cdot (T_{a,out} - T_{a,in}) = 220 \times 1.005 \times (300 - 40)$$

$$Q_a = 220 \times 1.005 \times 260 = 57{,}486 \text{ kW}$$

**APH Effectiveness (ε):**

$$\varepsilon_{APH} = \frac{Q_a}{Q_{g,max}} = \frac{57{,}486}{57{,}750} = 0.9954 = 99.54\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **>99% effectiveness** is characteristic of **regenerative (Ljungström) APHs**. Recuperative (tubular) APHs typically achieve only **50–70%** effectiveness. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
