# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Fuel Circuit in a PF Boiler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Stockpile / Bunker] --> B[Belt Conveyor / Gravimetric Feeder]
    B --> C[Pulverizer Pressurized Ball Mill]
    D[PA Fan Hot Primary Air] --> C
    C --> E[Coal Air Mixture to Burners]
    E --> F[Furnace Combustion Chamber]
    G[FD Fan Secondary Air] --> F
    H[APH Air Preheater] --> G
    F --> I[Boiler Bank Convective Pass]
    I --> J[APH Cold Side]
    J --> K[ESP Particulate Removal]
    K --> L[ID Fan Induced Draft]
    L --> M[Stack]

    subgraph AIR_PATH[Combustion Air Path]
        H
        G
    end

    subgraph FUEL_PATH[Coal Path]
        A
        B
        C
        E
    end

    subgraph FLUE_GAS[Flue Gas Path]
        F
        I
        J
        K
        L
        M
    end
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic: Pressurized Ball Mill (Vertical Spindle Mill)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MILL[Pressurized Ball Mill Vertical Spindle]
        A1[Hot Primary Air Inlet] --> B1[Rotating Classifier Zone]
        B1 --> C1[Grinding Zone with Steel Balls]
        C1 --> D1[Pulverized Coal Air Outlet to Burners]
        E1[Raw Coal Feeder] --> C1
        C1 --> F1[Reject Oversize Particles via Pyrites Loop]
        F1 --> B1
    end

    G1[Coal from Bunker] --> E1
    D1 --> H1[Coal Pipes to 4 to 6 Burners]
    H1 --> I1[Furnace]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Functional Points (KTU Board emphasis):**
> 1. **Pressurized** means the mill operates at **positive pressure** relative to furnace.
> 2. Hot primary air serves **three functions**: drying, classifying, and transporting.
> 3. The rotating classifier returns oversize particles to the grinding zone — a **closed-circuit** grinding operation.

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Regenerative Air Preheater (Ljungström Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APH_BODY[Rotating Cylinder of Heat Storage Matrix]
        A2[Hot Flue Gas Sector Upper Half] --> B2[Matrix Plates Absorb Heat]
        B2 --> C2[Cold Combustion Air Sector Lower Half]
        C2 --> D2[Matrix Plates Release Heat to Air]
        D2 --> B2
    end

    E2[Flue Gas In 380C] --> A2
    A2 --> F2[Flue Gas Out 160C to ESP]
    G2[Cold Air In 40C] --> C2
    C2 --> H2[Hot Air Out 300C to PA Fan / FDF]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Regenerative?**
> The same matrix surface is alternately heated by flue gas and then gives up heat to combustion air. This is more compact and higher-effectiveness than a tubular (recuperative) APH. However, it suffers from **air-side leakage** (typically 8–10%) which is a major source of efficiency loss.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Combustion Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Boiler Steam Pressure / Steam Flow] --> B1[Master Controller]
    B1 --> C1[Fuel Flow Demand Signal]
    B1 --> D1[Air Flow Demand Signal]
    C1 --> E1[Coal Feeder Speed Control]
    E1 --> F1[Furnace Fuel Input]
    D1 --> G1[FD Fan Damper / PA Fan Damper]
    G1 --> H1[Furnace Air Input]
    F1 --> I1[Combustion Process]
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> A1
    I1 --> J1[Flue Gas O2 Analyzer]
    J1 --> K1[Trim Corrector for Excess Air]
    K1 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:**
> This is a classic **parallel metering** combustion control strategy. The **master demand signal** from boiler load is sent to both fuel and air simultaneously, and an **O₂ trim** corrects the air-to-fuel ratio for optimum excess air. The cross-coupling (cross-limit) prevents **fuel-rich puff** explosions on rapid load changes.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Air Supply for Mills

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PA Fan] --> B[Hot Air Damper]
    B --> C[Air Heater Cold APH Out]
    C --> D[Distribution Plenum]
    D --> E1[Mill 1]
    D --> E2[Mill 2]
    D --> E3[Mill 3]
    D --> E4[Mill 4]
    E1 --> F1[Classifier Outlet to Burner 1]
    E2 --> F2[Classifier Outlet to Burner 2]
    E3 --> F3[Classifier Outlet to Burner 3]
    E4 --> F4[Classifier Outlet to Burner 4]
    G[Tempering Air from APH Cold Inlet] --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tempering Air:**
> A small amount of **cold APH inlet air** is mixed with the hot primary air to **control the mill outlet temperature** to **70–90 °C**. This prevents coal moisture recondensation and pre-ignition in the mill. This is a high-yield KTU instrumentation point.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Define excess air in combustion. Why is it necessary and what happens if it is too high or too low?**

### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Excess air (EA)** is the amount of air supplied beyond the stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage of theoretical air. Mathematically:

$$EA(\%) = \frac{A_{actual} - A_{th}}{A_{th}} \times 100$$

- **Necessity**: Practical mixing is imperfect; excess air ensures complete combustion, prevents smoke, CO formation, and flame instability.
- **Excessively high EA**: Increases flue gas losses (sensible heat wasted), reduces boiler efficiency, lowers flame temperature.
- **Excessively low EA**: Incomplete combustion, smoke, soot, unburnt carbon in ash, CO formation, possible **furnace explosion** (puff).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**List any three functions of primary air in a pressurized ball mill.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):

The three primary functions of **primary air** in a pulverizer circuit are:
1. **Drying** — removes surface and inherent moisture from coal by carrying it away as vapor.
2. **Transportation (pneumatic conveying)** — carries pulverized coal from the mill classifier outlet to the burner nozzles.
3. **Classifying assistance** — provides the necessary flow for separating fine particles from oversize at the rotating classifier, returning coarse particles for re-grinding.

> Additional accepted point: **Preheating** the coal-air mixture to assist ignition at the burner.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions under the 2024 scheme feature **internal choice**. Below are two complete, independent alternative question sets.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete fuel circuit of a pulverized-fuel (PF) fired thermal power plant**. Label all major equipment from coal receipt to flue gas exit. (7 marks)

**(b)** A coal has the following ultimate analysis: C = 68%, H = 4.5%, O = 7%, S = 0.8%, N = 1.2%, Moisture = 5%, Ash = 13.5%. Calculate: (i) Theoretical O₂ required, (ii) Theoretical air required, (iii) Actual air supplied at 30% excess air, (iv) Air-fuel ratio by mass. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description:**

The PF boiler fuel circuit consists of three integrated paths:

1. **Fuel Path (Coal)**:
   - Coal stockyard → crushers → bunkers → gravimetric feeders → pulverizers (ball mills) → coal-air mixture → burners → furnace.

2. **Air Path (Combustion + Drying)**:
   - Cold air from FD fan → APH (heats to ~300 °C) → splits into:
     - **Secondary air** → directly to burner windbox → furnace.
     - **Primary air** → through mills (with coal) → pulverized coal transport → burners → furnace.

3. **Flue Gas Path**:
   - Furnace → boiler bank / superheater / economizer → APH (cold side) → ESP → ID fan → stack.

> **Marking allocation**: 1 mark for fuel path, 1 mark for air path, 1 mark for flue gas path, 2 marks for block diagram, 1 mark for instrumentation sensors, 1 mark for key temperatures.

**Key Instrumentation Mentioned:**
- Furnace draft transmitter (±25 mmH₂O).
- O₂ analyzer in flue gas (1.5–3% range).
- Mill outlet temperature thermocouple (70–90 °C).
- Coal feeder load cell / speed pickup.

---

### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**: C = 0.68, H = 0.045, O = 0.07, S = 0.008 (per kg of coal)

**Step 1 — Gross O₂ required:**

$$O_{2,C} = 2.667 \times 0.68 = 1.8136 \text{ kg}$$

$$O_{2,H} = 8.0 \times 0.045 = 0.3600 \text{ kg}$$

$$O_{2,S} = 1.0 \times 0.008 = 0.0080 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Gross O}_2 = 1.8136 + 0.3600 + 0.0080 = 2.1816 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Net O₂ (deduct fuel O₂):**

$$O_{2,net} = 2.1816 - 0.07 = 2.1116 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical air required:**

$$A_{th} = \frac{2.1116}{0.232} = 9.1017 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Actual air (EA = 30%):**

$$A_{actual} = 9.1017 \times 1.30 = 11.832 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis):**

$$AFR = \frac{11.832}{1.0} = 11.832 : 1$$

**Final Answer:** Theoretical air = **9.10 kg/kg**, Actual air = **11.83 kg/kg**, AFR = **11.83:1**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a pressurized ball mill** with a labeled diagram. What is the role of the rotating classifier? (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **construction and working of a regenerative air preheater (Ljungström type)**. Mention any two advantages over the recuperative type. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

**Working Principle of Pressurized Ball Mill:**

The pressurized ball mill is a **medium-speed (50–100 rpm) vertical spindle pulverizer** that grinds coal to a fineness of **70–75% through 200 mesh** (74 μm).

**Components and Function:**
1. **Grinding ring & balls (lower chamber)**: Coal falls between the rotating ring table and a set of steel balls (10–15 balls of dia 200–500 mm). Centrifugal force and spring/hydraulic load press the balls against the ring. Raw coal is crushed by impact and attrition.
2. **Rotating classifier (upper chamber)**: A **centrifugal classifier with adjustable blades** allows only fine particles to escape with the primary air. Coarse particles are returned to the grinding zone.
3. **Hot primary air inlet**: Enters at the bottom, sweeps up the pulverized coal, and **dries + transports** the coal-air mixture to burners.
4. **Pressurization**: The mill operates above atmospheric pressure (positive housing pressure) so that hot primary air can be pushed through it. This eliminates the need for an exhauster.

**Role of Rotating Classifier** (mandatory mention for 2 marks):
- **Particle size control**: Adjustable blade angle sets the cut-point size.
- **Circulating load**: Oversize particles are returned to the grinding zone → improves fineness.
- **Efficiency**: Reduces over-grinding and unnecessary power consumption.
- **Prevents** large particles from entering burners (avoids unburnt carbon losses).

---

### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

**Construction of Ljungström Regenerative APH:**

The Ljungström APH is a **slowly rotating cylindrical rotor** (1–2 rpm) packed with **corrugated metal (or ceramic) heat-storage matrix plates**.

**Working:**
- The rotor is divided into two sectors by **radial sealing plates**:
  - **Upper half (gas sector)**: Hot flue gas (380–420 °C from economizer) passes **downward** through the matrix, heating the metal plates to ~300 °C.
  - **Lower half (air sector)**: Cold combustion air (40 °C from FD fan inlet) passes **upward** through the matrix, absorbing heat from plates → exits at **280–320 °C** to PA fan / FD fan duct.
- The continuous rotation ensures uniform heat transfer and self-cleaning.

**Two Advantages over Recuperative Type:**
1. **Higher effectiveness (90–99%)** vs. 50–70% for recuperative — due to intimate contact of gas/air with hot matrix.
2. **Compact and economical** for large gas flows — lower cost per unit heat transfer area.
3. **Self-cleaning by rotation** — ash falls off the plates by gravity into ash hoppers (continuous soot-blowing not required between air/gas changes).

**Disadvantages** (for balance):
- Air-side leakage (8–10%) reduces efficiency.
- Higher maintenance for seals, bearings, and motor drive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistakes (Examiner's Pitfall):**
> 1. **Confusing "Pressurized" mill with "Pressurized Furnace"** — they are different. A pressurized mill means positive housing pressure, not a pressurized furnace.
> 2. **Forgetting tempering air** in the mill circuit — many students draw only hot air going to mills, missing the cold-air bypass.
> 3. **Mixing up APH types** — students often write "tubular APH" but describe a Ljungström rotor, or vice versa. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this confusion.
> 4. **Missing the O₂ trim loop** in combustion control — the cross-limit / O₂ trim is **essential** for a complete answer.
> 5. **Forgetting to deduct fuel-bound oxygen** in stoichiometric O₂ calculation — a 1-mark penalty in numerical questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (High-Density Summary)**

### Furnace
- Encloses combustion; heat release rate **400–500 kW/m³** (typical PF).
- Furnace exit gas temperature (FEGT): **1000–1200 °C**.
- Residence time: **2–4 seconds** for coal burnout.
- Water walls line the furnace for radiant heat absorption.

### Combustion Process
- Three T's: **Time, Temperature, Turbulence**.
- Excess air formula: $EA = \frac{A_{actual} - A_{th}}{A_{th}} \times 100$.
- Optimal EA for PF boilers: **15–25%** (bituminous).
- Stoichiometric O₂ coefficients: **2.667 (C), 8.0 (H), 1.0 (S)**.
- Theoretical air = $\frac{O_{2,net}}{0.232}$ (air has 23.2% O₂ by mass).

### Fuel Systems
- Coal path: **Yard → Crusher → Bunker → Feeder → Mill → Burner**.
- Feeder types: **Gravimetric** (mass-based, accurate) vs. **Volumetric** (volume-based).
- Burner types: **Circular, Cell, Tangential, Wall-fired**.

### Pressurized Ball Mill
- **Medium speed (50–100 rpm)**, vertical spindle.
- Three functions of primary air: **Drying, Transporting, Classifying**.
- Outlet temperature: **70–90 °C** (above acid dew point).
- Fineness target: **≥70% through 200 mesh (74 μm)**.
- Pressurization eliminates the need for an exhauster fan.

### Air Preheater (APH)
- **Regenerative (Ljungström)**: rotating matrix, **90–99% effectiveness**, compact, but air leakage ~8–10%.
- **Recuperative (Tubular)**: stationary, gas-air separated by tubes, **50–70% effectiveness**, no leakage.
- Typical air heating: **40 °C → 280–320 °C**.
- Located **downstream of economizer**, upstream of ESP/ID fan.

### Air Supply for Mills
- **PA Fan (Primary Air Fan)** supplies hot air to all pulverizers.
- **Tempering air** is a cold bypass to control mill outlet temperature.
- PA fan is the **critical path** equipment — its failure trips the boiler.

### Instrumentation Touch-Points
- **O₂ analyzer** (in flue gas duct) — for excess air trim.
- **Coal feeder speed pickup** — for fuel flow feedback.
- **Mill outlet thermocouple** — for drying control.
- **Furnace draft transmitter** — for FD/ID fan balancing.
- **APH air/gas temperature** — for APH performance monitoring.
- **CO analyzer** (optional, modern) — for combustion efficiency feedback.

### Common Numerical Values to Memorize
- Air composition: **O₂ = 23.2% by mass, 21% by volume**.
- Coal fineness: **70–75% through 200 mesh**.
- Excess air: **15–25%** PF boilers, **5–10%** for oil, **5–15%** for gas.
- $C_{p,air}$ ≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**; $C_{p,flue gas}$ ≈ **1.05 kJ/kg·K**.
- Air density at NTP: **1.293 kg/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treatment of Flue Gases, Soot Blowers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Treatment of Flue Gases, Soot Blowers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flue Gas — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flue Gas:** The exhaust gas produced at the outlet of combustion equipment (coal-fired boiler, furnace, or turbine) that is discharged through a chimney or flue. It is a complex gaseous mixture containing combustion products ($CO_2$, $H_2O$, $SO_2$, $SO_3$, $NO_x$), excess $O_2$, $N_2$, and particulate matter (fly ash, soot, unburnt carbon).

In a typical coal-fired thermal power station, the flue gas leaving the economizer/air-preheater (economizer exit) is at a temperature of **120 °C – 180 °C** and carries **15–30 g/Nm³** of particulate matter along with **SO₂ (800–2000 mg/Nm³)** and **NOₓ (300–600 mg/Nm³)**.

## 1.2 Why Treat Flue Gas?

> [!NOTE]
> **Environmental Compliance Drivers (Kerala PCB & MoEFCC Norms):**
> - Particulate Matter (PM) limit: **≤ 30 mg/Nm³** for new units.
> - $SO_2$ emission limit: **≤ 100 mg/Nm³** (within 500 m of sensitive zones).
> - $NO_x$ emission limit: **≤ 300 mg/Nm³** for new tangentially-fired units.
> - Mercury (Hg), and water consumption norms are also governed under the **Environmental (Protection) Amendment Rules, 2015**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Kitchen Exhaust Chimney of a Restaurant":**
> Imagine a massive industrial kitchen where coal is being burnt 24×7. The smoke rising out is black (soot), smelly ($SO_2$), and acidic (acid mist). Before releasing this smoke into the neighbourhood, the restaurant owner must:
> 1. **Filter the soot** (Electrostatic Precipitator – ESP).
> 2. **Neutralize the acid smell** (Flue Gas Desulfurization – FGD).
> 3. **Lower toxic nitrogen fumes** (DeNOₓ systems – SCR/SNCR).
>
> This three-stage "wash and clean" of smoke is called **Flue Gas Treatment (FGT)**.

## 1.4 Soot Blowers — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soot Blower:** A mechanical/ pneumatic device installed in a boiler to remove soot and ash deposits from heat-transfer surfaces (water walls, superheater, reheater, economizer, and air-preheater) by projecting a high-velocity jet of steam, air, or water onto these surfaces.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Soot Blowers

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Pressure Washer of a Boiler":**
> Just as you use a high-pressure water jet to clean algae off your compound wall, soot blowers use high-pressure steam/air jets (typically **10 bar – 40 bar**) to blast off ash and soot clinging to boiler tubes. Without periodic cleaning, the boiler's efficiency drops dramatically.

## 1.6 Standard Metrics to Remember

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Flue gas velocity in ESP duct | **1.0 – 1.5 m/s** |
| ESP collection efficiency | **≥ 99.9 %** |
| FGD reagent (Limestone) stoichiometric ratio | **1.0 – 1.1 mol CaCO₃/mol SO₂** |
| Soot blower steam pressure | **10 – 40 bar** |
| Soot blower steam temperature | **300 – 350 °C** |
| Typical SO₂ removal efficiency (Wet Limestone FGD) | **≥ 95 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flue Gas Treatment — Process Hierarchy

The treatment is performed in a strict sequence based on temperature and chemistry:

1. **Particulate Removal (First Stage)** — at ~**150 °C**
   - Mechanical: Cyclone Separators (pre-cleaner, ≤ 10 µm).
   - Electrostatic: **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** — high-efficiency.
   - Fabric: Bag Filters (Baghouses) — alternative to ESP.

2. **Sulphur Removal (Second Stage)** — at **120 °C – 160 °C** (Wet FGD) or ~**400 °C** (Spray-Dry / SDA).
   - **Wet Limestone Gypsum Process (most common).**
   - Dry / Semi-Dry FGD.
   - Wet Ammonia Process.

3. **Nitrogen Oxide Removal (Third Stage)** — at **300 °C – 400 °C** (SCR).
   - **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR).**
   - Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR).

4. **Mercury / Trace Pollutant Removal** — activated carbon injection.

## 2.2 Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) — Deep Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle:** ESP operates on the principle of **Coulomb's electrostatic attraction**. The flue gas passes through a high-voltage DC corona discharge (typically **–30 kV to –70 kV** between discharge electrodes and grounded collection plates). Particles acquire a negative charge and migrate to the positively charged collection plates, where they are periodically dislodged by **rapping**.

### 2.2.1 ESP Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)
- **Step 1 — Charging:** A high-voltage negative discharge electrode (tungsten wire) ionizes the surrounding gas, producing a corona of free electrons.
- **Step 2 — Particle Charging:** Particulates flowing through this corona region attach electrons, acquiring negative charge.
- **Step 3 — Migration (Drift):** Negatively charged particles drift toward the grounded (positive) collection plates under the influence of the electric field $E$.
- **Step 4 — Collection:** Particles deposit on the plates forming a dust layer.
- **Step 5 — Dislodgement (Rapping):** A motorized hammer strikes the plate, loosening the dust into the hopper below.
- **Step 6 — Disposal:** Collected fly ash is pneumatically conveyed to storage silos.

### 2.2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — ESP

| Symbol | Formula / Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Drift velocity ($w$) | $w = \dfrac{q \cdot E \cdot C}{3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d_p}$ (Stokes region) | m/s |
| Deutsch–Anderson Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - e^{(-w \cdot A / Q)}$ | — (dimensionless) |
| Corona Current | $I = k \cdot V \cdot (V - V_0)$ | A |
| Specific Collection Area (SCA) | $\text{SCA} = A / Q$ | s/m |
| Migration velocity (typical) | $w = 0.05 - 0.20$ m/s (industrial design) | m/s |
| ESP voltage | $V = 30$–$70$ kV DC | kV |
| Migration velocity equation (field) | $w = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot E^2 \cdot d_p}{3 \mu}$ | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "QWEA"** for Deutsch–Anderson: $Q$ (volumetric flow) × $W$ (drift) = $E$ (efficiency). Larger collection area ($A$) and lower flow ($Q$) → higher efficiency.

## 2.3 Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD) — Wet Limestone Gypsum

### 2.3.1 Core Chemistry

$$SO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow H_2SO_4 \ \ \text{(in absorber slurry)}$$

$$CaCO_3 + H_2SO_4 \longrightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + CO_2 \uparrow$$

> Overall: $SO_2 + CaCO_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + CO_2$

### 2.3.2 Operational Logic
- **Step 1 — Quench:** Hot flue gas enters the absorber (Scrubber Tower) and is saturated with water spray.
- **Step 2 — Absorption:** $SO_2$ dissolves in the limestone slurry ($CaCO_3$ + water).
- **Step 3 — Oxidation:** Air is injected to oxidize sulfite ($SO_3^{2-}$) to sulfate ($SO_4^{2-}$).
- **Step 4 — Crystallization:** Gypsum ($CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$) crystallizes and is removed via a centrifuge.
- **Step 5 — Stacking:** Cleaned flue gas exits the stack at **~50–80 °C** (moisture-saturated).

### 2.3.3 KTU Formula Sheet — FGD

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Stoichiometric Ratio (SR) | $SR = \dfrac{\text{mol CaCO}_3 \text{ actually used}}{\text{mol SO}_2 \text{ removed}}$ |
| Desulfurization Efficiency | $\eta_{SO_2} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 \ \%$ |
| Lime consumption | $m_{CaCO_3} = \dfrac{M_{CaCO_3}}{M_{SO_2}} \times \eta \times \dot{n}_{SO_2}$ |
| Gypsum yield | $m_{gypsum} = \dfrac{M_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O}}{M_{SO_2}} \times \dot{n}_{SO_2, removed}$ |
| L/G Ratio (Liquid to Gas) | $L/G = 8 - 15 \ L/\text{Nm}^3$ |
| Slurry pH | $5.0 - 6.0$ (absorber) |
| Absorption tower type | Spray tower / packed tower / tray tower |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Wet limestone FGD is the **industry workhorse** (used in ~85 % of all desulfurization installations globally) because it produces **commercial-grade gypsum** (used in cement and plaster of Paris), turning pollution into a sellable byproduct.

## 2.4 DeNOₓ Systems — SCR and SNCR

### 2.4.1 Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)

$$4 NO + 4 NH_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{TiO_2/WO_3 \text{ catalyst},\, 300-400°C} 4 N_2 + 6 H_2O$$

$$NO + NO_2 + 2 NH_3 \xrightarrow{\text{cat.}} 2 N_2 + 3 H_2O$$

**Operating temperature window:** **300 °C – 400 °C** (for $TiO_2$-based catalysts).
**NOₓ reduction efficiency:** **80 – 95 %**.

### 2.4.2 Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR)

$$4 NO + 4 NH_3 + O_2 \xrightarrow{850-1100°C} 4 N_2 + 6 H_2O$$

Lower capital cost but lower efficiency (**30 – 70 %**).

### 2.4.3 KTU Formula Sheet — DeNOₓ

| Parameter | SCR | SNCR |
|---|---|---|
| Operating temp | **300 – 400 °C** | **850 – 1100 °C** |
| Efficiency | **80 – 95 %** | **30 – 70 %** |
| NH₃ slip (typical) | $\le 5 \ ppm$ | $\le 10 \ ppm$ |
| Reagent | $NH_3$ or urea $(NH_2)_2CO$ | Urea or $NH_3$ |
| Molar ratio ($NH_3$/NOₓ) | 0.8 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 2.0 |

## 2.5 Soot Blowers — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.5.1 Need for Soot Blowing

When coal is burnt, ash and soot deposit on heat-transfer surfaces:
- **Convective pass** (superheater, reheater, economizer).
- **Air preheater** (APH).
- **Water walls** (radiant zone).

This causes:
- **Reduction in heat-transfer coefficient** (fouling factor increases).
- **Boiler efficiency loss** of 1 – 5 %.
- **Higher tube-metal temperature** → risk of tube failure.
- **Increased draft loss** across APH.

### 2.5.2 Classification of Soot Blowers

| Type | Cleaning Surface | Medium Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Long Retractable Soot Blower (LRSB)** | Superheater / Reheater | Steam (10–40 bar) |
| **Short Retractable Soot Blower (SRSB)** | Water walls | Steam (10–25 bar) |
| **Rotary Soot Blower (RSB)** | Economizer | Steam (10–20 bar) |
| **Wall Blower (Fixed)** | Water walls (slag) | Steam / Air |
| **Air-Preheater Soot Blower (APHSB)** | APH baskets | High-pressure water/air |
| **Sonic Soot Blower** | All zones | Acoustic waves (low-frequency) |

### 2.5.3 Working Principle — Long Retractable Soot Blower

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism:** A carriage-mounted lance is inserted (retracted) into the boiler while the boiler is online. The lance carries nozzles that project high-velocity steam jets onto the tube banks. As the lance travels, the nozzles sweep across each tube in the lane, blowing off accumulated ash.

### 2.5.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Soot Blower Steam Consumption

| Parameter | Formula / Value |
|---|---|
| Steam pressure at nozzle | **10 – 40 bar** |
| Steam mass per blow | $m_s = C_d \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ |
| Velocity of jet | $v_j = \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho_{steam}}$ |
| Cleaning radius (effective) | $r_{eff} = 1.5 - 3.0$ m |
| Blower travel speed | $v_b = 0.1 - 0.3 \ m/s$ |
| Cleaning frequency | **2 – 24 hours/cycle** (zone dependent) |
| Steam consumption (per blower) | **100 – 500 kg/blow** |
| Effective cleaning force | Impulse = $m_s \cdot v_j$ |

### 2.5.5 Sonic Soot Blower — Modern Concept

> [!NOTE]
> Uses a **Horn Resonator** or **Acoustic Wave Generator** producing low-frequency sound (**60–300 Hz**, intensity **120–140 dB**) that dislodges loosely-bound ash. Suitable for zones where retractable soot blowers cannot access.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of FGT + Soot Blowers

| System | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| ESP | Protects downstream FGD/SCR from particulates; recovers fly ash for cement industry. |
| FGD | Compliance with $SO_2$ norms; gypsum commercialization. |
| SCR/SNCR | Controls photochemical smog and acid rain. |
| Soot Blowers | Maintains boiler thermal efficiency; prevents forced outages; extends tube life. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deutsch–Anderson Efficiency Equation — Full Derivation

This is a **frequently asked 7–10 mark derivation** in KTU.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider an ESP with a gas flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) and a total collection plate area $A$ (m²). Particulates drift towards the plates at a velocity $w$ (m/s) — called the **drift velocity** or **migration velocity**.

Let $C$ be the dust concentration at distance $x$ into the precipitator.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

In an infinitesimal slice of the precipitator $dx$, the dust removed per unit time is:

$$d\dot{m} = w \cdot B \cdot C(x) \cdot dx$$

where $B$ is the plate height (m).

Total mass flow of dust entering the slice:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = Q \cdot C(x)$$

The fractional reduction in concentration:

$$\dfrac{dC}{C(x)} = -\dfrac{w \cdot B \cdot dx}{Q}$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = L$:

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_L} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -\dfrac{w \cdot B}{Q} \int_0^L dx$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_L}{C_0}\right) = -\dfrac{w \cdot B \cdot L}{Q}$$

Since total plate area $A = 2 \cdot B \cdot L$ (both sides of the duct):

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_L}{C_0}\right) = -\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}$$

Exponentiate and express as efficiency:

$$\eta = 1 - \dfrac{C_L}{C_0} = 1 - e^{(-w \cdot A / Q)}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - e^{(-w \cdot A / Q)}}$$

### 3.1.3 Result and Significance

- Larger $A$ (more plates) → higher efficiency.
- Lower $Q$ (slower gas velocity) → higher efficiency.
- Larger drift velocity $w$ → higher efficiency.
- Drift velocity $w$ depends on particle size, electric field, and gas viscosity.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — ESP Sizing

**Problem:**
An ESP handles flue gas with flow rate $Q = 500{,}000 \ \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$. Drift velocity is $w = 0.10 \ \text{m/s}$. Required collection efficiency is $\eta = 99.5 \ \%$.

Find the required total plate area.

**Solution:**

$$A = -\dfrac{Q}{w} \cdot \ln(1 - \eta)$$

Convert $Q$:

$$Q = \dfrac{500{,}000}{3600} = 138.89 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Substitute:

$$A = -\dfrac{138.89}{0.10} \cdot \ln(1 - 0.995)$$

$$A = -1388.9 \cdot \ln(0.005)$$

$$A = -1388.9 \cdot (-5.2983)$$

$$A = 7358.9 \ \text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 7360 \ \text{m}^2}$$

If each plate has area $50 \ \text{m}^2$, the number of plates = $7360 / 50 \approx 147$ plates.

## 3.3 FGD Mass Balance — Worked Numerical

**Problem:**
A 500 MW thermal power plant burns coal with sulphur content $S = 1.0 \ \%$ by mass. Coal consumption is $C_{coal} = 200 \ \text{tons/hr}$. FGD removes $95 \ \%$ of $SO_2$. Find the daily gypsum production.

**Solution:**

Mass of $S$ in coal per hour:

$$\dot{m}_S = 0.01 \times 200{,}000 = 2000 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

Mass of $SO_2$ produced per hour (assuming complete combustion of $S$):

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2} = \dot{m}_S \times \dfrac{M_{SO_2}}{M_S} = 2000 \times \dfrac{64}{32} = 4000 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

$SO_2$ removed per hour:

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2,\,removed} = 0.95 \times 4000 = 3800 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

Gypsum ($CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$, $M = 172 \ \text{g/mol}$) produced per hour:

$$\dot{m}_{gypsum} = 3800 \times \dfrac{172}{64} = 3800 \times 2.6875 = 10{,}212.5 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

Daily production:

$$\dot{m}_{day} = 10{,}212.5 \times 24 = 245{,}100 \ \text{kg/day} \approx 245 \ \text{tons/day}$$

$$\boxed{m_{gypsum,\,day} \approx 245 \ \text{tons/day}}$$

## 3.4 Soot Blower Jet Velocity — Derivation

### 3.4.1 Setup

Steam at pressure $P_1$ expands through a nozzle to flue gas pressure $P_2$. The mass flow through a nozzle is:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_1 (P_1 - P_2)}$$

Jet exit velocity (ideal expansion):

$$v_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_{steam}}}$$

### 3.4.2 Numerical Substitution

Take $P_1 = 30 \ \text{bar (abs)} = 30 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa}$
$P_2 = 1.013 \ \text{bar} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \ \text{Pa}$

Steam density at source $\rho_{steam} \approx 13.5 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ (saturated steam at 30 bar).

$$v_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (30 - 1.013) \times 10^5}{13.5}}$$

$$v_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 28.987 \times 10^5}{13.5}}$$

$$v_j = \sqrt{\dfrac{57.974 \times 10^5}{13.5}} = \sqrt{4.295 \times 10^6}$$

$$v_j \approx 2073 \ \text{m/s} \quad \text{(supersonic — typical of soot blower jets)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although choking limits the actual velocity to sonic (~580 m/s) at the nozzle throat, this simplified energy equation captures the magnitude of the kinetic energy that dislodges ash deposits.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Viva & Theory

| Feature | ESP | FGD (Wet) | SCR | Soot Blower |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Purpose | PM removal | $SO_2$ removal | $NO_x$ removal | Surface cleaning |
| Pollutant | Fly ash | $SO_2$ | NO, $NO_2$ | Soot / slag |
| Working temp | **150 °C** | **120–160 °C** | **300–400 °C** | **In-situ** |
| Reagent | Electricity | Limestone + water | $NH_3$ + Catalyst | Steam/Air |
| Efficiency | **99.9 %** | **95 %** | **80–95 %** | Local cleaning |
| Location | Post-AH, pre-FGD | Post-ESP | Pre-AH (high-dust) or post-FGD (tail-end) | Throughout boiler |
| Byproduct | Fly ash (cement) | Gypsum | — | Slag, soot |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Flue Gas Treatment Train — Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid

    J1 --> K1[FGD Wet Limestone Scrubber SO2 Removal]
    K1 --> L1[Reheater for cleaned flue gas]
    L1 --> M1[Stack with Online CEMS]
    M1 --> N1[Atmosphere]

    H1 --> H2[Fly Ash Hopper - to Cement Industry]

    K1 --> K3[Wastewater Treatment Plant]
```

```mermaid
    ESP4 --> ESP5[Corona Discharge Region - Particle Charging Zone]

    ESP8 --> ESP9[Hopper for Collected Fly Ash]
    ESP9 --> ESP10[Outlet Duct - Clean Flue Gas to ID Fan]

```

## 4.3 FGD Scrubber Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FGD1[Hot Flue Gas In 150C] --> FGD2[Quench Zone - Water Spray]
    FGD2 --> FGD3[Absorber Tower - Spray Nozzles]
    FGD3 --> FGD4[Limestone Slurry Recirculation Pump]
    FGD4 --> FGD5[Slurry Tank - pH 5 to 6]
    FGD5 --> FGD6[Oxidation Air Blower]
    FGD6 --> FGD7[Gypsum Crystallization Tank]
    FGD7 --> FGD8[Gypsum Centrifuge]

```

## 4.4 Long Retractable Soot Blower — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
    SB5 --> SB6[Lance Extension - Into Boiler Furnace]
    SB6 --> SB7[Steam Nozzles - Directed at Tube Bank]
    SB7 --> SB8[Superheater / Reheater Tubes - Target Surface]
    SB8 --> SB9[Soot / Ash Particles Dislodged]
    SB9 --> SB10[Falling Ash to Hopper Bottom]

    SB11[Drive Motor - Carriage Movement] --> SB6
    SB12[Limit Switches - Fully Retracted / Extended Position] --> SB11
    SB13[DCS / PLC Control Panel - Sequential Blowing] --> SB3

```
