# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP). State its working principle. Mention its typical collection efficiency for fly ash in modern coal-fired boilers.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** An Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) is a particulate-control device that removes suspended dust particles from flue gas using the principle of electrostatic force acting on electrically charged particles.

> **Working Principle:** Flue gas passes between high-voltage negative discharge electrodes (–30 to –70 kV DC) and grounded positive collection plates. Dust particles acquire negative charge from the corona discharge and migrate towards the collection plates under the influence of the electric field. The collected dust is dislodged by rapping and falls into the hopper.

> **Efficiency:** Modern ESPs achieve **99.9 %** collection efficiency for particulate matter, with outlet dust concentration typically **≤ 30 mg/Nm³**.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**List any three types of soot blowers used in thermal power plants. State one area of application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Long Retractable Soot Blower (LRSB)** — Used for cleaning **superheater and reheater** tube banks; uses steam at 10–40 bar.
2. **Rotary Soot Blower (RSB)** — Used for cleaning **economizer** tube banks; uses a rotating nozzle with steam.
3. **Air-Preheater (APH) Soot Blower** — Used for cleaning **APH basket tubes**; uses high-pressure water/air jets.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a) Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) used for flue gas treatment in thermal power plants. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An ESP consists of:

1. **Shell/Housing:** A rectangular steel casing divided into multiple fields (typically 3–5 in series).
2. **Discharge Electrodes:** Tungsten wires suspended vertically between plates, connected to the negative terminal of a T-R (Transformer-Rectifier) set at **–30 to –70 kV DC**.
3. **Collection Electrodes (Plates):** Grounded steel plates (typically 0.5 m wide × 6–10 m tall), positively charged.
4. **Rapping System:** Motorized hammers that periodically strike the plates and electrodes to dislodge dust.
5. **Hoppers:** Pyramidal collection funnels below each field for dust storage.
6. **Gas Distribution System:** Perforated plates at the inlet to ensure uniform gas flow.

**Working:** Flue gas enters the ESP and is charged by the corona discharge. Particles migrate to the plates, get collected, and are dislodged by rappers. Clean gas exits via the outlet duct. Based on the **Deutsch–Anderson equation**, efficiency can reach 99.9 %.

**Block diagram:**

```
Gas In → Distribution Plates → [Corona Discharge Zone] → Collection Plates
        → Rapping → Hopper → Gas Out → ID Fan
```

**(b) Two 210 MW units burn coal with 0.8 % sulphur at a rate of 80 tons/hr each. The FGD system removes 92 % of SO₂. Calculate the daily (24 hr) gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) produced. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Two units × 80 tons/hr = **160 tons/hr** of coal
- Sulphur content $S = 0.8 \ \%$
- FGD efficiency $\eta = 92 \ \%$

**Step 1: Mass of sulphur burned per hour**

$$\dot{m}_S = 0.008 \times 160 \times 1000 = 1280 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 2: Mass of $SO_2$ produced per hour**

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2} = \dot{m}_S \times \dfrac{M_{SO_2}}{M_S} = 1280 \times \dfrac{64}{32} = 2560 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 3: Mass of $SO_2$ removed per hour**

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2,\,removed} = 0.92 \times 2560 = 2355.2 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 4: Mass of gypsum produced per hour**

$$\dot{m}_{gypsum} = \dot{m}_{SO_2,\,removed} \times \dfrac{M_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O}}{M_{SO_2}} = 2355.2 \times \dfrac{172}{64}$$

$$\dot{m}_{gypsum} = 2355.2 \times 2.6875 = 6329.6 \ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 5: Daily production**

$$\dot{m}_{day} = 6329.6 \times 24 = 151{,}910.4 \ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{gypsum,\,day} \approx 151.9 \ \text{tons/day}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**Q. (a) Explain with a sketch the construction and working of a Long Retractable Soot Blower (LRSB). List any four types of soot blowers with their application zones. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction of LRSB:**

1. **Carriage Assembly:** Mounted outside the boiler wall on rails; carries the lance tube.
2. **Lance Tube:** Stainless steel tube of length 6–10 m that is inserted into the boiler through a stuffing box.
3. **Steam Nozzles:** Multiple nozzles welded along the lance, directed at the target tube bank.
4. **Drive Motor & Gearbox:** To push/pull the lance into the boiler and retract it.
5. **Limit Switches:** To indicate full-extended and full-retracted positions.
6. **Steam Inlet Pipe + Flexible Hose:** Connects the steam header to the lance.
7. **Control Valve:** Motor-operated valve that admits steam during the blow cycle.

**Working:**

- On a sequence command from the DCS/PLC:
  1. The carriage motor advances the lance into the furnace at **0.1–0.3 m/s**.
  2. Steam at **10–40 bar, 300–350 °C** is admitted through nozzles.
  3. High-velocity steam jets (with cleaning radius 1.5–3 m) impinge on the superheater/reheater tubes.
  4. Ash and soot deposits are blasted off and fall into the bottom ash hopper.
  5. Steam is shut off and the lance is retracted.

**Types of Soot Blowers:**

| Type | Application Zone |
|---|---|
| Long Retractable Soot Blower (LRSB) | Superheater / Reheater |
| Short Retractable Soot Blower (SRSB) | Water Walls |
| Rotary Soot Blower (RSB) | Economizer |
| Wall Blower (Fixed) | Water walls (slag removal) |
| APH Soot Blower | Air Preheater baskets |
| Sonic Soot Blower | All zones (acoustic cleaning) |

**(b) An ESP handles 250,000 Nm³/hr of flue gas. The drift velocity is 0.12 m/s and required collection efficiency is 99.0 %. Calculate the required collection plate area using Deutsch–Anderson equation. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 250{,}000 \ \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$
- $w = 0.12 \ \text{m/s}$
- $\eta = 99 \ \% = 0.99$

**Step 1: Convert Q to SI**

$$Q = \dfrac{250{,}000}{3600} = 69.44 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Deutsch–Anderson equation**

$$\eta = 1 - e^{(-w \cdot A / Q)}$$

$$0.99 = 1 - e^{(-0.12 \cdot A / 69.44)}$$

$$e^{(-0.12 \cdot A / 69.44)} = 0.01$$

Taking natural log:

$$-\dfrac{0.12 \cdot A}{69.44} = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

$$A = \dfrac{4.6052 \times 69.44}{0.12} = \dfrac{319.78}{0.12}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 2664.8 \ \text{m}^2}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $Q$ to m³/s** in Deutsch–Anderson problems — leading to an answer that is off by **3600×**.
> 2. **Confusing SO₂ and S molecular weights** in FGD mass balance — always use 64 and 32 respectively.
> 3. **Missing the stoichiometric ratio** in FGD — excess limestone must be accounted for in reagent cost.
> 4. **Confusing SCR and SNCR operating temperatures** — SCR works at 300–400 °C (catalyst-protected), while SNCR requires 850–1100 °C (no catalyst).
> 5. **Confusing rotary vs. retractable soot blowers** — rotary types are for economizer (with rotating nozzle arm), not for superheater.
> 6. **Not mentioning rapper mechanism** in ESP — rappers are essential for dislodging the dust layer; without them the plates saturate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember — Final Memory Dump Before Exam:**
>
> - **Flue gas temperature sequence:** Boiler (1200 °C) → ESP (150 °C) → FGD (60–80 °C wet) → Stack.
> - **ESP Voltage:** **–30 to –70 kV DC**; Efficiency follows $\eta = 1 - e^{(-wA/Q)}$.
> - **ESP Drift velocity** for design: **0.05–0.20 m/s**.
> - **FGD Chemistry:** $SO_2 + CaCO_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + CO_2$.
> - **FGD efficiency target:** ≥ 95 %.
> - **SCR catalyst:** $TiO_2/WO_3/V_2O_5$ at **300–400 °C** with $NH_3$.
> - **SNCR** at **850–1100 °C**, lower capex, lower efficiency.
> - **Long Retractable Soot Blower** uses **10–40 bar steam** for superheater/reheater.
> - **Rotary Soot Blower** for economizer.
> - **Wall blower / Short Retractable** for water walls (slag removal).
> - **Sonic soot blower** uses **60–300 Hz acoustic waves at 120–140 dB**.
> - **Key numbers:**
>   - $M_{SO_2} = 64$, $M_S = 32$, $M_{CaCO_3} = 100$, $M_{CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O} = 172$.
>   - ESP collection efficiency ≥ 99.9 %.
>   - Particulate norm: ≤ 30 mg/Nm³.
>   - $SO_2$ norm: ≤ 100 mg/Nm³ (sensitive zones).
>   - $NO_x$ norm: ≤ 300 mg/Nm³.
> - **Process hierarchy:** ESP → SCR → FGD → Stack (with CEMS monitoring).
> - **On-line CEMS** (Continuous Emission Monitoring System) measures SO₂, NOₓ, PM, Hg, O₂ at the stack.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam and Water Circuit: Steam Generation, Superheater, Attemperator, Reheater, Steam Turbine, Condenser, Cooling Tower, Deaerator, Economizer, Alternator, Feed Water Treatment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam and Water Circuit: Steam Generation, Superheater, Attemperator, Reheater, Steam Turbine, Condenser, Cooling Tower, Deaerator, Economizer, Alternator, Feed Water Treatment

### 1.1 The Steam and Water Circuit — Formal Definition

The **Steam and Water Circuit** (also termed the **Working Fluid Circuit** or the **Boiler–Turbine–Condenser Loop**) is the closed thermodynamic loop of a steam power station in which high-purity water circulates continuously through a sequence of heat exchangers and prime movers. Starting as liquid feedwater at the economizer inlet, the fluid is progressively heated, vaporized, superheated, expanded through the turbine, condensed, deaerated, and pumped back to the boiler. The instrumentation of this circuit constitutes the bulk of industrial-grade process control in any thermal station of capacity $\geq 100 \text{ MW}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a **CO2 / CO3 mapped** module. Examiner frequently asks descriptive questions on **Steam Generation, Superheater/Reheater, Condenser, Cooling Tower, and Deaerator** carrying **7 or 14 marks** in End Semester Examinations.

### 1.2 Component-wise Intuitive Overview

The entire circuit is best visualized as a **continuous conveyor belt for water/steam**, where at each station, energy is either added or extracted. Consider an analogy: imagine a giant coffee machine with a feedback loop.

| # | Component | Real-World Analogy | Primary Function |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Steam Generator (Boiler) | The kettle on a stove | Converts feedwater to saturated steam using fuel heat |
| 2 | Superheater | The clothes iron on dry-mode | Raises steam temperature beyond saturation |
| 3 | Attemperator | Thermostatic shower mixer | Final temperature correction of superheated steam |
| 4 | Reheater | The clothes iron on damp-mode | Reheats partially expanded steam before IP/LP turbine |
| 5 | Steam Turbine | A windmill driven by steam | Converts enthalpy drop to rotational mechanical work |
| 6 | Condenser | The back of an air-conditioner | Condenses exhaust steam to water, creates vacuum |
| 7 | Cooling Tower | The radiator grille of a car | Dissipates condenser heat to atmosphere |
| 8 | Deaerator | The bubble column in an aquarium pump | Removes dissolved O₂ and CO₂ from feedwater |
| 9 | Economizer | The pre-heater coil before a geyser | Recovers flue gas waste heat to warm feedwater |
| 10 | Alternator | A bicycle dynamo scaled up | Converts turbine's mechanical work into electricity |
| 11 | Feed Water Treatment | The domestic RO + UV purifier | Removes hardness, TDS, silica, iron from raw water |

### 1.3 The Master Process Flow (Bird's-Eye View)

The closed loop follows this exact sequence (numbers indicate direction of mass flow, not numeric priority):

$$
\text{Feed Pump} \to \text{Economizer} \to \text{Drum (Boiler)} \to \text{Superheater} \to \text{Turbine (HP)} \to \text{Reheater} \to \text{Turbine (IP)} \to \text{Turbine (LP)} \to \text{Condenser} \to \text{Condensate Pump} \to \text{Deaerator} \to \text{Feed Pump} \to \text{Economizer (Cycle Repeats)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Parameters (KTU Examination Reference):** Subcritical drum boiler operates at **$\mathbf{180 \text{ bar}}$** pressure and **$540 \pm 5^\circ\text{C}$** final steam temperature with **$540^\circ\text{C}$** reheat. Supercritical units operate at **$\geq 221 \text{ bar}$** with **$600/600^\circ\text{C}$** (re)heat temperatures. These are the **IEEE/ASME standard values** examiners expect in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Steam Generation (Boiler Classification & Internals)

Steam generators are classified based on three independent axes:

**Axis A — Tube Configuration:**
- **Fire-tube boiler**: Hot gases flow inside the tubes, water surrounds them. Used for low-pressure (up to $10 \text{ bar}$), small capacity (Cochran, Lancashire).
- **Water-tube boiler**: Water flows inside the tubes, hot gases surround them. Universal choice above $40 \text{ bar}$.

**Axis B — Circulation Type:**
- **Natural Circulation (NC)**: Density difference between riser (steam-water mixture) and downcomer (cold water) drives flow. Limited to $\leq 180 \text{ bar}$.
- **Controlled Circulation (CC)**: Uses a circulating pump to maintain $\sim 3-4$ times mass flow rate in risers. Used between $180-200 \text{ bar}$.
- **Once-Through (OT)**: No drum. Pump-driven water is converted to steam in a single pass. Mandatory above $220 \text{ bar}$ (supercritical).

**Axis C — Furnace Design:**
- **Top-supported**, **Bottom-supported**, **Top & Bottom supported (Suspension type)** — chosen by capacity and erection philosophy.

### 2.2 Superheater — Theory of Operation

The superheater extracts additional heat from flue gas after the evaporator section to raise steam temperature **above saturation temperature** ($T_{sup} > T_{sat}$ at the operating pressure). Two functional categories exist:

- **Radiant Superheater**: Located on furnace walls, absorbs primarily by radiation. Dominant at high loads (reheat-type). **Inherently self-regulating** — temperature decreases with load.
- **Convective Superheater**: Located in the back-pass, absorbs by convection. **Non-self-regulating** — temperature increases with load.

Industrial practice uses a **combination** (radiant primary + convective secondary) to obtain a flat temperature-vs-load curve.

$$
\eta_{sup} = \frac{m_s \, (h_{sup} - h_{sat})}{Q_{sup,in}} \times 100
$$

where $m_s$ is steam mass flow rate, $h_{sup} - h_{sat}$ is the enthalpy rise in the superheater, and $Q_{sup,in}$ is the heat absorbed.

### 2.3 Attemperator (Desuperheater)

Attemperators are **spray-type** (most common) or **pan-type** (older design) temperature regulators installed between primary and secondary superheaters, or at the final outlet.

- **Spray type**: Feedwater from the boiler feed pump is atomized into the steam pipe through a spray nozzle. The water flashes to steam, absorbing **latent heat** and reducing temperature by $10-30^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Pan type**: A perforated pan holds water; steam passes through the perforations. Lower cost, less accurate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** Attemperators are *not* heat exchangers. They are **direct-contact mass and energy exchangers**. They cannot increase steam temperature, only decrease it.

### 2.4 Reheater

The exhaust steam from the HP turbine (typically at $40-60 \text{ bar}$ and $320-360^\circ\text{C}$) is sent back to the boiler, reheated to the original steam temperature ($540^\circ\text{C}$ or higher), and then expanded through IP and LP turbines.

**Benefits of Reheating:**
1. Thermal efficiency improvement: $\sim 1\%$ per $56^\circ\text{C}$ of reheat (from $538^\circ\text{C}$ base).
2. Reduces **blade erosion** in LP turbine (final-stage moisture content drops from $\sim 14\%$ to $< 10\%$).
3. Increases **net work output** by $20-25\%$.

$$
\text{Reheat Factor (RF)} = \frac{h_{HP\text{-}exhaust} - h_{IP\text{-}exhaust}}{h_{1'} - h_{2'}}
$$

where primed states refer to the isentropic ideal expansion between HP and IP stages.

### 2.5 Steam Turbine

Two fundamental blade aerodynamics govern all turbines:

- **Impulse Turbine**: Steam expands fully in stationary nozzles; the velocity at blade inlet is the driving force. Pressure is constant across the moving blades. Example: De Laval, Rateau, Curtis stages.
- **Reaction Turbine**: Steam expands gradually; pressure drops across both stationary and moving blades. Reaction degree typically $50\%$. Example: Parsons turbine (universal in modern power stations).

The governing equation (Euler's turbomachinery equation):

$$
W = U \, (V_{w1} + V_{w2})
$$

where $U$ is mean blade speed, $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ are inlet and outlet whirl velocities (with sign convention).

**Degree of Reaction ($R$):**

$$
R = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}} = \frac{\text{enthalpy drop in rotor}}{\text{total stage enthalpy drop}}
$$

### 2.6 Condenser

The condenser creates and maintains a **vacuum** at the LP turbine exhaust by condensing the spent steam, reducing back pressure and maximizing work output. Two principal types:

- **Surface Condenser (Universal)**: Thousands of brass/titanium tubes carry cooling water; steam condenses on the outside. Allows separate recovery of condensate and cooling water.
- **Jet (Direct Contact) Condenser**: Steam and cooling water mix directly. Used in old plants; cannot produce pure condensate.

The vacuum produced is governed by **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**:

$$
P_{total} = P_{steam} + P_{air}
$$

Since $P_{steam}$ at typical condenser temperature ($30-45^\circ\text{C}$) is only $4.2 - 9.6 \text{ kPa}$, the bulk of the absolute pressure inside the condenser is from non-condensable gases (air). Air-removal pumps (steam ejectors or liquid-ring vacuum pumps) are mandatory.

### 2.7 Cooling Tower

Cooling towers reject the heat absorbed by the condenser cooling water to the atmosphere. Four fundamental performance terms (must be memorized for KTU exams):

- **Range ($\Delta T$):** Difference between cooling water inlet (hot) and outlet (cold) temperatures at the tower. Typical: $8-12^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Approach:** Difference between cold water outlet temperature and ambient **wet-bulb** temperature. Typical: $4-6^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Drift Loss:** Water entrained by exhaust air as droplets. Loss is $\sim 0.02\%$ of circulating flow.
- **Blowdown:** Deliberate discharge of concentrated water to limit Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) and prevent scaling.

Two main categories:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Natural Draft (Hyperbolic)} &: \text{ Uses chimney effect, no fans, used for large units} \\
\text{Mechanical Draft} &: \text{ Forced Draft (FD) or Induced Draft (ID) fans}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Evaporation Loss Formula:**

$$
E = 0.00085 \, \times \, C_w \, \times \, (T_1 - T_2) \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{hr)}
$$

where $C_w$ is the circulating water flow rate in $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$, $T_1$ is hot water inlet temp, $T_2$ is cold water outlet temp (both in $^\circ\text{C}$).

**Blowdown Equation (Cycles of Concentration basis):**

$$
B = \frac{E}{(C - 1)}
$$

where $C$ is cycles of concentration (typically $3-6$) and $E$ is evaporation loss.

### 2.8 Deaerator

The deaerator removes **dissolved oxygen** and **free CO₂** from feedwater by heating it to saturation temperature (typically $105^\circ\text{C}$ at $0.2 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ gauge, i.e., $1.2 \text{ bar}$ absolute) and scrubbing it with steam. Henry's Law governs the gas solubility:

$$
C_{O_2} = k_H(T) \, \times \, P_{O_2}
$$

As temperature rises, $k_H$ decreases sharply, forcing gases out of solution. Two designs:

- **Spray Type**: Feedwater is sprayed into a steam dome through nozzles. Lower cost, faster response.
- **Tray Type**: Feedwater trickles down perforated trays counter-current to steam. Higher deaeration efficiency ($\leq 7 \text{ ppb}$ O₂), preferred for high-pressure units.

### 2.9 Economizer

The economizer is a **fin-tube heat exchanger** placed in the flue gas path between the boiler exit and the air preheater. It recovers sensible heat from flue gas (typically $300-400^\circ\text{C}$) to raise feedwater temperature from $215^\circ\text{C}$ (post-deaerator) to $260-285^\circ\text{C}$ (before drum).

**Energy Balance on Economizer (Steady-State, No Phase Change):**

$$
\dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g} \, (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out}) = \dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w} \, (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})
$$

**Effect on Boiler Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{boiler}^{new} = \eta_{boiler}^{old} \times \frac{\eta_{old}}{(1 - p_f)} \quad \text{or approximately} \quad \Delta\eta \approx \frac{T_{fw} - T_{sat}}{GCV} \times 100
$$

Typical fuel savings: **$5-10\%$**, depending on feedwater temperature lift.

### 2.10 Alternator (Synchronous Generator)

The alternator converts the turbine's mechanical output to electrical energy. Three-phase, salient-pole (low-speed, $\leq 1000 \text{ rpm}$ for 50 Hz) or cylindrical-rotor (high-speed, $3000 \text{ rpm}$ for 50 Hz, used with turbo-alternators).

**Frequency Equation:**

$$
f = \frac{P \times N}{120} \quad (\text{Hz})
$$

where $P$ is the number of poles and $N$ is rotor speed in rpm.

**Generated EMF per Phase:**

$$
E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_w
$$

where $N$ is turns per phase, $\phi$ is flux per pole in Wb, $k_w$ is winding factor ($\approx 0.95-0.96$).

**Cooling Methods:** Air (small units), Hydrogen (large turbo-alternators, hydrogen's high thermal conductivity), Water (stator winding of very large units).

### 2.11 Feed Water Treatment

Raw water must be treated to limit:
- **Total Hardness** $\leq 2 \text{ ppm}$ (as CaCO₃)
- **Silica** $\leq 0.02 \text{ ppm}$ for high-pressure boilers
- **Dissolved Oxygen** $\leq 7 \text{ ppb}$
- **pH** $= 8.5-9.5$
- **Conductivity** $\leq 0.5 \, \mu\text{S/cm}$

**Treatment Train (typical 500 MW subcritical unit):**

$$
\text{Raw Water} \to \text{Clarifier} \to \text{Multi-grade Filter} \to \text{UF Membrane} \to \text{RO} \to \text{Demin (MB + Degasifier)} \to \text{Condensate Polishers} \to \text{Boiler}
$$

**Lime-Soda Softening Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 &\to 2\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ca(OH)}_2 &\to \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + 2\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 &\to \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + \text{CaSO}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 2.12 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|-------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)}{h_1 - h_4}$ | $h$ in kJ/kg |
| 2 | Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}} \quad [\text{kg/kWh}]$ | $W_{net}$ in kJ/kg |
| 3 | Reheat Factor | $RF = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_{2'}) + (h_{3} - h_{4'})}{h_{1s} - h_{4}}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Condenser Vacuum (% vac) | $\%V = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} \times 100$ | pressure in bar/mmHg |
| 5 | Cooling Tower Range | $R = T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,out}$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 6 | Cooling Tower Approach | $A = T_{cold,out} - T_{wb}$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 7 | Evaporation Loss | $E = 0.00085 \cdot C_w \cdot \Delta T$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ |
| 8 | Blowdown | $B = \dfrac{E}{C-1}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ |
| 9 | Cycles of Concentration | $C = \dfrac{\text{TDS}_{cw}}{\text{TDS}_{makeup}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Deaerator Henry's Law | $C = k_H(T) \cdot P_{gas}$ | $k_H$ decreases with $T$ |
| 11 | Economizer Heat Duty | $Q = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | kW or kJ/s |
| 12 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | percent |
| 13 | Alternator EMF | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, k_w$ | Volts per phase |
| 14 | Alternator Frequency | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{120}$ | Hz |
| 15 | Degree of Reaction | $R = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \leq R \leq 1$ |
| 16 | Make-up Water Required | $M = E + B + D_{drift}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** The exam answer must explicitly state the **assumed working fluid state** (saturated liquid / superheated / wet steam) at every state point. Examiners deduct marks for unmarked states.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency with Reheat

**Given:**
- State 1: Superheated steam at HP turbine inlet, pressure $P_1 = 180 \text{ bar}$, temperature $T_1 = 540^\circ\text{C}$
- State 2: HP turbine exhaust (before reheat), $P_2 = 40 \text{ bar}$, $T_2 = 320^\circ\text{C}$
- State 3: Reheated steam at IP turbine inlet, $P_3 = 40 \text{ bar}$, $T_3 = 540^\circ\text{C}$
- State 4: LP turbine exhaust, $P_4 = 0.1 \text{ bar}$ (condenser pressure)
- State 5: Condensate saturated liquid at $0.1 \text{ bar}$
- State 6: Feedwater after pump, $P_6 = 180 \text{ bar}$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define Net Work Output**

The net work of the reheat cycle is the algebraic sum of turbine work and pump work:

$$
W_{net} = W_{turbine} + W_{pump}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Turbine Work**

In a reheat cycle, the turbine is split into HP and IP/LP sections:

$$
W_{turbine} = W_{HP} + W_{IP/LP}
$$

For $1 \text{ kg/s}$ of working fluid, this translates to enthalpy differences:

$$
W_{turbine} = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)
$$

where subscript 1→2 denotes HP turbine and 3→4 denotes IP/LP turbine.

**Step 3 — Pump Work Contribution**

The boiler feed pump raises condensate from condenser pressure ($0.1 \text{ bar}$) to economizer/boiler pressure ($180 \text{ bar}$). Pump work is usually small and **negative** (work input):

$$
W_{pump} = -(h_6 - h_5) \approx -v_5 (P_6 - P_5)
$$

where $v_5$ is specific volume of saturated liquid at $0.1 \text{ bar} \approx 0.00101 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$.

**Step 4 — Combine**

$$
W_{net} = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) - (h_6 - h_5)
$$

**Step 5 — Define Heat Input**

Heat is supplied in **two places**: economizer/evaporator/superheater (between state 6 and state 1) and reheater (between state 2 and state 3):

$$
Q_{in} = (h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)
$$

**Step 6 — Final Efficiency Equation**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) - (h_6 - h_5)}{(h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Verification (using standard steam tables)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= 3399.5 \text{ kJ/kg (at 180 bar, 540°C)} \\
h_2 &\approx 3040 \text{ kJ/kg (at 40 bar, 320°C)} \\
h_3 &= 3556.5 \text{ kJ/kg (at 40 bar, 540°C)} \\
h_4 &\approx 2240 \text{ kJ/kg (at 0.1 bar, isentropic to dryness } x \approx 0.86) \\
h_5 &\approx 192 \text{ kJ/kg (sat. liq. at 0.1 bar)} \\
h_6 &\approx 208 \text{ kJ/kg (after pump to 180 bar)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
W_{net} = (3399.5 - 3040) + (3556.5 - 2240) - (208 - 192) = 359.5 + 1316.5 - 16 = 1660 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = (3399.5 - 208) + (3556.5 - 3040) = 3191.5 + 516.5 = 3708 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{1660}{3708} \times 100 \approx 44.77\%
$$

This matches standard textbook data for a **subcritical reheat Rankine cycle**, validating the derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Condenser Vacuum from Barometer Reading

**Given:** A condenser vacuum gauge reads $720 \text{ mm Hg}$ vacuum. Local atmospheric pressure is $760 \text{ mm Hg}$. Find the absolute pressure inside the condenser and the percentage vacuum.

**Step 1 — Convert Vacuum to Absolute Pressure**

By definition of vacuum:

$$
P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}
$$

$$
P_{abs} = 760 - 720 = 40 \text{ mm Hg}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert mm Hg to kPa (SI)**

$$
P_{abs} = \frac{40}{760} \times 101.325 = 5.33 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.0533 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Percentage Vacuum**

$$
\%V = \frac{P_{vac}}{P_{atm}} \times 100 = \frac{720}{760} \times 100 = 94.74\%
$$

**Step 4 — Infer Condenser Saturation Temperature**

From saturated steam tables, $P_{sat} = 5.33 \text{ kPa}$ corresponds to $T_{sat} \approx 33.8^\circ\text{C}$. This is the operating temperature of the condensate inside the condenser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trap:** A "vacuum of 720 mm" means the *gauge* reads 720 mm below atmospheric. The absolute pressure is **40 mm Hg**, **not** 720 mm Hg. This is a very common mark-losing mistake.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Tower Mass Balance with Cycles of Concentration

**Given:** A cooling tower handles $C_w = 50000 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ of circulating water. Inlet hot temperature $T_1 = 42^\circ\text{C}$, outlet cold temperature $T_2 = 32^\circ\text{C}$. Cycles of concentration $C = 4$. Drift loss $D = 0.001 \times C_w$.

**Step 1 — Compute Range**

$$
\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = 42 - 32 = 10^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Evaporation Loss**

$$
E = 0.00085 \times 50000 \times 10 = 425 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Blowdown**

$$
B = \frac{E}{C - 1} = \frac{425}{4 - 1} = 141.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Drift Loss**

$$
D = 0.001 \times 50000 = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total Make-up Water**

$$
M = E + B + D = 425 + 141.67 + 50 = 616.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify Mass Balance**

Steady-state: Make-up $=$ Evaporation $+$ Blowdown $+$ Drift $= 616.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$. Mass is conserved.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Economizer Heat Duty & Fuel Saving

**Given:** A 200 MW boiler has flue gas flow $\dot{m}_g = 250 \text{ kg/s}$ at $c_{p,g} = 1.05 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Flue gas enters economizer at $380^\circ\text{C}$, leaves at $180^\circ\text{C}$. Feedwater enters at $215^\circ\text{C}$ ($c_{p,w} = 4.2 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$), flow rate $200 \text{ kg/s}$. Find feedwater outlet temperature and fuel saving if GCV of coal = $20000 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Energy Balance**

$$
\dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g} \, (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out}) = \dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w} \, (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in Numerical Values**

$$
250 \times 1.05 \times (380 - 180) = 200 \times 4.2 \times (T_{w,out} - 215)
$$

$$
52500 = 840 \times (T_{w,out} - 215)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Outlet Temperature**

$$
T_{w,out} - 215 = \frac{52500}{840} = 62.5^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
T_{w,out} = 215 + 62.5 = 277.5^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Heat Saved**

$$
Q_{saved} = \dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w} \, \Delta T_w = 200 \times 4.2 \times 62.5 = 52500 \text{ kW} = 52.5 \text{ MW (thermal)}
$$

**Step 5 — Translate to Fuel Saving**

$$
\dot{m}_{coal,saved} = \frac{52500}{20000} = 2.625 \text{ kg/s} \approx 9.45 \text{ tonnes/hour}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** A 200 MW unit at full load consumes ~200 t/h of coal. An economizer saving ~9.45 t/h is **4.7% fuel saving** — which is why economizers are non-negotiable in modern designs.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component Comparison

| Feature | Natural Circulation | Controlled Circulation | Once-Through |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Drum | Yes (large) | Yes (small) | No |
| Max Pressure (bar) | $\leq 180$ | $180-220$ | $\geq 221$ (supercritical) |
| Pump | None | Circulating pump | Feed pump only |
| Start-up Time | Slow (6-8 hr) | Moderate (3-5 hr) | Fast ($< 1$ hr) |
| Load Range | $50-100\%$ | $40-100\%$ | $25-100\%$ |
| Capital Cost | Moderate | Higher | Highest |

| Feature | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Pressure drop in rotor | Zero | Non-zero (50% typical) |
| Blade shape | Symmetrical | Asymmetrical (aerofoil) |
| Speed | High ($> 3000$ rpm) | Low ($\leq 3000$ rpm) |
| Stage efficiency | $70-85\%$ | $85-92\%$ |
| Typical use | Small/medium HP sections | LP and modern HP/IP |

| Feature | Surface Condenser | Jet Condenser |
|---------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Cooling water contact | Indirect | Direct |
| Condensate purity | High (recovered) | Mixed with cooling water |
| Vacuum achievable | $0.05 \text{ bar abs}$ | $0.1 \text{ bar abs}$ |
| Capital cost | High | Low |
| Use | Universal in modern plants | Obsolete |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Steam and Water Circuit (Plant-Level Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Water Treatment Plant] --> A2[DM Water Storage Tank]
    A2 --> A3[Condensate Polishers]
    A3 --> A4[Condensate Hotwell]
    A4 --> A5[Condensate Extraction Pump]
    A5 --> A6[Deaerator Storage Tank]
    A6 --> A7[Boiler Feed Pump BFP]
    A7 --> B1[Economizer]
    B1 --> B2[Boiler Drum]
    B2 --> B3[Water Walls and Downcomers]
    B3 --> B4[Primary Superheater]
    B4 --> B5[Attemperator Spray]
    B5 --> B6[Secondary Superheater]
    B6 --> B7[HP Turbine Stage 1]
    B7 --> B8[Hot Reheat Line]
    B8 --> B9[Reheater in Boiler]
    B9 --> B10[Cold Reheat to IP Turbine]
    B10 --> B11[IP Turbine]
    B11 --> B12[LP Turbine]
    B12 --> C1[Main Condenser]
    C1 --> C2[Condensate Hotwell - Cycle Restart]
    C1 --> C3[Cooling Water Inlet from Tower]
    C3 --> C4[Cooling Tower Basin]
    C4 --> C5[CW Pumps]
    C5 --> C6[Cooling Water Outlet to Condenser]
    C6 --> C1
    B12 --> D1[Alternator Rotor Coupled to LP Turbine Shaft]
    D1 --> D2[Step-up Transformer 21 kV to 400 kV]
    D2 --> D3[Switchyard and Grid]
```

### 4.2 Boiler Internals (Drum + Water Walls + Superheater Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Pulverized Coal from Mills] --> F2[Furnace Combustion Zone 1500C]
    F2 --> F3[Water Wall Tubes - Radiant Section]
    F2 --> F4[Primary Superheater - Platen]
    F2 --> F5[Reheater - Pendant Tubes]
    F4 --> F6[Attemperator - Spray Header]
    F6 --> F7[Secondary Superheater - Final Outlet 540C]
    F3 --> F8[Steam Drum - Saturated Steam at Top]
    F8 --> F9[Downcomers - Cold Water to Furnace]
    F9 --> F3
    F8 --> F4
    F5 --> F10[Reheat Steam to IP Turbine]
    F7 --> F11[Main Steam Line to HP Turbine]
    F3 --> F12[Economizer - In Convective Pass]
    F12 --> F13[Air Preheater]
    F13 --> F14[ESP / Bag Filter]
    F14 --> F15[ID Fan to Stack]
```

### 4.3 Condenser and Cooling Tower Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LP[LP Turbine Exhaust Steam] --> COND[Surface Condenser Shell]
    CW_IN[Cooling Water Inlet from Cooling Tower - Cold 32C] --> COND
    COND --> HW[Hotwell - Condensate at 33C]
    COND --> CW_OUT[Cooling Water Outlet - Hot 42C to Cooling Tower]
    HW --> CP[Condensate Pump]
    CP --> DEA[Deaerator]
    CW_OUT --> CT_TOP[Cooling Tower Top - Distribution Header]
    CT_TOP --> CT_FILL[PVC Fill Packing]
    CT_FILL --> CT_BASIN[Cooling Tower Basin Cold Water 32C]
    CT_BASIN --> CW_IN
    AIR[Ambient Air Inlet] --> CT_FILL
    CT_FILL --> AIR_OUT[Warm Saturated Air Exhaust to Atmosphere]
```

### 4.4 Feed Water Treatment Schematic (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RW[Raw Water Source] --> CLAR[Clarifier with Alum and Lime]
    CLAR --> MG[Multi-Grade Filter Sand and Anthracite]
    MG --> UF[Ultrafiltration UF Membrane]
    UF --> RO[Reverse Osmosis Two Pass]
    RO --> MB[Mixed Bed Exchanger]
    MB --> DG[Degasifier for CO2 Removal]
    DG --> DM[Demineralized Water Storage Tank]
    DM --> CP[Condensate Polishers]
    CP --> DEA[Deaerator at 105C]
    DEA --> BFP[Boiler Feed Pump]
    BFP --> ECO[Economizer and Boiler]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Rationale:** Mermaid does not support complex curved physical 3D schematics. The above block-level flow diagrams accurately capture the **functional sequence** of components, which is what examiners verify in the answer script for **14-mark process description** questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between the Radiant and Convective Superheater with reference to their location, heat transfer mode, and load response.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Radiant Superheater | Convective Superheater |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Location | Furnace walls / platen | Convective back-pass |
| Heat Transfer Mode | Primarily radiation | Primarily convection |
| Load Response | Temperature **decreases** with load (self-regulating) | Temperature **increases** with load (non-self-regulating) |
| Application | High-load, high-temperature units | Supplementary / trim heating |

**Key Point:** Modern boilers combine both — radiant first, convective last — to obtain a flat steam temperature across the load range. **(1 mark each sub-point)**

---

**Q2. What is an Attemperator? Why is it used in modern supercritical and subcritical boiler units?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

An **attemperator** is a temperature-correcting device installed between the primary and secondary superheater (or at the final outlet). It **sprays deaerated feedwater** directly into the steam pipe. The water flashes to steam, absorbing latent heat, and thereby **lowers** the steam temperature by $10$ to $30^\circ\text{C}$.

**Reasons for use:**
1. To keep the final steam temperature **within the specified tolerance** of $\pm 5^\circ\text{C}$ as load fluctuates.
2. To protect the superheater tubes from overheating at low loads.
3. To extend the life of high-temperature alloys by avoiding thermal cycling damage.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Any **ONE** question out of two is to be answered, carrying 14 marks total. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

#### ✅ **Question 1 (14 Marks): Steam Generation and Superheat / Reheat Circuit**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper / ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **water-tube, natural circulation, drum-type boiler** used in thermal power plants. Discuss the role of the steam drum, downcomers, risers, and water walls. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the functions and types of **Superheater** and **Reheater** in a thermal power station. Why is reheating essential? Show the effect of reheating on cycle efficiency with a T-s diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **drum-type, natural circulation water-tube boiler** is the workhorse of subcritical thermal stations (up to 180 bar). Its key components are:

1. **Steam/Water Drum** — A large cylindrical vessel at the top that separates saturated steam from boiling water. Internally fitted with **cyclone separators**, **scrubbers**, and **chemical dozing** points.
2. **Downcomers** — Large-diameter unheated pipes that carry cold water from the drum bottom to the furnace bottom headers by gravity.
3. **Risers / Water Walls** — Closely spaced tubes on the furnace walls that absorb radiant heat. Water boils, forming a steam-water mixture of lower density.
4. **Headers** — Top (drum-side) and bottom (downcomer-side) headers distribute water and collect steam.

**Working Principle:** Density difference ($\rho_{downcomer} > \rho_{riser}$) creates a **natural pressure head**:

$$
\Delta P_{driving} = (\rho_w - \rho_{mix}) \cdot g \cdot H
$$

This drives circulation. Typical circulation ratio (mass flow in riser / steam generated) is $4-8$. Beyond $180 \text{ bar}$, density difference collapses, requiring controlled or once-through circulation.

**Diagram:** Refer to **Figure 4.2** in Section 4 above.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Superheater:**
A superheater is a bank of tubes located in the high-temperature zone of the boiler that raises the temperature of saturated steam **above** its saturation point, producing **dry, superheated steam**. This prevents erosion of the LP turbine blades and increases cycle efficiency.

**Types:**
- **Primary (Radiant) Superheater**: On furnace walls, absorbs radiation. Temperature falls as load rises (self-regulating).
- **Secondary (Convective) Superheater**: In back-pass, absorbs convection. Temperature rises as load rises (non-self-regulating).

**Reheater:**
Steam exiting the HP turbine at $30-40\%$ moisture level (after partial expansion) is returned to the boiler and reheated to the original steam temperature before being sent to the IP/LP turbines.

**Why reheat is essential:**
1. Increases net work output by $20-25\%$.
2. Improves cycle efficiency by $\sim 5\%$ absolute.
3. Reduces moisture content in LP stages to $< 10\%$, preventing blade erosion.
4. Allows the use of higher steam pressure without increasing final-stage moisture.

**T-s Diagram:** State 1 (boiler exit, superheated) → 2 (HP exhaust, wet steam) → Reheat to 3 (reheated to original temperature at lower pressure) → 4 (LP exhaust). The average temperature of heat addition is higher than a non-reheat cycle, yielding higher $\eta_{th}$.

---

#### ✅ **Question 2 (14 Marks): Condenser, Cooling Tower, and Feed Water Treatment**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper / ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Surface Condenser** with a neat sketch. Define **percentage vacuum** and **condenser efficiency**. A condenser operates with $720 \text{ mm Hg}$ vacuum when atmospheric pressure is $760 \text{ mm Hg}$. Calculate the absolute pressure and saturation temperature inside the condenser. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a schematic, describe the construction and working of an **Induced Draft Counter-Flow Cooling Tower**. Define **Range, Approach, Drift, and Blowdown**. A cooling tower circulates $60000 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ with a range of $10^\circ\text{C}$ and cycles of concentration of $5$. Drift is $0.1\%$ of circulation. Compute the make-up water requirement. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Surface Condenser Construction:**
- **Shell**: Large cylindrical carbon-steel shell containing the steam-side.
- **Tube bundle**: Several thousand brass/titanium tubes, water-tight through tube sheets at both ends.
- **Steam space**: LP turbine exhaust enters the top, condenses on tube surfaces, drips down to the hotwell.
- **Hotwell**: Collects condensate below the lower tube sheet.
- **Air extraction**: Air leaking in (through turbine glands, pipe joints) is continuously removed by steam-jet ejectors or liquid-ring vacuum pumps.

**Working:** Cooling water flows inside the tubes. Exhaust steam from the LP turbine enters the shell. As steam contacts the cold tubes, it condenses, creating vacuum (low back pressure). The condensate falls into the hotwell and is pumped forward by the condensate extraction pump.

**Definitions:**
- **Percentage Vacuum**: $\%V = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} \times 100$
- **Condenser Efficiency**: $\eta_{cond} = \dfrac{T_{steam,in} - T_{condensate,out}}{T_{steam,in} - T_{cooling\,water,out}} \times 100$

**Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Absolute Pressure:**
$$
P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac} = 760 - 720 = 40 \text{ mm Hg}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kPa:**
$$
P_{abs} = \frac{40}{760} \times 101.325 = 5.33 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Saturation Temperature:**
From steam tables, at $P_{sat} = 5.33 \text{ kPa}$, $T_{sat} \approx 33.8^\circ\text{C}$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Induced Draft Counter-Flow Cooling Tower:**

In an IDCT (Induced Draft Counter-Flow Tower):
- **Air flow**: Drawn by an axial-flow fan at the top, moving upward.
- **Water flow**: Sprayed downward from the top distribution header through PVC fill packing.
- **Contact**: Air and water flow in **counter-current** direction, maximizing contact time and heat transfer.
- **Drift eliminators**: At the top, capture entrained water droplets to minimize drift loss.
- **Basin**: At the bottom, collects cooled water.

**Definitions:**

- **Range** ($R$): Difference between hot water inlet and cold water outlet temperature.
- **Approach** ($A$): Difference between cold water outlet and ambient wet-bulb temperature.
- **Drift**: Water droplets lost to atmosphere with the exhaust air (typically $0.02-0.1\%$ of circulation).
- **Blowdown**: Purge of concentrated water to maintain acceptable TDS / Cycles of Concentration.

**Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Evaporation Loss:**
$$
E = 0.00085 \times C_w \times \Delta T = 0.00085 \times 60000 \times 10 = 510 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Blowdown:**
$$
B = \frac{E}{C - 1} = \frac{510}{5 - 1} = 127.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Drift Loss:**
$$
D = 0.001 \times 60000 = 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Make-up Water:**
$$
M = E + B + D = 510 + 127.5 + 60 = 697.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** Make-up equals evaporation + blowdown + drift, satisfying mass conservation. A typical 500 MW unit requires $2500-3500 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ make-up; this tower's demand of $697.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ is consistent with a smaller 200 MW unit.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Where Marks Are Lost):**
> 1. **State-point confusion:** Forgetting to mark states on the T-s diagram (1→2→3→4→5→6) — loses **2 marks** even if the formula is correct.
> 2. **Wrong units:** Reporting pressure in `bar` instead of `Pa`, or temperature in `°C` without noting that steam tables use `K`. Examiner deducts for dimensional inconsistency.
> 3. **Pump work omission:** Many students compute $W_{turbine}$ only and ignore $W_{pump}$ in Rankine efficiency. Even though small ($<1\%$), examiners **expect** the $-v_5(P_6-P_5)$ term.
> 4. **Cooling Tower formula misuse:** Using $0.00085$ as a universal constant (it is empirical; varies with relative humidity). Acknowledge the assumption in the answer.
> 5. **Confusing approach vs range:** Approach is relative to **wet-bulb** (not dry-bulb) — a frequent conceptual error.
> 6. **No diagram:** Even a simple block diagram carries **2-3 marks** in 14-mark descriptive answers.
> 7. **Naming components but not explaining the working:** Listing the parts of a condenser without explaining "how" the vacuum forms loses depth marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Boiler types:** Fire-tube (low pressure, small capacity), Water-tube (universal). Within water-tube: **Natural** ($< 180$ bar), **Controlled** ($180-200$ bar), **Once-Through** ($> 220$ bar).
- **Superheater location:** Primary (radiant, furnace walls) + Secondary (convective, back-pass). Final steam temp $540-600^\circ\text{C}$ tolerance $\pm 5^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Attemperator** reduces temperature only (by water spray). Used as final trim.
- **Reheat factor** = ratio of actual to isentropic cumulative work; always $>1$.
- **Turbine governing equation** $W = U(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ is the most-tested formula.
- **Condenser vacuum** is the dominant performance indicator of LP section health.
- **Cooling Tower 4-mnemonic:** **RADB** — **R**ange, **A**pproach, **D**rift, **B**lowdown.
- **Deaerator** removes O₂ to $\leq 7 \text{ ppb}$, operates at $105^\circ\text{C}$, $0.2 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ gauge.
- **Economizer** saves **$5-10\%$** fuel by recovering flue gas sensible heat to raise feedwater from $215$ to $275^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Alternator EMF** formula $E = 4.44 f N \phi k_w$ is **per phase**, RMS value.
- **Feed water quality:** Hardness $< 2 \text{ ppm}$, Silica $< 0.02 \text{ ppm}$, pH $8.5-9.5$, Conductivity $< 0.5 \, \mu\text{S/cm}$.
- **Standard cycle parameters (KTU typical):** $180/540/540$ °C for subcritical, $250/600/600$ °C for supercritical.
- **Reheat benefit:** $\approx 5\%$ efficiency gain + protection of LP blades from moisture erosion.
- **Energy units in SI:** Always convert $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$ when using international steam tables.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Components Order:** **"B-S-A-R-T-C-C-D-E-A-F"** → **B**oiler, **S**uperheater, **A**ttemperator, **R**eheater, **T**urbine, **C**ondenser, **C**ooling Tower, **D**eaerator, **E**conomizer, **A**lternator, **F**eed water Treatment. Read clockwise on the plant layout.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Non-Electrical Parameters in Power Plants: Metal Temperature Measurement, Pressure and Temperature Measurement of Water and Steam, Drum Level Measurement, Smoke Density Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Non-Electrical Parameters in Power Plants: Metal Temperature Measurement, Pressure and Temperature Measurement of Water and Steam, Drum Level Measurement, Smoke Density Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Overview of Non-Electrical Parameter Measurement in Power Plants

In a thermal power plant, the efficient and safe conversion of chemical energy (fuel) into electrical energy depends critically on the continuous, accurate monitoring of various **process parameters** such as temperature, pressure, level, and smoke density. Since these parameters are inherently **non-electrical** in nature, they must first be transduced into proportional electrical signals (voltage, current, or resistance) before being displayed, recorded, or used for automatic control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Non-electrical parameter measurement in power plants refers to the use of transducers and sensor systems to convert physical quantities like temperature, pressure, level, and flue-gas opacity into standardized electrical signals (typically 4–20 mA or 0–10 V) for indication, recording, alarm, and closed-loop control.*

### 1.2 Metal Temperature Measurement

**Formal Definition:**
Metal temperature measurement in a power plant refers to the continuous monitoring of the surface or internal temperature of critical metallic components such as **boiler tubes, superheater tubes, reheater headers, and steam pipes** that operate at extremely high pressures and temperatures.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine placing a thermometer against a hot frying pan in your kitchen. The pan's metal temperature tells you whether the food will cook properly or burn. Similarly, in a power plant, if a superheater tube's metal temperature exceeds its design limit, the metal may **creep**, **oxidize**, or even **rupture catastrophically**. Hence, we embed small temperature sensors (thermocouples) directly on or inside the metal to monitor this "internal fever" continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Metal Temperature Limits (KTU Highlight):**
> - Carbon Steel Tubes: $\leq 450\ ^\circ C$
> - Alloy Steel (Cr-Mo): $\leq 570\ ^\circ C$
> - Austenitic Stainless Steel: $\leq 650\ ^\circ C$

### 1.3 Pressure Measurement of Water and Steam

**Formal Definition:**
Pressure measurement in a boiler system involves the determination of fluid (water or steam) pressure at strategic points such as the **drum, superheater outlet, reheater inlet/outlet, feedwater line, and condenser** using elastic-element transducers.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a bicycle pump's pressure gauge. When you pump air, a small dial needle deflects to show the pressure. In a power plant, the same principle is used, but with much sturdier gauges designed to handle pressures up to **$250\ \text{bar}$** and superheated steam temperatures up to **$600\ ^\circ C$**.

### 1.4 Temperature Measurement of Water and Steam

**Formal Definition:**
This involves measuring the bulk temperature of the working fluid (water, saturated steam, or superheated steam) at various stages of the Rankine cycle, typically using **sheathed thermocouples** or **RTDs** installed inside thermowells.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
When you check the temperature of milk in a saucepan using a cooking thermometer dipped into the liquid, you are performing fluid temperature measurement. In a power plant, the sensor must withstand corrosion, scaling, and high-velocity flow.

### 1.5 Drum Level Measurement

**Formal Definition:**
Drum level measurement refers to the determination of the water level inside the **steam drum** of a boiler, which is the single most critical measurement for boiler safety.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a pressure cooker on your stove. If all the water boils away, the cooker becomes dangerously hot and may explode. The boiler drum is similar — if the water level drops below a safe limit, the tubes are exposed and may **rupture within seconds**. Hence, accurate level measurement (typically controlled to within $\pm 50\ \text{mm}$) is non-negotiable.

### 1.6 Smoke Density Measurement

**Formal Definition:**
Smoke density (or **opacity**) measurement quantifies the concentration of particulate matter in the flue gas exiting the stack, expressed as a percentage of light obscuration per unit path length (typically % opacity per meter).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
When you drive behind a diesel truck emitting thick black smoke, you can barely see through the exhaust. Smoke density meters do the same thing using a light beam and a photo-detector to convert the "thickness" of smoke into an electrical signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Units in Power Plant Instrumentation:**
> - Temperature: $^\circ C$ or $K$ (SI)
> - Pressure: $\text{bar}$, $\text{MPa}$, or $\text{kg/cm}^2$
> - Drum Level: $\text{mm}$ of water column
> - Smoke Density: % opacity

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Metal Temperature Measurement

### 2.1.1 Why Measure Metal Temperature?
- **Tube failure prevention:** Creep rupture occurs when metal temperature exceeds design limits for prolonged periods.
- **Efficiency optimization:** Higher superheat temperatures improve Rankine cycle efficiency.
- **Thermal fatigue monitoring:** Cyclic stresses due to temperature variations.

### 2.1.2 Sensors Used

**A. Thermocouples (Most Common):**
- **Type K (Chromel-Alumel):** Range $-200\ ^\circ C$ to $1260\ ^\circ C$; used for superheater tubes.
- **Type R / S (Pt-Pt/Rh):** Range $0\ ^\circ C$ to $1480\ ^\circ C$; high accuracy for reheater headers.
- **Type E:** Higher sensitivity ($\approx 68\ \mu V/^\circ C$); used in lower-temperature regions.

**Seebeck Effect Working Principle:**
$$E_{AB}(T) = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] dT$$

where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the **Seebeck coefficients** of the two dissimilar metals.

**B. Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs):**
- Use the change in electrical resistance of pure metals (Platinum, Nickel, or Copper).
- Most common: **Pt100** ($100\ \Omega$ at $0\ ^\circ C$).

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$$

For higher accuracy (Callendar-Van Dusen equation):
$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + AT + BT^2 + C(T - 100)T^3 \right]$$

where for Platinum: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$.

**C. Optical Pyrometers / Radiation Pyrometers:**
- Used where contact is impossible (e.g., rotary kiln, furnace flame).
- Based on **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:
$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2 K^4$.

### 2.1.3 Installation Methods
1. **Surface Mounting:** Thermocouple welded or clamped to the tube outer surface.
2. **Weld-on Pad Method:** A pad is welded to the tube and TC is inserted into the pad.
3. **Thermowell Method:** A pocket is drilled into the tube wall and TC is inserted.
4. **Embedded Method:** Thermocouple is permanently embedded during tube manufacture.

### 2.1.4 Practical Considerations
- **Thermal inertia** of the thermowell causes a time lag.
- **Heat conduction** error: The lead wire acts as a fin, conducting heat away from the junction.
- **Decalibration** due to high-temperature drift, especially in Type K thermocouples.

## 2.2 Pressure Measurement of Water and Steam

### 2.2.1 Categories of Pressure in Power Plant

| Parameter | Typical Range | Sensor Type |
|---|---|---|
| Feedwater pressure | 200–250 bar | Bourdon tube |
| Steam drum pressure | 100–180 bar | Diaphragm + DP cell |
| Superheater outlet | 100–180 bar | Bourdon tube |
| Condenser vacuum | $-0.95$ to $-0.99\ \text{bar}$ | Diaphragm (vacuum gauge) |
| Air/flue gas pressure | $-50$ to $+50\ \text{mbar}$ | Inclined tube manometer |

### 2.2.2 Working Principles

**A. Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge:**
- A curved metal tube (elliptical cross-section) straightens when internal pressure rises.
- The tip displacement is amplified through a mechanical linkage to a pointer.

**B. Diaphragm-Type Pressure Transducer:**
- A thin metallic or quartz diaphragm deflects in proportion to pressure.
- Deflection is measured via **strain gauge bridge** or **capacitive sensing**.

**C. Capacitive Pressure Transducer:**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - \Delta d}$$

A small change in $\Delta d$ (diaphragm deflection) causes a measurable change in capacitance.

**D. Strain Gauge Pressure Transducer:**
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $G_f$ is the gauge factor ($\approx 2$ for constantan) and $\varepsilon$ is the strain.

### 2.2.3 Siphon and Pigtail Installation
A **siphon (pigtail)** is used between the steam pipe and the gauge to:
- Prevent direct contact of high-temperature steam with the gauge.
- Allow condensate to collect and act as a thermal barrier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Practice:**
> Steam pressure gauges **must** be installed below the tapping point with a siphon to form a water seal. The gauge reads the **static head** difference, which is added as a correction.

## 2.3 Temperature Measurement of Water and Steam

### 2.3.1 Sensor Selection Criteria

| Service | Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Economizer inlet water | Pt100 RTD | Low temp, high accuracy |
| Boiler drum water | Type K TC | High temp, fast response |
| Superheater outlet steam | Type K TC | High temp, durability |
| Reheater outlet | Type R/S TC | High accuracy, stability |
| HP Heater | Pt100 RTD | Moderate temp |

### 2.3.2 Installation in Thermowells
- Thermowells protect the sensor from corrosion, pressure, and mechanical damage.
- Materials: **Stainless Steel 316**, **Inconel 600**, **Monel**.
- The well introduces a **time lag** characterized by:

$$\tau = \frac{m \cdot c_p}{h \cdot A}$$

where $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $h$ = heat transfer coefficient, $A$ = area.

### 2.3.3 Lead Wire Compensation (for RTD)
For 2-wire RTD, the lead resistance $R_L$ adds error:
$$R_{\text{measured}} = R_T + 2R_L$$
For 3-wire or 4-wire connection, this error is eliminated using the **Kelvin bridge principle**.

## 2.4 Drum Level Measurement

### 2.4.1 Critical Importance
The boiler drum is the most critical pressure vessel. Under low water conditions:
- Tube metal is exposed to high-temperature gases.
- Tube failure occurs within **30–60 seconds**.
- This is a major cause of boiler explosions.

### 2.4.2 Methods of Measurement

**A. Differential Pressure (DP) Cell Method — Most Common**
- Two tapping points on the steam drum: top (steam) and bottom (water).
- The DP across the water column is proportional to drum level.

$$h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g}$$

where $\Delta P$ is the differential pressure, $\rho_{\text{water}}$ is the density of saturated water, and $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$.

**B. Conductivity / Probe Type**
- Uses the electrical conductivity difference between water and steam.
- Electrodes are inserted at multiple levels; each electrode gives a discrete "level reached" signal.
- Useful for **alarm** purposes (high/low water level).

**C. Float-Type**
- A float rests on the water surface and moves with level.
- Mechanical or magnetic coupling transmits position.

**D. Displacer Type**
- A displacer immersed in the boiling water experiences buoyancy proportional to submerged length.
- Works on Archimedes' principle: $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{submerged}}$.

**E. Sight Glass (Gauge Glass)**
- A transparent glass tube connected to the drum.
- Provides direct visual indication.
- **Reflex glass type** uses prismatic grooves for clearer reading.

**F. Capacitance Probe**
- The probe acts as one plate of a capacitor; water (high $\varepsilon_r$) and steam (low $\varepsilon_r$) produce different capacitances.

$$C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$$

### 2.4.3 Drum Level Compensation
At saturated conditions, water density changes with pressure. Compensation is needed using:
$$h_{\text{corrected}} = \frac{\Delta P}{(\rho_w - \rho_s) \cdot g}$$

## 2.5 Smoke Density (Opacity) Measurement

### 2.5.1 Definition
**Smoke density (opacity)** = percentage of light absorbed or scattered by flue gas particulates over a known path length.

$$\text{Opacity (\%)} = \left( 1 - \frac{I}{I_0} \right) \times 100$$

where $I_0$ = incident light intensity, $I$ = transmitted light intensity.

By **Beer-Lambert Law**:
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha L}$$

where $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient, $L$ = path length.

### 2.5.2 Transmissometer / Opacity Monitor
- A light source (LED or incandescent) is placed on one side of the stack.
- A photodetector on the other side measures transmitted intensity.
- Output: 4–20 mA proportional to opacity (0–100%).

### 2.5.3 Applications
- **Stack emissions monitoring** for environmental compliance (CPCB norms in India).
- **Combustion optimization** — high opacity indicates incomplete combustion.
- **Baghouse/ESP performance** evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Recall Point:** Indian CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) standards limit particulate matter to $\leq 50\ \text{mg/Nm}^3$ for thermal power plants, and opacity to $\leq 20\%$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T}[S_B - S_A]dT$ | Seebeck EMF in thermocouple | $\text{mV}$ |
| 2 | $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | RTD linear approximation | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann radiation | $W/m^2$ |
| 4 | $h = \Delta P / (\rho g)$ | Drum level from DP cell | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | $F_B = \rho g V_{\text{sub}}$ | Buoyancy on displacer | $N$ |
| 6 | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha L}$ | Beer-Lambert for opacity | — |
| 7 | $\text{Opacity} = (1 - I/I_0)\times 100$ | Smoke density definition | % |
| 8 | $C = 2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L / \ln(D/d)$ | Capacitance probe | $F$ |
| 9 | $P_{\text{steam}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + \rho_{\text{water}} g H$ | Static head correction | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | $P_{\text{critical}} = 221.2\ \text{bar}$ | Critical pressure of water | $\text{bar}$ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drum Level from DP Cell

**Problem Statement:**
A boiler drum has saturated water of density $\rho_w = 700\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and saturated steam density $\rho_s = 35\ \text{kg/m}^3$. A differential pressure transmitter is connected with a high-tap on the steam space and a low-tap submerged $0.5\ \text{m}$ below the normal water level. If the transmitter reads $\Delta P = 4.2\ \text{kPa}$, find the actual drum water level above the low tap.

**Given:**
- $\rho_w = 700\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_s = 35\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- $\Delta P = 4.2\ \text{kPa} = 4200\ \text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Write the General DP Equation:**
The DP transmitter measures the difference in hydrostatic head between the two taps. The high-tap is in steam (density $\rho_s$), and the low-tap is in water (density $\rho_w$).

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{low}} - P_{\text{high}}$$

**Step 2 — Express Each Pressure:**
Let $H$ be the height of water above the low tap. The vertical distance from the low tap to the high tap is fixed (say $L$). The portion above water (in steam) is $L - H$.

$$P_{\text{low}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot H$$

$$P_{\text{high}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_s \cdot g \cdot (L - H) + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (H_{\text{reference}})$$

For simplicity, we consider the net effective pressure difference due to the water column:

$$\Delta P = (\rho_w - \rho_s) \cdot g \cdot H + \text{constant (zeroed at calibration)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Level:**
$$H = \frac{\Delta P}{(\rho_w - \rho_s) \cdot g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values:**
$$H = \frac{4200}{(700 - 35) \times 9.81}$$

$$H = \frac{4200}{665 \times 9.81}$$

$$H = \frac{4200}{6523.65}$$

$$H = 0.6437\ \text{m} \approx 643.7\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The actual drum water level is approximately **644 mm** above the low-tap reference.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why drum level is a **compensated measurement** — the steam density cannot be ignored at high pressures where $\rho_s$ becomes significant.

## 3.2 Derivation: Smoke Opacity from Photocell Output

**Problem Statement:**
A smoke density monitor uses a light source of intensity $I_0 = 1000$ lux. The photocell at the receiver measures $I = 750$ lux. Calculate the smoke opacity in % and the absorption coefficient if the stack width is $L = 3\ \text{m}$.

**Given:**
- $I_0 = 1000\ \text{lux}$
- $I = 750\ \text{lux}$
- $L = 3\ \text{m}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Opacity:**
$$\text{Opacity} = \left( 1 - \frac{I}{I_0} \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Opacity} = \left( 1 - \frac{750}{1000} \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Opacity} = (1 - 0.75) \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Step 2 — Apply Beer-Lambert Law to Find $\alpha$:**
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha L}$$

$$750 = 1000 \cdot e^{-\alpha \times 3}$$

$$e^{-3\alpha} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Take Natural Logarithm:**
$$-3\alpha = \ln(0.75)$$

$$-3\alpha = -0.2877$$

$$\alpha = 0.0959\ \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**
- Opacity = **$25\%$**
- Absorption coefficient $\alpha = 0.0959\ \text{m}^{-1}$

## 3.3 Derivation: RTD Temperature from Resistance

**Problem Statement:**
A Pt100 RTD has resistance $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0\ ^\circ C$. The temperature coefficient is $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /^\circ C$. If the measured resistance is $R_T = 138.5\ \Omega$, find the temperature.

**Step 1 — Apply the Linear Approximation Equation:**
$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values:**
$$138.5 = 100 \left[ 1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times (T - 0) \right]$$

$$1.385 = 1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot T$$

$$0.385 = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T$:**
$$T = \frac{0.385}{3.85 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{385}{3.85} = 100\ ^\circ C$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The measured temperature is exactly **$100\ ^\circ C$**, demonstrating the linearity of the Pt100 RTD in this range.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Drum Level Measurement Method

| Method | Range (mm) | Accuracy | Response | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DP Cell (compensated)** | 0–600 | $\pm 10\ \text{mm}$ | Fast | Industrial standard |
| **Gauge Glass** | 0–600 | $\pm 5\ \text{mm}$ | Visual | Backup / verification |
| **Float Type** | 0–400 | $\pm 15\ \text{mm}$ | Slow | Older plants |
| **Conductivity Probe** | Discrete levels | $\pm 25\ \text{mm}$ | Instant | Alarms only |
| **Displacer** | 0–500 | $\pm 12\ \text{mm}$ | Fast | High pressure drums |
| **Capacitance Probe** | 0–1000 | $\pm 8\ \text{mm}$ | Fast | Modern plants |

## 3.5 Derivation: Steam Pressure Correction for Static Head

**Problem Statement:**
A pressure gauge is installed $3\ \text{m}$ below the steam pipe tapping point. The gauge reads $142\ \text{bar}$. The condensate in the siphon has density $\rho_c = 960\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate the actual steam pressure in the pipe.

**Step 1 — Recognize the Static Head:**
The gauge reads the steam pressure **minus** the hydrostatic head of the condensate column.

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{steam}} - \rho_c \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $P_{\text{steam}}$:**
$$P_{\text{steam}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + \rho_c \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Static Head in Pascal:**
$$\Delta P_{\text{head}} = 960 \times 9.81 \times 3 = 28,252.8\ \text{Pa} = 0.283\ \text{bar}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result:**
$$P_{\text{steam}} = 142 + 0.283 = 142.283\ \text{bar}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The correction is small (0.283 bar) but becomes significant in vacuum or low-pressure measurement applications.
