# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation (TSI). List any four parameters monitored by it.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation (TSI) is an online, continuous monitoring system that protects steam/gas turbines by measuring critical mechanical parameters and initiating alarms and trips. The four primary parameters monitored are:
1. Shaft absolute vibration (radial displacement),
2. Pedestal (bearing housing) vibration,
3. Rotor eccentricity (thermal bow at slow roll),
4. Axial shaft position (thrust bearing wear).
*Additional parameters:* speed, case expansion, differential expansion, and turning-gear engagement status.

---

### **Q2.** Why is a non-contact eddy-current probe preferred over a contact-type sensor for shaft vibration measurement?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A non-contact eddy-current probe is preferred because:
1. The shaft is rotating at 3000/3600 rpm; a contact sensor would wear out rapidly and introduce friction loading.
2. The clearance between shaft and bearing is only 200–400 μm; any contact sensor would alter the rotor dynamics being measured.
3. Eddy-current probes have a flat frequency response from **DC to 10 kHz**, capturing both static position (eccentricity, axial thrust) and dynamic vibration.
4. They are immune to oil contamination, water, and dust, which is essential in the bearing environment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always draw a labelled block diagram** for vibration / eccentricity questions. A purely textual answer will cap your marks at ~8/14.
> 2. **Mention the transducer type explicitly** (e.g., "velocity pickup", "eddy-current proximity probe") in the first sentence of your answer.
> 3. **State the formula** for EMF (for velocity pickup) and for % eccentricity (for eccentricity measurement) — these are the two formula marks the examiner looks for.
> 4. **End with a "consequence" sentence** — e.g., "If eccentricity exceeds 10%, the start-up permissive is denied, protecting the rotor from a thermal-bow-induced rub." This is the "engineering judgment" mark worth 1–2 points.
> 5. Do **not** confuse shaft vibration (dynamic) with eccentricity (quasi-static). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`

**A(a).** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a velocity pickup used for pedestal vibration measurement in a steam turbine. Derive the expression for the output EMF. **(7 marks)**

**A(b).** With a neat block diagram, explain the measurement of shaft vibration using eddy-current proximity probes. Compare the velocity pickup and the eddy-current probe. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition and Location:**
A velocity pickup (also called a seismic transducer or geophone) is a contact-type transducer that is rigidly bolted to the bearing pedestal of a steam turbine. It converts the mechanical vibration velocity of the casing into a proportional alternating voltage.

**Step 2 — Construction:**
It consists of a cylindrical permanent magnet suspended by soft springs inside a hollow coil. The coil is rigidly attached to the casing. The magnet is the inertial mass and the springs allow it to remain momentarily at rest (in space) when the casing is vibrated.

**Step 3 — Working:**
When the pedestal vibrates with velocity $v$, the casing (and hence the coil) moves with velocity $v$. By Newton's first law, the magnet resists this motion and remains relatively stationary. The relative velocity between coil and magnet is exactly $v$.

**Step 4 — EMF Generation:**
The relative motion causes the coil conductors to cut the magnetic flux lines of the magnet. By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, an EMF is induced:

$$e = B \, l \, v$$

where $B$ is the flux density of the magnet (Tesla), $l$ is the total effective conductor length in the field (metres), and $v$ is the relative velocity (m/s).

**Step 5 — Sinusoidal Vibration Substitution:**
For a sinusoidal pedestal displacement $X(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, the velocity is $v(t) = A \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. Substituting:

$$e(t) = B \, l \, A \, \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 6 — Signal Conditioning and Display:**
The output EMF is amplified, integrated (if displacement is needed), and displayed as **mm/s RMS** on the TSI monitor. The signal is also compared against two thresholds — alarm and trip — typically **4.6 mm/s and 7.1 mm/s** as per ISO 10816-3.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
The velocity pickup is rugged, simple, and provides a direct measure of vibration severity. Its drawback is the $\omega$ dependence, which gives higher output at higher frequencies, often requiring a flat-response accelerometer in critical applications.

#### **Model Solution for A(b):**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Shaft vibration is the dynamic radial displacement of the rotating shaft journal relative to the bearing housing, measured using **non-contact eddy-current proximity probes**.

**Step 2 — Construction and Location:**
Two probes (X and Y) are mounted in the bearing housing, 90° apart, with their tips facing the shaft journal. The nominal gap is typically **1.0 to 1.5 mm**. An additional axial probe measures the thrust position.

**Step 3 — Working Principle (Eddy Currents):**
A high-frequency alternating current (1–2 MHz) flows through the probe coil, generating an alternating magnetic field at the coil tip. This field induces **eddy currents** in the conductive shaft surface. The eddy currents produce their own opposing magnetic field, which modifies the **impedance $Z$** of the probe coil. The change in $Z$ is a monotonic, near-linear function of the gap $x$ between the probe tip and the shaft.

**Step 4 — Signal Conversion:**
The detector circuit converts the impedance change to a DC-coupled voltage: $V_{out} = k_1 - k_2 \, x$. The output is linear over a calibrated band (typically ±0.5 mm around the set point).

**Step 5 — Display and Diagnostics:**
The TSI monitor displays the peak-to-peak shaft displacement in **μm**. From the X and Y signals, an **orbit plot (Lissajous figure)** is generated showing the path traced by the shaft center inside the bearing clearance. The spectrum (FFT) is also computed for unbalance, misalignment, and oil-whip identification.

**Step 6 — Comparison Table (must be in the answer):**

| Property | Velocity Pickup (Pedestal) | Eddy-Current Probe (Shaft) |
|---|---|---|
| Contact | Yes (rigidly mounted) | No (across air gap) |
| Output | Velocity (mm/s) | Displacement (μm) |
| Frequency Range | 10 Hz – 1 kHz | DC – 10 kHz |
| Subject to Oil/Dust | No | No (immune) |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
The two sensors are complementary: pedestal vibration measures the integrated casing response, while shaft vibration directly measures the rotor orbit. Modern TSI systems use **both** in parallel for complete coverage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`

**B(a).** Explain the phenomenon of rotor eccentricity in a steam turbine. With a neat block diagram, describe the eddy-current probe-based eccentricity measurement system. Derive the formula for percentage eccentricity. **(7 marks)**

**B(b).** List the major subsystems of a Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation system. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an axial-position (thrust) monitoring system using an eddy-current probe. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Eccentricity:**
Rotor eccentricity is the **radial offset between the rotor's geometric centre and the bearing's geometric centre** at low rotational speed. It is expressed as a percentage of the nominal air gap.

**Step 2 — Physical Cause (Thermal Bow):**
When a steam turbine is shut down, the rotor retains significant internal heat (especially the HP and IP sections) for many hours. Heat radiates outward asymmetrically (top cools faster than bottom, or steam-side cools slower than exhaust-side), causing **non-uniform thermal expansion**. The shaft physically bends — this is called **thermal bow**.

**Step 3 — Why It Is Dangerous:**
If a thermally bowed rotor is brought to rated speed (3000/3600 rpm), the bowed section experiences huge centrifugal and gyroscopic forces, leading to:
- Severe unbalance vibration,
- Rub between rotating and stationary blades,
- Catastrophic destruction of the turbine in seconds.

**Step 4 — Measurement Principle:**
The rotor is rotated by the **turning gear** at 3–10 rpm. Two eddy-current probes (X and Y) at 90° measure the gap continuously. Because the rotor is rotating, the offset $e$ traces a near-circular path.

**Step 5 — Derivation of % Eccentricity:**
Let $\Delta g_X$ and $\Delta g_Y$ be the peak-to-peak variations in gap at probes X and Y during one revolution. The offset magnitude is:

$$e = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\Delta g_X)^{2} + (\Delta g_Y)^{2}}$$

The % eccentricity is this offset expressed as a percentage of the nominal gap $g_{nom}$:

$$\% \, e = \frac{\sqrt{(\Delta g_X)^{2} + (\Delta g_Y)^{2}}}{2 \, g_{nom}} \times 100$$

**Step 6 — Safe-Start Envelope:**
The industry standard safe-start limit is **% e ≤ 10%**. If % e > 10%, the turning gear is kept running until the rotor cools uniformly and the bow dissipates. Only when the bow is below the limit is the start-up permissive granted by the TSI to the DCS.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
Eccentricity measurement is a **permissive interlock**, not a trip. It is the single most important measurement for protecting the turbine against a thermal-bow-induced start-up failure.

#### **Model Solution for B(b):**

**Step 1 — Major Subsystems of TSI (must list at least 6):**
1. Shaft absolute vibration (radial X-Y probes).
2. Pedestal (casing) vibration (velocity / accelerometer).
3. Rotor eccentricity (X-Y pair at one shaft end).
4. Axial shaft position / thrust wear.
5. Speed and keyphasor (tachometer).
6. Case expansion and differential expansion.
7. Turning-gear engagement status.

**Step 2 — Axial-Position Monitoring — Definition:**
Axial position monitoring measures the **displacement of the rotor along its axis** relative to the stationary thrust bearing. It detects wear of the thrust bearing pads and protects the turbine from axial thrust excursions that could destroy the stationary and rotating blade seals.

**Step 3 — Transducer:**
A **single (or dual redundant) eddy-current proximity probe** is mounted in the bearing housing facing a collar or thrust disc mounted on the rotor shaft. The probe is oriented along the shaft axis (not radial).

**Step 4 — Working Principle:**
- As the rotor shifts axially (forward or backward), the gap $x$ between the probe tip and the thrust disc face changes.
- The eddy-current probe produces a DC-coupled voltage proportional to $x$: $V_{out} = k_1 - k_2 \, x$.
- A calibrated voltage band (e.g., $\pm 0.5$ mm around the centre) corresponds to normal running.
- An axial shift of +0.5 mm triggers an **alarm**; a shift of +1.0 mm triggers a **trip**.

**Step 5 — Block Diagram Description:**
[Reference the Mermaid block diagrams in Section 4.3 — replace the radial X-Y probes with a single axial probe facing a thrust disc.]

**Step 6 — Diagnostic Insight:**
A positive axial shift (toward the turbine exhaust) indicates **forward thrust**, often caused by sudden loss of load (turbine trips off the grid). A negative axial shift indicates **backward thrust**, often caused by over-pressure in the exhaust or loss of steam admission.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
Axial-position monitoring is a hardwired trip in every large utility turbine, as uncontrolled axial motion can wipe out the thrust bearing and cause immediate mechanical destruction within seconds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **TSS / TSI** = Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation; an online monitoring + protection system.
- [ ] **Pedestal vibration** uses a **velocity pickup** (electrodynamic) — output is in **mm/s RMS**, limit **4.6 mm/s** per ISO 10816-3.
- [ ] **Velocity pickup EMF formula:** $e = B \, l \, v = B \, l \, A \, \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$.
- [ ] **Accelerometer** uses a **piezoelectric crystal** — output is in **g or m/s²**, output voltage is $V = S \, a$.
- [ ] **Shaft vibration** uses **eddy-current proximity probes** (X and Y at 90°) — output is in **μm peak-to-peak**, alarm **125 μm**, trip **200 μm**.
- [ ] **Eddy-current probe principle:** RF coil → magnetic field → eddy currents in shaft → impedance $Z$ changes with gap → voltage output $V_{out} = k_1 - k_2 \, x$.
- [ ] **Eccentricity** is the **radial offset** of the rotor centre from the bearing centre at **slow roll (turning gear, 3–10 rpm)**.
- [ ] **% Eccentricity formula:** $\% e = \dfrac{\sqrt{(\Delta g_X)^{2} + (\Delta g_Y)^{2}}}{2 \, g_{nom}} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Safe-start limit for eccentricity:** **< 10%**; above this, start-up is blocked.
- [ ] **Cause of eccentricity:** **Thermal bow** due to uneven cooling of the rotor after shutdown.
- [ ] **Axial position** monitoring uses an **eddy-current probe facing the thrust disc**; alarm at $\pm 0.5$ mm, trip at $\pm 1.0$ mm (typical).
- [ ] **Pedestal vibration = contact, slow, integrated view** vs. **shaft vibration = non-contact, fast, direct view** of rotor orbit.
- [ ] **Lissajous (orbit) plot** is constructed from X-Y probe signals for shaft-vibration diagnostics.
- [ ] **Two trip categories:** *Alarm* (operator warning) and *Trip* (hardwired relay opens turbine stop valves).
- [ ] **Standard frequency band for pedestal vibration (ISO 10816-3):** 10 Hz – 1000 Hz.
- [ ] **Rotor critical speed:** Through which the rotor passes during start-up; eccentricity is the *pre-check* for a safe critical-speed crossing.
- [ ] **Exam Mantra:** Always include **(1) Block diagram, (2) Sensor type, (3) Formula, (4) Consequence** in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation of Non-Contacting Transducers for Speed Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Installation of Non-Contacting Transducers for Speed Measurement**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **non-contacting transducer** for speed measurement is a sensing device that determines the angular velocity (rotational speed) of a rotating machine element — such as a turbine shaft, rotor, or gearbox — **without any physical/mechanical contact** with the moving part. The transducer detects changes in a physical field (magnetic, optical, electrostatic, or electromagnetic) induced by markers, discontinuities, or features on the rotating surface, and converts these field variations into a proportional electrical signal (frequency or voltage) from which the speed in **RPM (revolutions per minute)** or **rad/s** is computed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Non-contacting transducers are **mandatorily** used in power plants for monitoring turbine speed, generator rotor speed, and auxiliaries because they offer **zero frictional drag, zero wear, long life, high reliability**, and the ability to operate in hostile environments (high temperature, vibration, presence of oil/steam).

### 1.2 Common Families of Non-Contacting Speed Transducers

| Family | Physical Principle | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnetic Pickup (Variable Reluctance / VR Sensor)** | Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction | Sinusoidal AC voltage |
| **Hall-Effect Sensor** | Lorentz force on charge carriers | Digital square wave (TTL) |
| **Optical (Photoelectric) Tachometer** | Modulation of light beam | Pulses (digital) |
| **Proximity (Inductive / Eddy Current)** | Change in inductance / eddy current loss | Frequency / analog voltage |
| **Capacitive Pickup** | Change in capacitance | Frequency-modulated signal |
| **Tachogenerator (DC/AC)** | Rotating coil in magnetic field | DC or AC voltage proportional to ω |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Listening to a Heartbeat through a Stethoscope"**
>
> Imagine you want to know how fast a person is running, but you are told **not to touch them at all** (perhaps because they are in a sterilised room). You can either:
> - **Watch them pass a marked doorway** (Optical method — counting light pulses blocked by the runner).
> - **Hold a microphone near the track** and listen to the rhythmic footsteps (Acoustic/Vibration method).
> - **Hold a magnetic "sniffer" near a metallic patch on their shirt** and feel the magnetic field pulse each time it passes (Magnetic/VR pickup).
>
> Each of these is "non-contact." The transducer in a power plant does exactly this: it *senses* a feature (a tooth, a hole, a reflective strip, a magnet) on the rotating shaft as it sweeps past, and counts the rhythm to determine speed.

### 1.4 Why Non-Contacting? (Engineering Justification)

In a power plant, the rotating shaft of a steam turbine or generator may rotate at **3000 RPM (India standard)** or **3600 RPM (60 Hz regions)**, and the bearings cannot tolerate any additional friction or heat from a contacting tachometer (e.g., a drag-cup or centrifugal governor-style speedometer). Hence, **non-contacting transducers** are the **only** viable option. Additional benefits:

- **No mechanical loading** on the shaft → no measurement error due to torque absorption.
- **Wear-free** → service life can exceed 20 years.
- **Suitable for high-temperature zones** (turbine casings at 500–600 °C) when probe is properly cooled.
- **Immune to dirt, oil mist, and vibration** when correctly installed.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used (per ISO, IS, and IEEE Power Plant Practice)

- **Speed unit:** RPM (revolution per minute) and **rad/s** where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- **Frequency unit:** Hz (pulses per second).
- **Air gap:** mm (typically **0.5 mm to 5 mm**).
- **Output signal level:** mV to V (sensor-dependent).
- **Operating temperature:** **−40 °C to +200 °C** for uncooled probes; up to **+800 °C** with water-cooled housings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Universal Speed–Frequency Relationship (Applies to ALL Pulse-Producing Transducers)

Every non-contacting transducer that produces a discrete pulse each time a target feature passes is governed by **one master equation**:

$$
f = \frac{N \times P}{60}
$$

Where:
- $f$ = pulse output frequency in **Hz**
- $N$ = rotational speed in **RPM**
- $P$ = number of pulses per revolution (PPR) — i.e., the number of teeth / holes / magnets on the target wheel

Solving for speed:

$$
N = \frac{60 \, f}{P}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important formula** for KTU Board exam questions on this topic. If the target wheel has 60 teeth and the transducer outputs 3000 Hz, then $N = \frac{60 \times 3000}{60} = 3000$ RPM.

---

## 2.2 Type 1 — Magnetic Pickup (Variable Reluctance / VR Sensor)

### Working Principle
A permanent magnet with a soft-iron pole piece is wound with a coil. A **ferrous target (gear tooth, key, slot)** passing the pole tip changes the magnetic reluctance of the flux path, causing the flux through the coil to vary. By **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced:

$$
e(t) = -N_c \, \frac{d\phi}{dt}
$$

Where:
- $e(t)$ = induced instantaneous voltage (V)
- $N_c$ = number of turns in the pickup coil
- $\phi$ = instantaneous magnetic flux (Wb)

As the tooth approaches, flux rises sharply; as it leaves, flux collapses. The result is an **approximately sinusoidal waveform** whose **frequency** equals the tooth-passing frequency.

### Installation Requirements (VR Sensor)

1. **Air gap (g):** 0.5 mm – 2.0 mm for small teeth; 2 mm – 5 mm for large gears. Too small → mechanical strike; too large → signal loss.
2. **Target material:** Ferromagnetic (steel, cast iron). **Stainless steel (austenitic) and aluminium produce negligible output** — common exam trap!
3. **Diameter of target wheel / shaft:** Should be **≥ 75 mm** for stable mounting.
4. **Surface speed:** Typical usable range 0.5 – 25 m/s.
5. **Mounting:** Vibration-isolated clamp; cable shielded and grounded at one end; probe perpendicular to tooth face within ±5°.
6. **Cooling:** For turbines, use **water-cooled housing** if ambient > 150 °C.

### Output Voltage Magnitude (Empirical)
$$
V_{pk} \approx K_{VR} \cdot \frac{N_c \cdot A \cdot \Delta B}{\Delta t}
$$

Where $K_{VR}$ is a constant, $A$ the pole-face area, $\Delta B$ the flux density swing, $\Delta t$ the tooth transition time.

---

## 2.3 Type 2 — Hall-Effect Speed Sensor

### Working Principle
A Hall plate carries a constant control current $I_c$. When a ferromagnetic target (or a magnet) passes the sensor, the magnetic flux density **B** through the Hall element changes, producing a **Hall voltage**:

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \, I_c \, B}{t}
$$

Where:
- $V_H$ = Hall voltage (V)
- $R_H$ = Hall coefficient (m³/C)
- $I_c$ = control current (A)
- $B$ = magnetic flux density normal to the plate (T)
- $t$ = thickness of the Hall element (m)

The internal signal conditioner converts $V_H$ into a **clean digital square wave** at $V_{OH} \approx 5$ V and $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V — directly TTL-compatible for PLCs and DCS.

### Installation Requirements
- Air gap typically **1 – 3 mm** (larger than VR sensor).
- Less sensitive to surface finish than VR sensor.
- Can detect **non-ferrous targets if a back-bias magnet is integrated** in the sensor housing.

---

## 2.4 Type 3 — Optical (Photoelectric) Tachometer

### Working Principle
A light source (LED) projects a beam onto the rotating surface. A photodetector (photodiode / phototransistor) on the other side (transmissive mode) or on the same side (reflective mode) receives the light, which is **modulated** by alternating reflective (tape) and non-reflective (background) zones — or by holes in a disc. Each transition produces a pulse.

Output is a digital pulse train; speed is recovered from the same master equation $N = 60 f / P$.

### Installation Requirements
- **Lens-to-target distance:** 10 – 300 mm (depends on model).
- Surface must be **clean and matte-free of oil** (oil film causes false reflections).
- Ambient light must be filtered (modulated LED + bandpass filter is standard).
- **Not preferred inside hot turbine casings** — better suited for **cleaner external couplings** (governor shafts, gearbox outputs).

---

## 2.5 Type 4 — Inductive / Eddy-Current Proximity Pickup

### Working Principle
A high-frequency oscillator drives a coil at the probe tip. The **eddy currents induced in a nearby metallic target** absorb energy from the oscillator, changing its amplitude. A trigger circuit converts the amplitude change into a switching pulse. The frequency of pulses equals the tooth-passing frequency.

### Installation Requirements
- Air gap typically **1 – 4 mm**.
- Works on **any electrically conducting target** (ferrous, non-ferrous, aluminium, copper).
- **Cannot work on plastic / wood / rubber** (no eddy current).
- Excellent for **small, high-speed targets** (e.g., gear teeth on governor pinions).

---

## 2.6 Type 6 — Tachogenerator (an exception: requires no target wheel, but still non-contact with the *measurement* circuit)

A DC or AC generator coupled magnetically to the shaft. The induced EMF is **directly proportional to angular velocity**:

$$
E = K \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega
$$

Where:
- $E$ = generated EMF (V)
- $K$ = machine constant
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb)
- $\omega$ = angular speed (rad/s)

For $N$ in RPM, $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, hence:

$$
E = \frac{2\pi K \Phi}{60} \cdot N
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although the tachogenerator is mechanically coupled, it is classified as a **non-contacting transducer for the *measurement* loop** because there is no friction-based dragging of an indicating pointer. However, in modern KTU-style questions, the term "non-contacting transducer for speed" almost always refers to the **VR / Hall / Optical / Eddy-current** family, which requires no shaft coupling.

---

## 2.7 Consolidated KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed from frequency | $N = \dfrac{60\,f}{P}$ | RPM | $P$ = teeth/holes/poles per revolution |
| 2 | Frequency from speed | $f = \dfrac{N \, P}{60}$ | Hz | Master relationship |
| 3 | Angular speed | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | Use for tachogenerator EMF |
| 4 | Tachogenerator EMF | $E = K \Phi \omega$ | V | DC machine constant times flux times speed |
| 5 | Faraday's Law (VR sensor) | $e = -N_c \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ | V | Negative sign is Lenz's law |
| 6 | Hall Voltage | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H I_c B}{t}$ | V | Linear in B for fixed $I_c, t$ |
| 7 | Tooth-passing period | $T = \dfrac{60}{N P}$ | s | Reciprocal of frequency |
| 8 | Linear surface speed | $v = \pi D N / 60$ | m/s | $D$ = target wheel diameter (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Examiners *love* converting between RPM, rad/s, Hz, and linear m/s. Practice the unit-conversion chain: $\text{RPM} \xrightarrow{\times 2\pi/60} \text{rad/s} \xrightarrow{\times r} \text{m/s}$. Also remember $1 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ pulse/s}$ and $1 \text{ RPM} = \dfrac{1}{60} \text{ Hz}$ (when $P = 1$).

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam turbine overspeed protection:** VR sensor on a 60-tooth gear produces 3000 Hz at 3000 RPM; trip circuit activates if frequency exceeds 3150 Hz (105 % of rated).
- **Generator rotor monitoring:** Hall sensor on a magnet embedded in the rotor end provides speed feedback to the AVR / governor.
- **Boiler feed pump auxiliaries:** Optical tachometer used on clean, accessible shafts.
- **Wind turbine:** Inductive pickup on the gearbox output gives 1 pulse per tooth for condition monitoring and grid synchronisation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Master Speed–Frequency Relation

**Step 1.** Consider a rotating shaft fitted with a target wheel having $P$ equally-spaced features (teeth, holes, magnets). Let the shaft rotate at $N$ revolutions per minute.

**Step 2.** In one minute, the shaft completes $N$ revolutions, so the number of target features sweeping past a stationary sensor is:

$$
\text{Features per minute} = N \times P
$$

**Step 3.** Since there are 60 seconds in a minute, the number of features (i.e. pulses) per second — which by definition is the frequency $f$ — is:

$$
f = \frac{N \times P}{60}
$$

**Step 4.** Solving for the rotational speed $N$:

$$
N = \frac{60\,f}{P}
$$

**Step 5.** The angular speed in rad/s is then:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi f}{P}
$$

**Step 6.** The time interval between two consecutive pulses (tooth-passing period) is the reciprocal of the frequency:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{60}{N P} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Induced EMF in a Variable-Reluctance (VR) Sensor

**Step 1.** The flux through the pickup coil is $\phi(t)$. The tooth passing the pole tip modulates this flux between a **maximum** $\phi_{\max}$ (when tooth aligns with pole) and a **minimum** $\phi_{\min}$ (when gap aligns with pole).

**Step 2.** Over a small tooth transition time $\Delta t$, the average rate of change of flux is:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{\phi_{\max} - \phi_{\min}}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 3.** Applying **Faraday's Law** to a coil of $N_c$ turns:

$$
e(t) = -N_c \, \frac{d\phi}{dt} \;\approx\; -N_c \, \frac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 4.** The negative sign (Lenz's law) tells us the induced EMF **opposes** the change in flux. The **magnitude** of the peak induced voltage is:

$$
V_{pk} = N_c \, \frac{\Delta\phi}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 5.** For a sinusoidal approximation of the output, $\phi(t) = \Phi_0 + \Phi_1 \cos(\omega_e t)$ where $\omega_e = 2\pi f$ is the electrical (tooth-passing) angular frequency, so:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -\omega_e \Phi_1 \sin(\omega_e t)
$$

**Step 6.** Substituting:

$$
e(t) = N_c \, \omega_e \, \Phi_1 \, \sin(\omega_e t)
$$

**Step 7.** Hence the **peak EMF** is:

$$
V_{pk} = N_c \, \omega_e \, \Phi_1 = 2\pi f \, N_c \, \Phi_1
$$

**Step 8.** Substituting $f = N P / 60$:

$$
V_{pk} = \frac{2\pi \, N_c \, \Phi_1 \, N \, P}{60}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The peak voltage is **directly proportional to the speed $N$** — making the VR sensor a self-generating tachometer that requires **no external power supply**. The trade-off is that at very low speeds, $V_{pk}$ becomes too small to detect (typically signal drops below 50 mV below ~10 RPM with 60-tooth gear).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Hall Voltage for Speed Sensor

**Step 1.** The Hall effect arises from the **Lorentz force** on charge carriers moving with velocity $v_d$ through a magnetic field $B$:

$$
\vec{F} = q \left( \vec{v}_d \times \vec{B} \right)
$$

**Step 2.** In equilibrium, the transverse electric field $E_H$ balances the magnetic force on the carriers:

$$
q E_H = q v_d B \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_H = v_d B
$$

**Step 3.** The Hall voltage is the transverse field times the width $w$ of the element:

$$
V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d \, B \, w
$$

**Step 4.** Using the drift-velocity relation $v_d = I_c / (n q t)$ where $n$ is the carrier concentration and $t$ the element thickness, we obtain:

$$
V_H = \frac{I_c \, B}{n q t}
$$

**Step 5.** Defining the **Hall coefficient** $R_H = \dfrac{1}{n q}$, we get the compact form used in Section 2.3:

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \, I_c \, B}{t}
$$

**Step 6.** For a speed sensor, the magnetic field $B(t)$ varies as the target passes. If $B(t) = B_0 + B_1 \cos(2\pi f t)$, then:

$$
V_H(t) = \frac{R_H I_c}{t} \left[ B_0 + B_1 \cos(2\pi f t) \right]
$$

**Step 7.** The DC component $B_0$ is blocked by a coupling capacitor; the AC component becomes a sinusoidal Hall voltage, which is then **squared up** by an internal comparator to produce the digital pulse train used for speed measurement.

---

## 3.4 Installation Geometry & Air-Gap Derivation

**Step 1.** For an axially-oriented target feature (e.g., a key on a shaft) of width $w_t$ rotating at radius $r$, the **time** the feature spends in front of the sensor pole of width $w_p$ is:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{w_p + w_t}{\pi D \, N / 60} = \frac{60 (w_p + w_t)}{\pi D N}
$$

where $D$ is the shaft diameter at the sensing point.

**Step 2.** The air gap $g$ affects the magnetic field strength at the pole tip. For a VR sensor with a pole of face area $A$:

$$
B_{\text{pole}} \approx \frac{\mu_0 \, N_c I_m}{\ell_{\text{iron}} + g \, \mu_r}
$$

where $I_m$ is the equivalent MMF of the permanent magnet, $\ell_{\text{iron}}$ the iron path length, $\mu_r$ the relative permeability, and $g$ the air gap.

**Step 3.** The flux swing $\Delta\phi$ is proportional to $B_{\text{pole}}$, and from Section 3.2 step 8:

$$
V_{pk} \propto \frac{N}{g}
$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, **doubling the air gap halves the output voltage** for a given speed. This is why installation manuals specify a *maximum* gap (e.g., 1.0 mm for small teeth, 2.5 mm for large gear teeth) — to keep $V_{pk}$ above the threshold of the signal conditioner.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem.** A steam turbine shaft rotates at 3000 RPM. A magnetic pickup is mounted against a 60-tooth gear fitted on the shaft.
**(a)** Find the output frequency of the pickup.
**(b)** Convert this speed to rad/s and to linear surface speed at a radius of 0.4 m.

**Solution.**

**(a)** Frequency from $f = \dfrac{NP}{60}$:

$$
f = \frac{3000 \times 60}{60} = 3000 \;\text{Hz}
$$

**(b)** Angular speed:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \;\text{rad/s} \approx 314.16 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

Linear surface speed at $r = 0.4$ m:

$$
v = \omega \times r = 314.16 \times 0.4 \approx 125.66 \;\text{m/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using $v = \pi D N / 60$:** $v = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.8 \times 3000}{60} = \dfrac{2400\pi}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66$ m/s. ✓ Consistent.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selection Guide for Installation

| Parameter | VR (Magnetic) | Hall-Effect | Optical | Eddy-Current |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| External power needed | No (self-generating) | Yes (5–24 V) | Yes (LED drive) | Yes (oscillator) |
| Target material | Ferrous only | Ferrous (+ non-ferrous with bias magnet) | Reflective / transmissive | Any conductor |
| Typical air gap | 0.5 – 2 mm | 1 – 3 mm | 10 – 300 mm (lens dist.) | 1 – 4 mm |
| Output | Sine wave | Square wave (TTL) | Square wave | Square / analog |
| Output amplitude vs. speed | Linear in $N$ | Constant (digitised) | Constant (digitised) | Constant (digitised) |
| Hot environment (≥ 200 °C) | Excellent (with cooling) | Poor (active IC) | Poor (LED degrades) | Good (passive coil) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium-High |
| Best for | Turbine rotors, gearbox | Tachometer modules, automotive | Clean lab, governor | High-speed small gears |
| Preferred in KTU power plant context | **Yes (most common)** | Yes (modern digital) | Limited (clean shafts) | Yes (governor pinion) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Non-Contacting Speed Measurement Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotating Shaft] -->|Carries| B[Target Wheel with P Features]
    B -->|Mechanical Position Signal| C[Non-Contacting Transducer Probe]
    C -->|Raw mV or V signal| D[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    D -->|Filtered Waveform| E[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    E -->|Clean Square Wave Pulses| F[Frequency-to-Voltage Converter F to V]
    E -->|Pulse Train| G[Microcontroller or PLC Counter]
    F -->|Analog Voltage proportional to RPM| H[Indicator or Recorder]
    G -->|Digital RPM Display| I[Display HMI or DCS]
    H --> J[Control Room Annunciator]
    I --> J
    G --> K[Overspeed Trip Relay]
    K --> L[Trip Solenoid Turbine Stop Valve]
```

## 4.2 Installation Geometry of a Variable-Reluctance (VR) Pickup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensor_Assembly [VR Sensor Probe Assembly]
        P1[Permanent Magnet] --> P2[Soft Iron Pole Piece]
        P2 --> P3[Pickup Coil Nc turns]
        P3 --> P4[Shielded Cable]
    end
    subgraph Shaft_Assembly [Shaft and Target Wheel]
        S1[Rotating Shaft] --> S2[Key or Gear Tooth]
        S2 --> S3[Adjacent Gap or Valley]
    end
    S2 -->|Air Gap g mm| P2
    S3 -->|Air Gap g mm| P2
    P4 -->|Output EMF e t| AMP[Signal Conditioner]
    AMP --> OS[Overspeed Trip Circuit]
    AMP --> DISP[RPM Display]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting a Non-Contacting Speed Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is target material ferromagnetic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is environment very hot or vibration-heavy?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is target electrically conductive?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> VR[Use Variable Reluctance Pickup]
    Q2 -- No --> HALL[Use Hall Effect Sensor with Back Bias Magnet]
    Q3 -- Yes --> EC[Use Eddy Current Proximity Pickup]
    Q3 -- No --> OPT[Use Optical Photoelectric Pickup]
    VR --> COMM[Common to Steam Turbines and Generators]
    HALL --> COMM
    EC --> COMM
    OPT --> COMM
```

## 4.4 Signal Conditioning Path for a VR Pickup Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VRSine[Sinusoidal mV from VR coil] --> BPF[Bandpass Filter 100 Hz to 20 kHz]
    BPF --> AMP[Low Noise Preamplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    AMP --> ST[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    ST --> SQW[Clean 5 V Square Wave at Tooth Passing Frequency]
    SQW --> CPLR[Optocoupler Galvanic Isolation]
    CPLR --> PLC[PLC High Speed Counter Input]
    PLC --> MATH[Math Block N equals 60 f divided by P]
    MATH --> HMI[Operator HMI Display]
    MATH --> OSP[Overspeed Protection Trip Logic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Installation Cross-Check:** The diagrams above correspond to the standard ISA-72 (Instrumentation Symbols & Identification) and IEC 60617 conventions. During installation, ensure the **probe axis is radial and perpendicular** to the tooth face within ±5° to maximise the rate of change of flux $d\phi/dt$ and hence the peak voltage $V_{pk}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $P$ (teeth per revolution):** Many students write $N = 60 f$ instead of $N = 60 f / P$. This is the **single most common error** and will cost full marks.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** $f$ in kHz vs. Hz; $N$ in rps vs. RPM. Always re-state units in the final answer.
> 3. **Confusing tachogenerator with VR pickup:** Tachogenerator gives voltage proportional to speed (analog), VR pickup gives frequency proportional to speed (requires $f$ to $V$ converter or counter).
> 4. **Choosing wrong target material:** A VR pickup on an aluminium shaft will give **zero output** — a classic viva question.
> 5. **Ignoring air-gap effect on $V_{pk}$:** When asked to justify "why air gap must be small," students often say "to get more signal" without writing $V_{pk} \propto 1/g$ or invoking Faraday's Law.
> 6. **Conflating overspeed trip speed with rated speed:** Standard practice is trip at **110 % of rated** for boilers/turbines (per Indian Boiler Regulations and ASME PTC).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a non-contacting transducer for speed measurement. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A non-contacting transducer for speed measurement is a device that measures the rotational speed of a shaft **without any physical/mechanical contact** with the moving part. It detects variations in a physical field (magnetic, optical, or electromagnetic) caused by features on the rotating surface and converts them into a proportional electrical signal (frequency or voltage) from which the speed in RPM is computed.
**Examples:** (1) **Variable-Reluctance (VR) magnetic pickup** — senses flux changes from passing gear teeth (self-generating). (2) **Hall-effect sensor** — senses magnetic field changes and outputs a digital pulse train.
*(Any two valid examples with brief principle → 1.5 marks each. Definition → 1 mark.)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**A 60-tooth gear is mounted on a shaft and a magnetic pickup gives 1800 Hz. Find the shaft speed in RPM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Given $P = 60$ teeth, $f = 1800$ Hz.
Using $N = \dfrac{60 f}{P}$:

$$
N = \frac{60 \times 1800}{60} = 1800 \;\text{RPM}
$$

*(Step 1: identify given → 0.5; Step 2: formula → 1; Step 3: substitution and result with unit → 1.5.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Variable-Reluctance (VR) magnetic pickup** for speed measurement with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the induced EMF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **installation procedure and precautions** to be followed while mounting a non-contacting speed transducer on a steam turbine shaft. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Choice A — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A VR pickup consists of a **permanent magnet**, a **soft-iron pole piece**, and a **pickup coil** of $N_c$ turns wound around the pole. When a ferromagnetic target (gear tooth) sweeps past the pole tip, the **magnetic reluctance** of the flux path falls, causing the flux $\phi$ through the coil to **increase**. As the tooth moves away (gap aligns), reluctance rises and flux **decreases**. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the changing flux induces an EMF in the coil. The output is an **alternating voltage** at the **tooth-passing frequency** $f = NP/60$.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let the flux vary sinusoidally: $\phi(t) = \Phi_0 + \Phi_1 \cos(\omega_e t)$, where $\omega_e = 2\pi f$.

From Faraday's Law:

$$
e(t) = -N_c \frac{d\phi}{dt} = -N_c \frac{d}{dt}\left[ \Phi_0 + \Phi_1 \cos(\omega_e t) \right]
$$

$$
e(t) = -N_c \left[ -\omega_e \Phi_1 \sin(\omega_e t) \right] = N_c \, \omega_e \, \Phi_1 \, \sin(\omega_e t)
$$

Peak voltage:

$$
V_{pk} = N_c \, \omega_e \, \Phi_1 = 2\pi f \, N_c \, \Phi_1
$$

Substituting $f = NP/60$:

$$
V_{pk} = \frac{2\pi \, N_c \, \Phi_1 \, N P}{60}
$$

> Hence the **peak EMF is directly proportional to shaft speed $N$**, and the **frequency** is the **tooth-passing frequency**.

*(Principle explanation → 3 marks; derivation → 4 marks. The labelled diagram referenced in the answer can be substituted by the schematic in Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Choice A — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Installation Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Survey the site:** Identify the location on the shaft where a target feature (key, gear, drilled hole, or mounted tooth wheel) already exists or can be installed. The location should be on a **clean, accessible, vibration-isolated** section of the shaft, away from bearings and oil seals.
2. **Mount the probe bracket:** Bolt the bracket rigidly to a **machined flat** on the turbine casing or a separate mounting boss. Use **stainless-steel fasteners** with thread-locking compound to prevent loosening due to vibration.
3. **Set the air gap:** Use a **feeler gauge** or non-magnetic shim to set the gap between the probe tip and the target. Typical gap: **0.5 – 2.0 mm** for fine-pitch gears; **2 – 5 mm** for large gear teeth. Tighten the lock nut.
4. **Cable routing:** Use **shielded twisted-pair cable**; ground the shield at **one end only** (instrument end). Route away from power cables (minimum 300 mm separation) to avoid EMI.
5. **Cooling arrangement:** For hot turbine casings, fit a **water-cooled probe housing** with cooling water supplied at 30 °C inlet, flow 1–2 L/min.
6. **Calibration & verification:** Spin the shaft at a known reference speed (e.g., turning gear at 5 RPM) and verify signal amplitude ≥ 1 V peak on an oscilloscope.

**Precautions (3 marks):**
1. **Probe perpendicular to tooth face within ±5°** to ensure maximum $d\phi/dt$.
2. **Target material must be ferromagnetic**; austenitic SS or aluminium is unacceptable.
3. **Avoid mounting near magnetic fields** of large motors or welding equipment.
4. **Provision for axial/radial thermal expansion** of the shaft; use flexible mounting.
5. **Wire the cable through a cable gland rated for the zone classification** (Ex-d for hazardous areas in oil-fired plants).
6. **Do not route signal cable in the same tray as variable-frequency-drive (VFD) output cables.**

---

### Question B — Choice B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare the working principles of **Hall-effect**, **optical**, and **eddy-current** non-contacting speed transducers. State one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **tachogenerator** is coupled to a motor shaft. At 1500 RPM it gives 75 V DC. Find: (i) the generator constant $K\Phi$, (ii) the voltage at 2000 RPM, and (iii) the frequency of the pulse train if the same shaft drives a 30-tooth gear and a VR pickup. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Choice B — Part (a) (7 marks)

| Transducer | Working Principle | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Hall-Effect** | A constant current $I_c$ flows through a thin semiconductor plate placed in a magnetic field. The **Lorentz force** deflects the carriers, producing a transverse **Hall voltage** $V_H = R_H I_c B / t$. As a magnetised or ferrous target passes, $B$ modulates, and the signal is squared up to a digital pulse train. | Requires an **external DC power supply**; cannot operate at very high temperatures (> 150 °C) because the active IC fails. |
| **Optical (Photoelectric)** | An **LED** emits light onto a rotating disc with **reflective/non-reflective bands** (or holes). A **photodiode** receives the modulated light. Each transition (bright→dark) produces a pulse. Speed is recovered from $f = NP/60$. | **Sensitive to oil film, dust, and ambient light**; not suitable for hot/casing-mounted applications. |
| **Eddy-Current (Inductive Proximity)** | A **high-frequency oscillator** drives a coil. When a conducting target approaches, **eddy currents** in the target absorb energy, damping the oscillator. A trigger circuit produces a switching pulse at each passing feature. | Works only on **electrically conducting targets**; cannot sense plastics, wood, or rubber. Also requires **external power**. |

*(Each transducer explanation → 2 marks; each limitation → ~0.33 marks; total → 7 marks.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Choice B — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** At $N_1 = 1500$ RPM, $E_1 = 75$ V. Generator equation: $E = K \Phi \, \omega$, with $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.

**(i) Generator constant (3 marks):**
At $N_1 = 1500$ RPM:

$$
\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \;\text{rad/s} \approx 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
K\Phi = \frac{E_1}{\omega_1} = \frac{75}{50\pi} = \frac{1.5}{\pi} \;\text{V·s/rad} \approx 0.4775 \;\text{V·s/rad}
$$

**(ii) Voltage at $N_2 = 2000$ RPM (2 marks):**

$$
\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \times 2000}{60} = \frac{200\pi}{3} \;\text{rad/s} \approx 209.44 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
E_2 = K\Phi \cdot \omega_2 = 0.4775 \times 209.44 \approx 100 \;\text{V}
$$

**Cross-check using ratio:** $\dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{2000}{1500} \Rightarrow E_2 = 75 \times \dfrac{4}{3} = 100$ V. ✓

**(iii) Frequency of VR pickup with $P = 30$ teeth at $N_2 = 2000$ RPM (2 marks):**

$$
f = \frac{N_2 P}{60} = \frac{2000 \times 30}{60} = 1000 \;\text{Hz}
$$

*(Each sub-part clearly states the formula, substitutes correctly, and gives a final numerical answer with units. Marks allocated per KTU 2024 scheme: 3 + 2 + 2 = 7.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Master equation:** $N = 60 f / P$ — must be memorised in **all three algebraic forms**.
- ✅ **Angular speed:** $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s; **linear surface speed:** $v = \pi D N / 60$ m/s.
- ✅ **VR Pickup** is **self-generating** (no supply needed) and gives **sine wave** output whose **amplitude is proportional to $N$** and **frequency equals tooth-passing rate**.
- ✅ **Hall-effect** gives **digital square wave** and needs **5 V supply**; works on ferrous and non-ferrous targets with a bias magnet.
- ✅ **Optical** requires **clean, reflective surface**; **eddy-current** works on **any conductor**.
- ✅ **Tachogenerator EMF:** $E = K \Phi \, \omega$ — output is **analog voltage** directly proportional to $\omega$.
- ✅ **Air gap** must be **small** (0.5 – 5 mm) for VR and Hall sensors; larger gaps reduce $V_{pk} \propto 1/g$.
- ✅ **Target material rule:** VR → ferrous only; Hall → ferrous (+ non-ferrous with back-bias); Optical → reflective/holey; Eddy-current → any conductor.
- ✅ **Installation must include:** vibration-isolated bracket, shielded cable (single-end ground), proper air-gap setting, cooling for hot zones, perpendicular alignment within ±5°.
- ✅ **Overspeed trip:** Typically set at **110 % of rated RPM** (per Indian Boiler Regulations and ASME guidelines).
- ✅ **Frequency unit watch:** $1$ Hz $= 1$ pulse/s; for a 60-tooth wheel at 3000 RPM, $f = 3000$ Hz.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** confusing $N = 60 f$ (true only when $P = 1$) with the general $N = 60 f / P$.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis:** Module 4 questions test **principle + installation procedure + numerical problem on the speed-frequency relation**; prepare a labelled diagram of a VR pickup with shaft-gear assembly for full marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Notes — PEAET751 / Module 4 / Installation of Non-Contacting Transducers for Speed Measurement.** These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme, B.Tech Outcome-Based Education framework (CO4 typically mapped to this module), and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels **L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyse)** as required by the ESE pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controls in Power Plants (Basics Only): Steam Temperature Control, Feed Water Control, Boiler-Following Operation, Turbine-Following Operation, Co-ordinated Unit Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET751 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Controls in Power Plants (Basics Only): Steam Temperature Control, Feed Water Control, Boiler-Following Operation, Turbine-Following Operation, Co-ordinated Unit Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Steam Temperature Control

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Steam Temperature Control refers to the closed-loop regulatory process employed in a fossil-fired steam generator to maintain the temperature of superheated steam (typically 535 °C to 565 °C in sub-critical units, and up to 600 °C+ in supercritical units) exiting the final superheater within a tight tolerance band of **± 5 °C** of the setpoint, irrespective of load variations, fuel quality fluctuations, or ambient condition changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The temperature being controlled is the **final superheater outlet steam temperature (TSH)**, NOT the boiler drum saturation temperature. The saturation temperature is a thermodynamic consequence of drum pressure, not a controlled variable.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Shower Mixer Tap:**
Imagine you are taking a shower. The hot water pipe is your "flame/fuel energy," and the cold water pipe is your "spray attemperation water." If someone opens a tap downstairs (sudden demand/load change), the hot water pressure suddenly drops, and you get scalded. To prevent this, you slightly turn the cold tap (attemperator) to bring the mixed temperature back to comfort. The thermometer at the showerhead is your **temperature sensor**, and your hand on the cold tap is the **final control element**. That is exactly how an attemperator-based steam temperature control loop functions in a boiler.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Memory Retention):**
- Superheated steam setpoint: **540 °C ± 5 °C** (typical sub-critical once-through baseline)
- Attemperator spray water temperature: **260 °C to 290 °C** (drawn from economizer outlet)
- Loop response time constant: **τ ≈ 30 s to 90 s** (depends on superheater mass and stage count)
- Spray water-to-steam mass ratio limit: **≤ 0.15** (beyond this, thermal efficiency drops sharply)
- Sensor: **Thermocouple Type K** (sheathed) or **RTD Pt-100** in thermowell

---

## 1.2 Feed Water Control (Drum Level Control)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Feed Water Control is the multi-element regulatory scheme that maintains the water level inside the steam generator's steam drum within an operational band of **± 25 mm to ± 50 mm** of the normal water level (NWL) setpoint, by modulating the feedwater regulating valve (or feedwater pump speed) using measurements of drum level, steam flow, and feedwater flow as control inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is drum level control critical?**
> - **Low level** → uncovering of generating tubes → tube failure from overheating
> - **High level** → water carryover into superheater → superheater tube rupture, turbine blade erosion

**Intuitive Analogy — The Bucket With a Hole:**
Consider a bucket with water pouring in from a tap (feedwater) and draining out through a hole at the bottom (steam being drawn off to the turbine). The water level in the bucket is the drum level. The challenge? When you suddenly increase the drain hole size (more steam demand), the water level initially DROPS, even though more water is actually flowing in. This counter-intuitive transient is called **"swell" and "shrink"** caused by changing bubble density in the risers. The three-element control system cleverly uses BOTH the inflow rate (feedwater flow) and outflow rate (steam flow) to anticipate and correct this — much like a smart car driver who uses the speedometer AND fuel gauge to maintain steady cruising.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
- Drum level setpoint tolerance: **± 50 mm** of NWL
- Drum diameter range: **1.2 m to 1.8 m** for 210/500 MW units
- Feedwater flow sensor: **Venturi / Annubar** with dP transmitter
- Steam flow sensor: **Flow nozzle** at superheater outlet
- Drum pressure: **110 bar to 180 bar** (sub-critical)
- Drum level measurement: **Differential Pressure (dP) cell** across column references

---

## 1.3 Boiler-Following Operation (Turbine-Lead, Boiler-Lag)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
In a Boiler-Following control mode, the **boiler is the controlled element** — the turbine governor is set to maintain a constant turbine inlet steam pressure (or constant throttle pressure setpoint), while the boiler's combustion control and feedwater control act as the master controllers that adjust fuel, air, and water to match the **load demand placed by the turbine admission valves**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Memory Tag:** "Boiler follows the demand — the turbine tells the boiler how much steam it wants, and the boiler delivers it."

**Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter:**
Picture a busy restaurant. The customer (turbine/grid load) decides how much food they want. The chef (boiler) prepares the food as ordered. The waiter (throttle valve) brings it out as soon as it's ready. If more customers arrive suddenly, the chef is told to cook faster (more fuel/air/water). The **bottleneck is the kitchen (boiler)**, not the waiter. This is boiler-following.

**Key Characteristic (Bolded):**
- **Boiler response time:** 100 s to 300 s (slow — large thermal mass)
- **Turbine response time:** 1 s to 5 s (fast — hydro-mechanical)
- Suitable for: **base-load units** with slow load changes

---

## 1.4 Turbine-Following Operation (Boiler-Lead, Turbine-Lag)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
In Turbine-Following control mode, the **boiler is the master controller** — the boiler's master fuel/air controller adjusts combustion rate according to a pre-defined load schedule (or grid frequency demand), and the **turbine governor is the controlled element** that adjusts its inlet valves to maintain the steam pressure within the boiler's allowable pressure band by modulating steam extraction rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Memory Tag:** "Turbine follows the supply — the boiler decides how much steam it can generate, and the turbine consumes it at a rate that keeps pressure stable."

**Intuitive Analogy — The Water Tap on a Storage Tank:**
Think of a water tank on a hill (boiler generating steam). A tap at the bottom (turbine) is opened to irrigate a field. If the field needs more water, the tap is opened wider — but the tank (boiler) doesn't immediately know about the demand change. The water level in the tank (drum pressure) starts to drop, and the inlet valve from the pump (boiler firing rate) compensates slowly. The bottleneck here is the **tap's response**, not the pump. This is turbine-following.

**Key Characteristic (Bolded):**
- Boiler sets the pace; turbine throttles to maintain pressure
- **Suitable for: load-following units** in grid frequency regulation
- The throttle pressure setpoint may be allowed to "droop" by **2 % to 5 %** to facilitate load sharing

---

## 1.5 Co-ordinated Unit Control (Master Control)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Co-ordinated Unit Control is the **integrated master control strategy** in which both the boiler and turbine participate in load regulation, sharing the load-change demand in a complementary manner — the **turbine takes the fast initial load step (fast response)**, while the **boiler takes the slow sustained load change (slow but accurate energy balance)**. A single Unit Master Controller (UMC) computes the Load Index (LI) and dispatches it to both boiler master and turbine master controllers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This is the **most advanced** and the most common mode in **modern utility boilers (210 MW and above)**. The UMC ensures that the initial transient pressure deviation in the throttle is recovered by boiler master within **30 s to 60 s**, while the turbine valve positions are returned to their nominal operating point.

**Intuitive Analogy — A Two-Person Rowing Boat:**
A two-person rowing team crossing a river. The front person (turbine) is strong and fast — they make quick, sharp strokes to react to sudden currents (fast load changes). The back person (boiler) is steady and rhythmic — they maintain the long-term pace and direction (sustained energy balance). The coxswain (Unit Master Controller) calls out instructions to coordinate both. Neither person works alone, and the boat moves smoothly. This is co-ordinated control.

**Key Characteristic (Bolded):**
- Combines **fast response of turbine** + **stable long-term generation of boiler**
- Uses a **Unit Master Controller (UMC)** with Load Index (LI) signal
- Minimizes throttle pressure deviation to **± 1 % to ± 2 %** of setpoint
- **Standard for: 210 MW, 500 MW, 660 MW supercritical units**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Steam Temperature Control — Theoretical Breakdown

### A. Why is steam temperature the most difficult variable to control?

Steam temperature is the most heavily interacted control loop in a boiler because it is influenced by **multiple disturbances acting simultaneously**:

1. **Steam flow (load) change** — higher load → higher tube wall heat flux → higher temperature
2. **Fuel flow change** — calorific value variation, excess air changes
3. **Excess air ratio** — affects heat transfer coefficient
4. **Feedwater temperature change** — affects economizer outlet enthalpy
5. **Fouling/slagging** of superheater tubes — changes overall heat transfer coefficient `U`
6. **Fuel composition** — moisture in coal, CV variations

The superheater behaves as a **series of first-order lags with a long dead time** (transport delay of high-pressure steam from burner zone to superheater outlet). This is why the loop requires a **cascade control structure**.

### B. Cascade Control Structure for Steam Temperature

**Primary (Master) Loop:**
- **PV (Process Variable):** Final superheater outlet temperature `T_SH_out`
- **SP (Setpoint):** Operator-entered temperature setpoint (e.g., 540 °C)
- **Controller Output:** Secondary loop setpoint (becomes the "remote setpoint" for the inner loop)

**Secondary (Slave) Loop:**
- **PV:** Attemperator outlet steam temperature `T_SH_in` (or first-stage superheater outlet)
- **SP:** Output of primary controller
- **Controller Output:** Attemperator spray water valve position

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cascade?** The secondary loop is **10× faster** than the primary. It "absorbs" disturbances occurring between the attemperator and the secondary measurement point, leaving the master loop to handle the slower, large disturbances.

### C. Operating Principles of the Three Main Steam Temperature Control Methods

**Method 1: Spray Attemperation (Most Common)**
- Cold water (from economizer outlet) is sprayed directly into the superheated steam
- The enthalpy balance: `m_s × h_s + m_spray × h_spray = (m_s + m_spray) × h_out`
- Effective for supercritical units where gas-side control is sluggish

**Method 2: Gas Recirculation (GR) / Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)**
- Recirculated flue gas is mixed with combustion air to lower flame temperature
- Affects heat flux distribution across the superheater
- Slow response — **time constant: 60 s to 180 s**

**Method 3: Burner Tilt / Swivel**
- Tilting burners moves the peak heat flux zone away from or towards the superheater
- Used in **tangentially-fired boilers** (e.g., 500 MW units)
- Slowest — **time constant: 120 s to 300 s**

---

## 2.2 Feed Water Control — Theoretical Breakdown

### A. The Three-Element Control Architecture

The "three elements" are:
1. **Drum level (H)** — the controlled variable
2. **Steam flow (D)** — feedforward signal (disturbance)
3. **Feedwater flow (W)** — feedback signal (manipulated variable feedback)

**Why single-element control fails:**
On sudden load increase, drum level initially **rises** ("swell") due to bubble expansion as drum pressure momentarily drops. A single-element controller sees this as "level is high" and **reduces** feedwater — which is the exact opposite of what's needed. This is the **"inverse response"** or **"swell/shrink"** problem.

**Three-element control logic:**
- The steam flow `D` is multiplied by a gain block and **subtracted** from the drum level error before being fed to the feedwater flow controller
- The feedwater flow `W` is then matched to `D` (with offset compensation) to maintain `W = D` in steady state
- The drum level controller adds a **trim signal** to handle inventory errors

### B. Mass Balance Equation (Drum Level Dynamics)

For the steam drum, the governing differential equation is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[\rho_w \cdot A \cdot H\bigr] = W - D - B
$$

Where:
- `ρ_w` = saturated water density (kg/m³)
- `A` = drum cross-sectional area (m²)
- `H` = drum water level (m)
- `W` = feedwater mass flow (kg/s)
- `D` = steam mass flow leaving drum (kg/s)
- `B` = continuous blowdown (kg/s), usually negligible except in high-purity boilers

The steam flow `D` is a function of drum pressure `P_drum` and turbine throttle pressure `P_throttle`:

$$
D = C_v \cdot \sqrt{P_{drum} - P_{throttle}}
$$

---

## 2.3 Boiler-Following vs Turbine-Following — Comparison

| Aspect | Boiler-Following | Turbine-Following |
|---|---|---|
| **Master controller** | Boiler combustion & FW | Turbine governor |
| **Fast unit** | Turbine (responds to pressure error) | Boiler (responds to load schedule) |
| **Response time** | 100 s–300 s (boiler-limited) | 60 s–120 s (boiler-limited on ramp) |
| **Throttle pressure** | Held constant by turbine valves | Allowed to vary within band (droop 2 %–5 %) |
| **Load range** | Narrow (~30 %–100 % of MCR) | Wide (~25 %–100 % of MCR) |
| **Application** | Base-load, constant load | Load-following, grid frequency regulation |
| **Suitability for supercritical** | Limited | Better (faster load change) |

---

## 2.4 Co-ordinated Unit Control — The Master Architecture

### A. Unit Master Controller (UMC) Signal Flow

1. **Load Demand** comes from the grid dispatcher (or operator's Load Setpoint)
2. The UMC compares the Load Demand with the **Actual Generation** (measured by generator output MW)
3. The UMC produces a **Load Index (LI)** signal = `f(Demand, Generation, Rate Limit)`
4. The LI is sent to:
   - **Boiler Master (BM)** → controls fuel, air, FD/ID fan speeds
   - **Turbine Master (TM)** → controls governor valves (HP/IP LP)
5. A **Pressure Deviation (ΔP)** signal is added to TM setpoint to keep throttle pressure stable

### B. Why Co-ordinated Control is Required

- **Pure Boiler-Following:** Slow — cannot pick up sudden grid frequency drops
- **Pure Turbine-Following:** Boiler pressure sags dangerously on sudden valve opening
- **Co-ordinated:** Turbine opens valve first (fast steam release, picks up load quickly) → throttle pressure dips slightly → BM responds by increasing fuel/air → pressure restored → turbine valve closes back to nominal

### C. Transfer Function Representation (Simplified)

The boiler-turbine system can be approximated by:

$$
G_{boiler}(s) = \frac{K_B}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} \cdot e^{-L_B s}
$$

$$
G_{turbine}(s) = \frac{K_T}{(\tau_T s + 1)}
$$

Where:
- `K_B, K_T` = steady-state gains
- `τ_1, τ_2` = boiler time constants (typically **30 s, 150 s** for a 210 MW unit)
- `L_B` = boiler dead time (typically **10 s to 30 s**)
- `τ_T` = turbine time constant (typically **0.5 s to 2 s**)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Master the units of every variable. Marks are frequently lost for incorrect or missing units.

| S.No. | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Values / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W - D - B = \rho_w A \dfrac{dH}{dt}$ | Drum mass balance | W, D, B in kg/s; A in m²; H in m |
| 2 | $D = C_v \sqrt{P_{drum} - P_{throttle}}$ | Steam flow from drum | C_v in kg/s·bar⁰·⁵; P in bar |
| 3 | $\tau_{thermal} = \dfrac{M \cdot c_p}{U \cdot A_s}$ | Thermal time constant | M = mass; c_p in J/kg·K; U in W/m²·K |
| 4 | $T_{out} = T_{in} + \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m} \cdot c_p}$ | Superheater outlet temperature | T in °C or K; Q in W |
| 5 | $G_{boiler}(s) = \dfrac{K_B}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} e^{-L_B s}$ | Boiler transfer function (approx.) | τ, L in seconds |
| 6 | $G_{turbine}(s) = \dfrac{K_T}{(\tau_T s + 1)}$ | Turbine transfer function (approx.) | τ_T = 0.5 s to 2 s |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{throttle} = \pm 1\% \text{ to } \pm 2\% \text{ of setpoint}$ | Co-ordinated control band | Within band, no boiler action |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{spray} \le 0.15 \cdot \dot{m}_{steam}$ | Spray attemperator limit | Mass flow ratio |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|
| Steam Temperature Control | Prevents superheater tube metal creep rupture (T > 580 °C causes 100,000-hr creep life loss of 30 %) |
| Feed Water Control | Prevents **circulating water carryover** which can erode turbine HP blades in 6 months |
| Boiler-Following | Used in Indian NTPC **Dadri, Singrauli** base-load stations |
| Turbine-Following | Used in gas-based **CCGT stations** (Rajasthan CCPP) for fast load pickup |
| Co-ordinated Control | Standard in **500 MW+ units** at NTPC Sipat, Kudgi, Khargone |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drum Level Time Constant (Inverse Response)

### Statement
Show that the drum level transfer function with respect to feedwater flow exhibits **inverse response** (initial level rise on step increase in feedwater flow) for a single-element controller, and derive the condition for stable operation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the volumetric balance**

The mass of water + steam mixture in the drum changes due to imbalance of feedwater inflow and steam outflow.

$$
\frac{dM_w}{dt} = W - D
$$

We ignore blowdown B for simplicity.

**Step 2: Express the total drum mass**

The drum contains saturated water plus steam bubbles entrained in the riser water. Define:
- `V_w` = volume of water in drum (m³)
- `α` = steam void fraction in the riser mixture

$$
M_w = \rho_w (1 - \alpha) A H + \rho_s \alpha A H
$$

Where `A` is the cross-sectional area and `H` is the level.

**Step 3: Differentiate with respect to time**

$$
\frac{dM_w}{dt} = A \left[ (\rho_w - \rho_s) H \frac{d\alpha}{dt} + (\rho_w - \rho_s) \alpha \frac{dH}{dt} + \rho_w \frac{dH}{dt} \right]
$$

Since the change in void fraction is much faster than the change in level, we treat `α` as a function of drum pressure `P`:

$$
\alpha = f(P_d) \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = \frac{\partial \alpha}{\partial P} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}
$$

**Step 4: Apply ideal gas relation for steam in risers**

The steam mass in the risers `m_s` follows:

$$
m_s = \frac{P \cdot V_{riser}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{sat}}
$$

Taking isothermal assumption (saturated at drum pressure):

$$
\frac{dm_s}{dt} = \frac{V_{riser}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{sat}} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}
$$

**Step 5: Combine the equations**

The drum water mass balance, when written in terms of level `H`, gives:

$$
A \cdot \rho_w \frac{dH}{dt} = W - D - A \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_s) \cdot H \cdot \frac{1}{P} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} \cdot \beta
$$

Where `β` is a constant relating void fraction to pressure. This shows that even if `W > D` (net inflow), the term involving `dP/dt` can cause `dH/dt` to be **negative** if pressure rises — this is the **swell phenomenon**.

**Step 6: Linearize and take Laplace transform**

For small perturbations around operating point (denoted by overbar):

$$
A \cdot \rho_w \cdot s \cdot \tilde{H}(s) = \tilde{W}(s) - \tilde{D}(s) - K_s \cdot s \cdot \tilde{P}(s)
$$

**Step 7: Drum pressure dynamics**

Pressure in the drum is related to steam flow out:

$$
C_d \frac{dP}{dt} = (D - D_{turbine})
$$

In Laplace form:

$$
\tilde{P}(s) = \frac{1}{C_d s} \left( \tilde{D}(s) - \tilde{D}_{turbine}(s) \right)
$$

**Step 8: Eliminate pressure to get level transfer function**

Substituting Step 7 into Step 6:

$$
A \rho_w s \tilde{H}(s) = \tilde{W}(s) - \tilde{D}(s) - \frac{K_s}{C_d} \left( \tilde{D}(s) - \tilde{D}_{turbine}(s) \right)
$$

**Step 9: Final drum level transfer function with respect to feedwater flow (with steam flow as disturbance)**

$$
\frac{\tilde{H}(s)}{\tilde{W}(s)} = \frac{1}{A \rho_w s} \cdot \frac{(C_d s - K_s / \text{const})}{C_d s + K_s / \text{const}}
$$

The numerator has a **right-half-plane zero**, which is the mathematical signature of **inverse response**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A single-element controller (using only H) sees initial rise in H on a step W increase and responds with negative action — leading to oscillation or instability. This is why **three-element control** is essential for high-pressure drums.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Three-Element Control Loop Equation

### Statement
Derive the closed-loop equation of a three-element feedwater control system and show that in steady state, `W = D` (feedwater flow equals steam flow) when level error is zero.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the three measurement signals**

- `H_meas` = measured drum level (m)
- `D_meas` = measured steam flow (kg/s)
- `W_meas` = measured feedwater flow (kg/s)

**Step 2: Set up the level controller (master)**

The level controller `G_cH` (typically PI) produces an output `u_H`:

$$
u_H = G_{cH}(s) \cdot (H_{SP} - H_{meas})
$$

**Step 3: Set up the feedforward and feedback paths**

The steam flow signal `D_meas` is added to `u_H` (with sign depending on convention). The feedwater flow `W_meas` is subtracted (negative feedback) at the summing junction before the flow controller.

The signal to the flow controller `e_W` is:

$$
e_W = (u_H + K_f \cdot D_{meas}) - K_w \cdot W_{meas}
$$

Where `K_f` is the steam flow feedforward gain and `K_w` is the feedwater flow scaling gain.

**Step 4: Flow controller output to valve**

The flow controller `G_cW` (PI) produces a valve command `m_v`:

$$
m_v = G_{cW}(s) \cdot e_W
$$

**Step 5: Valve and process dynamics**

The feedwater valve + piping dynamics `G_v` and the boiler feedwater process `G_p`:

$$
W = G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot m_v
$$

**Step 6: Combine into closed-loop transfer function**

In the Laplace domain, the drum level `H` is the integral of `(W - D)` (from mass balance). Using the relation:

$$
H(s) = \frac{1}{A \rho_w s} [W(s) - D(s)]
$$

**Step 7: Substitute W(s) and simplify**

The closed-loop level response is given by:

$$
H(s) = \frac{G_{cH}(s) \cdot G_{cW}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot \dfrac{1}{A \rho_w s}}{1 + G_{cW}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot K_w} \cdot H_{SP}(s) - \dfrac{\left[ 1 - K_f \cdot G_{cW}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot \dfrac{1}{A \rho_w s} \right]}{1 + G_{cW}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot K_w} \cdot D(s)
$$

**Step 8: Steady-state analysis (s → 0)**

In steady state, all `G(s) → K` (their DC gains). The integral term in `1/(A ρ_w s)` → ∞. The feedforward term `K_f` is tuned such that:

$$
K_f = \frac{1}{G_{cW}(0) \cdot G_v(0) \cdot G_p(0)}
$$

This makes the numerator of the disturbance term zero at `s = 0`.

**Step 9: Verify the steady-state balance**

With the above tuning, at `s = 0`:

$$
\lim_{s \to 0} H(s) = H_{SP} \quad \text{and} \quad W(0) = D(0)
$$

**Conclusion:** The three-element controller achieves perfect disturbance rejection of steam flow changes and maintains `W = D` in steady state. ✅

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Five Control Schemes

| Parameter | Steam Temp. Control | Feed Water Control | Boiler-Follow | Turbine-Follow | Co-ordinated |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Controlled variable** | Superheater outlet T | Drum level H | Throttle pressure | Throttle pressure | Load (MW) + Throttle P |
| **Manipulated variable** | Spray water valve | Feedwater valve / pump speed | Fuel + Air + Water | Turbine governor valve | Fuel + Air + Water + Valve |
| **Primary disturbance** | Steam flow change | Steam flow change | Load demand | Load schedule | Load demand + Frequency |
| **Controller type** | Cascade (T in + T out) | Cascade (W in + H) | PI on pressure | PI on pressure | UMC + PI masters |
| **Number of loops** | 2 (master + slave) | 2 (master + slave) | 3 (master + pressure + individual) | 2 (master + governor) | 4+ (UMC, BM, TM, pressure) |
| **Response time** | 30 s–90 s | 5 s–30 s (3-element) | 100 s–300 s | 60 s–120 s | 30 s–90 s |
| **Sensor count** | 2 (primary + secondary T) | 3 (H, D, W) | 4+ (P, F, A, W) | 3 (P, F, A) | 6+ (MW, P, F, A, W, T) |
| **Failure consequence** | Tube overheat | Tube dryout / carryover | Boiler pressure deviation | Pressure surge | Load rejection from grid |
| **KTU exam weightage** | 3 / 14 marks | 3 / 14 marks | 7 marks (Part B) | 7 marks (Part B) | 14 marks (Part B) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Cascade Steam Temperature Control Loop (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Temp Setpoint 540C] --> SUM1
    TSH[Superheater Outlet T] --> SUM1
    SUM1[Level Error Sum] --> PID1[Primary PI Controller]
    PID1 --> SP2[Inner Loop Setpoint]
    TIN[Attemperator Outlet T] --> SUM2
    SP2 --> SUM2
    SUM2[Inner Loop Error] --> PID2[Secondary PI Controller]
    PID2 --> VALVE[Spray Water Valve]
    VALVE --> SPRAY[Attemperator]
    SPRAY --> SH[Superheater Section]
    SH --> TSH
```

## 4.2 Three-Element Feedwater Control (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HSP[Level Setpoint] --> SUM1
    HMEA[Drum Level H] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> PIH[Master PI Controller]
    PIH --> SUM2
    DMEA[Steam Flow D] --> FF[Feedforward Gain Kf]
    FF --> SUM2
    WMEA[Feedwater Flow W] --> SUM3
    SUM2 --> SUM3
    SUM3[Combined Error] --> PIW[Flow PI Controller]
    PIW --> FWVALVE[Feedwater Valve]
    FWVALVE --> FW[Feedwater Pipe]
    FW --> DRUM[Boiler Drum]
    DRUM --> HMEA
    DRUM --> DMEA
```

## 4.3 Boiler-Following Control Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GRID[Grid Load Demand] --> THROT[Turbine Throttle Valve]
    THROT --> TURB[Steam Turbine]
    TURB --> GEN[Generator MW Output]
    PTHROT[Throttle Pressure Sensor] --> COMP[Pressure Comparator]
    PSP[Pressure Setpoint] --> COMP
    COMP --> BOILM[Boiler Master Controller]
    BOILM --> FUEL[Fuel Controller]
    BOILM --> AIR[Air Controller]
    BOILM --> FW[Feedwater Controller]
    FUEL --> BLR[Boiler Furnace]
    AIR --> BLR
    FW --> BLR
    BLR --> STEAM[Generated Steam]
    STEAM --> THROT
```

## 4.4 Turbine-Following Control Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UMC[Unit Master Controller] --> BOILM[Boiler Master Controller]
    BOILM --> FUEL[Fuel]
    BOILM --> AIR[Air]
    BOILM --> FW[Feedwater]
    FUEL --> BLR[Boiler]
    AIR --> BLR
    FW --> BLR
    BLR --> DRUM[Steam Drum]
    DRUM --> THROT[Turbine Throttle Valve]
    THROT --> TURB[Turbine]
    PTHROT[Throttle Pressure] --> GOVERNOR[Turbine Governor]
    GOVERNOR --> THROT
    TURB --> GEN[Generator]
```

## 4.5 Co-ordinated Unit Control — Master Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GRID[Grid Dispatcher Demand] --> UMC
    GENMW[Generator MW] --> UMC
    UMC[Unit Master Controller] --> LI[Load Index LI]
    LI --> BM[Boiler Master]
    LI --> TM[Turbine Master]
    PTHROT[Throttle Pressure] --> SUMT
    PSP[Pressure Setpoint] --> SUMT
    SUMT[Pressure Error] --> TM
    BM --> FUEL
    BM --> AIR
    BM --> FW
    FUEL[Fuel Control] --> BLR[Boiler]
    AIR[Air Control] --> BLR
    FW[Feedwater Control] --> BLR
    BLR --> DRUM[Drum Steam]
    DRUM --> THROT[Turbine Valve]
    TM --> THROT
    THROT --> TURB[Steam Turbine]
    TURB --> GEN[Generator]
    GEN --> GENMW
```

## 4.6 Functional Block Diagram — Drum Level with Inverse Response (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Feedwater Flow W] --> SUM
    D[Steam Flow D] --> SUM
    SUM[Mass Balance] --> INV[Inverse Response RHP Zero]
    INV --> H[Drum Level H]
    H --> HFB[Level Feedback]
    HFB --> CONTL[Level Controller PI]
    CONTL --> W
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block labeled "INV" represents the **right-half-plane zero** in the drum level transfer function. Its presence mathematically causes the level to initially move **opposite** to the feedwater flow change — the famous "swell/shrink" effect.
